topics.title: Transverse Stability: Centre of Gravity of Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transverse Stability: Centre of Gravity of Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Centre of Gravity (G)** of a ship is the theoretical point through which the entire weight of the vessel, including its hull, machinery, cargo, fuel, crew, and all consumables, is considered to act vertically downwards due to the force of gravity. In naval architecture, the position of the centre of gravity is fundamental to the analysis of **transverse stability**, which governs a ship's tendency to return to an upright position when heeled (rolled) about its longitudinal (fore-aft) axis.

The position of the ship's centre of gravity is denoted as **G** and is typically located in the ship's **vertical centre plane** (centreline) assuming even side-to-side weight distribution. Its vertical position above the keel is called the **height of the centre of gravity above keel (KG)** or the **Vertical Centre of Gravity (VCG)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> The Centre of Gravity (G) of a ship is the point of application of the resultant force of gravity acting on the entire vessel, located in the vertical longitudinal plane, and characterised by its height above the keel (KG). It is the most critical reference point in the analysis of transverse stability as it forms one of the four key points: G, M (metacentre), B (centre of buoyancy), and K (keel).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a long stick (representing a ship) vertically with one end on the ground. If you push it slightly to the side, it will either fall over or return to its original position depending on **where you are holding it**. If you hold it near the bottom (low G), it's easy to balance — but you can push it over with some effort. If you hold it near the top (high G), it becomes very wobbly and topples over easily.

A ship works in exactly the same way, but in reverse:

- A **low G** means the weight is concentrated near the bottom → ship is naturally stable and returns upright when tilted.
- A **high G** means weight is concentrated near the top (like a tall stack of containers) → ship becomes tender, rolls easily, and can even capsize.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Intuition:** Think of a fully loaded container ship with heavy containers stacked 10 decks high. This ship has a very high G and behaves like a "tall" object — even small wind forces can cause large rolling angles. In contrast, a fully loaded tanker (where all the cargo sits in the bottom hull) has a very low G, making it extremely stable and "stiff" — it doesn't roll much but creates uncomfortable motions for crew.

## 1.3 Why Centre of Gravity Matters in Transverse Stability

In transverse stability analysis, the centre of gravity (G) is one of the four fundamental reference points:

| Symbol | Point | Significance |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **G** | Centre of Gravity | Where weight (W) acts vertically downwards |
| **B** | Centre of Buoyancy | Where buoyancy force (Δ) acts vertically upwards |
| **M** | Metacentre | Point about which the ship oscillates when heeled |
| **K** | Keel | The reference baseline for vertical measurements |

The **transverse stability** is quantified by the **metacentric height (GM)**, which is calculated using the position of G:

$$GM = KM - KG$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasises that determining the position of G is a prerequisite to all stability calculations. A ship may have a perfectly designed hull form, but if G is too high, the vessel will be dangerously unstable. Therefore, the calculation, control, and monitoring of G is treated as a **core competency** for marine engineers and naval architects.

## 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

- **VCG (Vertical Centre of Gravity):** Same as KG — height of G above the keel.
- **LCG (Longitudinal Centre of Gravity):** Position of G measured from midships or from the aft perpendicular, along the ship's length.
- **TCG (Transverse Centre of Gravity):** Position of G measured from the centreline; ideally zero for a symmetrically loaded ship.
- **Displacement (Δ or W):** Total weight of the ship and everything on board, equal to the buoyant force when floating.
- **Lightweight (LWT):** Weight of the empty ship (hull, machinery, outfit) without any cargo, fuel, or consumables.
- **Deadweight (DWT):** Sum of all variable weights (cargo, fuel, water, crew, stores).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When KTU questions ask for the position of G, students must always specify **three coordinates** — KG (vertical), LCG (longitudinal), and TCG (transverse). Even if only the vertical (KG) is required, mentioning the coordinate system used earns full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Ship Weight into Weight Groups

To calculate the position of the centre of gravity, the total weight of the ship is broken down into a series of **discrete weight groups** or **load items**. Each weight group has two key properties:

1. **Magnitude (Weight, Wᵢ):** The mass/weight of the item, measured in tonnes (t) or kilonewtons (kN).
2. **Location (Lever, Kᵢ):** The vertical position of the item's centre of gravity above the keel (or sometimes above the base line), measured in metres (m).

The ship's total weight is then:

$$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i = W_1 + W_2 + W_3 + \cdots + W_n$$

## 2.2 The Fundamental Formula for KG (VCG)

The vertical position of the ship's centre of gravity above the keel is found using the **principle of moments** about the keel:

$$KG = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times K_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i} = \frac{\sum W_i K_i}{\sum W_i}$$

Where:
- $KG$ = Height of centre of gravity above keel (metres)
- $W_i$ = Weight of the iᵗʰ item (tonnes)
- $K_i$ = Height of centre of gravity of the iᵗʰ item above the keel (metres)

This formula is sometimes called the **"first moment principle"** or the **"weight-moment method"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Formula Works:** This is a direct application of the classical mechanics principle that the moment of a resultant force about a point equals the sum of the moments of its component forces about the same point. By taking moments of every weight about the keel (K), the "average height" of all the weights — weighted by their magnitude — gives KG.

## 2.3 Same Formula Applied to LCG and TCG

The same principle of moments applies for the other two coordinates:

$$LCG = \frac{\sum W_i \times L_i}{\sum W_i}$$

$$TCG = \frac{\sum W_i \times T_i}{\sum W_i}$$

Where $L_i$ and $T_i$ are the longitudinal and transverse levers of each weight item.

## 2.4 Determination of Lightweight KG

The lightweight of a ship (empty ship weight) is determined at the shipyard using one of these methods:

- **Design Weight Audit:** Based on detailed design calculations, component lists, and material density data.
- **Weighing Experiment:** The completed ship is physically weighed using shore-based load cells.
- **Inclining Experiment:** Once the ship is in the water, a controlled inclining test is performed (covered in detail in Module 2).

The lightweight is divided into groups such as:
- Steel weight (hull plating, frames, decks)
- Machinery weight (engine, boilers, generators)
- Outfit weight (piping, electrical, HVAC, furniture)
- Miscellaneous fixed weights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** When a problem provides a "stability booklet" or "loading manual" value for lightweight KG, you must **use it directly** without recalculating. The lightweight KG is fixed once the ship is built; only the deadweight contributions are recomputed for each loading condition.

## 2.5 The "Weight Added" or "Weight Removed" Method (Running Moments)

A more practical approach used in loading conditions is the **running moment** or **additions/removals** method. Starting from a known reference condition (e.g., lightship or departure condition), successive additions or removals of weight are tracked.

**Formula for Weight Added:**

$$KG_{new} = \frac{W_{old} \times KG_{old} + W_{added} \times K_{added}}{W_{old} + W_{added}}$$

**Formula for Weight Removed:**

$$KG_{new} = \frac{W_{old} \times KG_{old} - W_{removed} \times K_{removed}}{W_{old} - W_{removed}}$$

**Formula for Weight Shifted (Same Weight, New Position):**

Since the weight is constant, the new KG becomes:

$$KG_{new} = KG_{old} - \frac{w \times d_{vertical}}{W_{total}}$$

Where $d_{vertical}$ is the vertical distance through which the weight has been moved (positive if moved up, negative if moved down).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Shortcut:** A weight shifted up by distance *d* increases KG by $\frac{w \times d}{W}$. A weight shifted down decreases KG by the same amount. This is a powerful tool for solving KTU numerical problems quickly.

## 2.6 The Inclining Experiment (Conceptual Overview)

Although covered in detail in Module 2, the **inclining experiment** is the **definitive method** for determining the lightweight KG of a ship. A known small weight (typically a test rig of lead pigs or a calibrated water tank) is moved transversely across the ship by a measured distance *y*. The resulting heel angle *θ* is measured. Then:

$$GM = \frac{w \times y}{W \times \tan(\theta)}$$

Since $GM = KM - KG$, and KM is known from the hull form (Bonjean curves), KG is back-calculated:

$$KG = KM - GM$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the only KTU-approved method that directly measures the *actual* KG of a complete ship, accounting for all asymmetries, pipe fluids, and onboard items that may not appear on design drawings.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\displaystyle KG = \frac{\sum W_i K_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Fundamental VCG formula (Principle of Moments) | m |
| 2 | $\displaystyle LCG = \frac{\sum W_i L_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Longitudinal centre of gravity | m (from AP or midships) |
| 3 | $\displaystyle TCG = \frac{\sum W_i T_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Transverse centre of gravity (ideally 0) | m (from CL) |
| 4 | $\displaystyle W_{total} = W_{light} + W_{deadweight}$ | Total displacement = Lightship + Deadweight | tonnes (t) |
| 5 | $\displaystyle KG_{new} = \frac{W_1 KG_1 + W_2 KG_2}{W_1 + W_2}$ | KG after adding weight | m |
| 6 | $\displaystyle KG_{new} = \frac{W_1 KG_1 - W_2 KG_2}{W_1 - W_2}$ | KG after removing weight | m |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \Delta KG = \frac{w \times d_{v}}{W_{total}}$ | Change in KG when weight *w* is moved vertically by $d_v$ | m |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \Delta KG = \frac{w \times d_{l}}{W_{total}}$ | Change in KG (parallel shift in LCG) when weight moved longitudinally | m |
| 9 | $\displaystyle GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height from KG | m |
| 10 | $\displaystyle GM = \frac{w \times y}{W \times \tan(\theta)}$ | GM from inclining experiment | m |
| 11 | $\displaystyle \overline{KB} = \frac{\text{Volume of Immersion}}{A_W}$ | Centre of buoyancy above keel | m |
| 12 | $\displaystyle \overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{V}$ | BM from transverse moment of inertia | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When the table requires the absolute value or modulus operation, write it as $\vert x \vert$ in the equation. Do **not** use the vertical pipe character $\vert$ inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks the table rendering.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Calculating the centre of gravity is not merely an academic exercise — it is performed **every day** on operational ships for:

- **Load planning:** Ensuring cargo is distributed to keep G within safe limits.
- **Ballast operations:** Adjusting water ballast to compensate for cargo consumed during voyage.
- **Damage stability:** Estimating new G after flooding of a compartment.
- **Regulatory compliance:** IMO and classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require documented proof of KG ≤ maximum allowable KG (KG_max).
- **Lifting operations:** Heavy lift vessels must verify G before lifting enormous offshore structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application:** In real ship operation software (e.g., LOADICATOR, MaxCon), the entire stability calculation reduces to a single question: "Where is G right now?" All stability criteria (GM, GZ curves, free surface, weather criteria) ultimately depend on the accurate computation of KG.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the KG Formula (Principle of Moments)

### Statement of the Problem

A ship is composed of $n$ discrete weight items. Each weight item $W_i$ is located at a height $K_i$ above the keel. We wish to find the height of the equivalent single point — the centre of gravity G — where the total weight $W_{total}$ may be considered to act.

### Derivation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Consider the moment of each weight about the keel (taking clockwise moments as positive, consistent with the convention that weights act downward).

The moment of the $i^{th}$ weight about the keel is:
$$M_i = W_i \times K_i$$

**Step 2:** Sum the moments of all weights about the keel.

By the principle of moments, the total moment about the keel equals the sum of individual moments:
$$M_{total} = M_1 + M_2 + M_3 + \cdots + M_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times K_i)$$

**Step 3:** Express the total moment using the resultant weight and its point of action.

The total weight $W_{total}$ is the sum of all individual weights:
$$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$$

The moment of this total weight about the keel, where the resultant is assumed to act at height $KG$, is:
$$M_{total} = W_{total} \times KG = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i\right) \times KG$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $M_{total}$.

Since both expressions represent the same physical quantity (the moment of the total weight about the keel):
$$\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i\right) \times KG = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times K_i)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $KG$.

Dividing both sides by $\sum W_i$:
$$KG = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times K_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$$

**Conclusion:** This is the fundamental formula for the height of the centre of gravity. The numerator is called the **total moment about the keel**, and the denominator is the **total displacement**.

$$\boxed{\displaystyle KG = \frac{\text{Total Moment about Keel}}{\text{Total Displacement}} = \frac{\sum W_i K_i}{\sum W_i}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1: Basic Weight Moment Calculation

### Problem Statement

A small vessel has the following loading condition:

| Item | Weight (t) | KG (m) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Lightship | 250 | 3.50 |
| Cargo Hold 1 | 100 | 2.00 |
| Cargo Hold 2 | 80 | 4.00 |
| Fuel Oil | 30 | 1.20 |
| Fresh Water | 20 | 1.50 |
| Crew & Stores | 5 | 6.00 |

**Find:** The height of the centre of gravity (KG) of the vessel in this loading condition.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Set up a weight-moment table.

| Item | Weight $W_i$ (t) | $K_i$ (m) | Moment $W_i \times K_i$ (t·m) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Lightship | 250 | 3.50 | $250 \times 3.50 = 875.00$ |
| Cargo Hold 1 | 100 | 2.00 | $100 \times 2.00 = 200.00$ |
| Cargo Hold 2 | 80 | 4.00 | $80 \times 4.00 = 320.00$ |
| Fuel Oil | 30 | 1.20 | $30 \times 1.20 = 36.00$ |
| Fresh Water | 20 | 1.50 | $20 \times 1.50 = 30.00$ |
| Crew & Stores | 5 | 6.00 | $5 \times 6.00 = 30.00$ |
| **TOTAL** | $\sum W_i = 485$ | — | $\sum W_i K_i = 1491.00$ |

**Step 2:** Apply the fundamental formula.

$$KG = \frac{\sum W_i K_i}{\sum W_i} = \frac{1491.00 \text{ t·m}}{485 \text{ t}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerical value.

Performing the division:
$$KG = \frac{1491.00}{485} = 3.0742 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Round to appropriate significant figures (typically 3 decimals for KTU answers).

$$\boxed{KG = 3.074 \text{ m above the keel}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2: Weight Added/Removed Method

### Problem Statement

A ship in departure condition has a displacement of 12,000 t and KG = 7.20 m. During the voyage, the following operations occur:

1. 500 t of fuel oil with KG = 1.50 m is consumed.
2. 200 t of cargo with KG = 9.00 m is loaded.
3. 300 t of ballast water with KG = 2.00 m is pumped out.

**Find:** The new KG after all three operations.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate initial moment.

$$M_1 = W_1 \times KG_1 = 12000 \times 7.20 = 86400 \text{ t·m}$$

**Step 2:** Operation 1 — Remove 500 t of fuel oil at KG = 1.50 m.

Moment removed:
$$M_{fuel} = 500 \times 1.50 = 750 \text{ t·m}$$

After Operation 1:
$$W_2 = 12000 - 500 = 11500 \text{ t}$$
$$M_2 = 86400 - 750 = 85650 \text{ t·m}$$
$$KG_2 = \frac{85650}{11500} = 7.448 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Operation 2 — Add 200 t of cargo at KG = 9.00 m.

Moment added:
$$M_{cargo} = 200 \times 9.00 = 1800 \text{ t·m}$$

After Operation 2:
$$W_3 = 11500 + 200 = 11700 \text{ t}$$
$$M_3 = 85650 + 1800 = 87450 \text{ t·m}$$
$$KG_3 = \frac{87450}{11700} = 7.474 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Operation 3 — Remove 300 t of ballast water at KG = 2.00 m.

Moment removed:
$$M_{ballast} = 300 \times 2.00 = 600 \text{ t·m}$$

After Operation 3:
$$W_4 = 11700 - 300 = 11400 \text{ t}$$
$$M_4 = 87450 - 600 = 86850 \text{ t·m}$$
$$KG_4 = \frac{86850}{11400} = 7.618 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{KG_{final} = 7.618 \text{ m above the keel}}$$

**Note:** Removing low-lying ballast water (KG = 2.00 m) has **increased** the overall KG of the ship — this is why ballasting and deballasting operations are carefully planned to maintain stability during voyage.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3: Weight Shift Effect on KG

### Problem Statement

A ship of displacement 10,000 t has a current KG of 6.00 m. A cargo crane shifts 50 t of cargo from hold A (KG = 3.00 m) to a position on the upper deck (KG = 11.00 m).

**Find:** The new KG of the ship.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the vertical movement of the weight.

The cargo has been moved upward by:
$$d_v = 11.00 - 3.00 = 8.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the "weight-shift" formula for KG change.

$$\Delta KG = \frac{w \times d_v}{W_{total}} = \frac{50 \times 8.00}{10000}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate.

$$\Delta KG = \frac{400}{10000} = 0.040 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Determine the new KG.

Since the weight has moved **upward**, the centre of gravity rises:
$$KG_{new} = KG_{old} + \Delta KG = 6.000 + 0.040$$

$$\boxed{KG_{new} = 6.040 \text{ m}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that a 50 t shift of cargo in a 10,000 t ship only raised G by 4 cm. This demonstrates that for large ships, individual cargo shifts have only small effects on KG — but **stacking containers high** (a series of such shifts) can have a cumulative effect of several metres, drastically reducing GM.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operations on KG

| Operation | Effect on Displacement | Effect on KG | Stability Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Load cargo at low KG | Increases W | Decreases KG | GM increases → Stiffer ship |
| Load cargo at high KG | Increases W | Increases KG | GM decreases → Tender ship |
| Discharge cargo from low KG | Decreases W | Increases KG | GM decreases → Tender ship |
| Discharge cargo from high KG | Decreases W | Decreases KG | GM increases → Stiffer ship |
| Take on ballast (low) | Increases W | Decreases KG | GM increases → Stiffer |
| Discharge ballast (low) | Decreases W | Increases KG | GM decreases → Tender |
| Transfer weight downward | Unchanged | Decreases | Stiffens ship |
| Transfer weight upward | Unchanged | Increases | Reduces stability |
| Add weight above G | Increases W | Increases KG | Reduces stability |
| Add weight below G | Increases W | Decreases KG | Increases stability |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **highly tested** in KTU viva and Part A questions. Memorising the directions of change gives students a quick way to verify their numerical answers.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Derivation: Relationship Between KG, GM, and KM

The classical transverse stability relationship is:

$$GM = KM - KG$$

This is derived from the geometry of the heeling ship:

**Step 1:** In the upright position, M is on the centreline directly above B at height KM = KB + BM above the keel.

**Step 2:** When the ship heels by a small angle $\theta$, B shifts to a new position $B'$, and the new vertical line through $B'$ intersects the original vertical centreline at the metacentre M.

**Step 3:** For small angles, M remains approximately fixed in space. The transverse stability is determined by the relative position of G with respect to M.

**Step 4:** If G is **below M** (i.e., $KG < KM$), the ship is stable.
If G is **above M** (i.e., $KG > KM$), the ship is unstable and will capsize.

**Step 5:** Therefore, the metacentric height GM is the controlling parameter:

$$GM = KM - KG$$

Where $KM$ is determined by the hull form (specifically by KB + BM, both functions of the hull geometry and waterplane area), and $KG$ is determined by the weight distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The hull form (KM) is essentially fixed for a given ship at a given displacement. **The only way the ship's officer can influence stability is by controlling the loading condition (KG).** This is why KG calculation is a primary skill for marine engineers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: KG Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ship Loading Condition] --> B[Identify Weight Groups]
    B --> C1[Lightweight from Stability Booklet]
    B --> C2[Deadweight Items: Cargo, Fuel, Water, Stores]
    C1 --> D[For each weight Wi, determine lever Ki]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Compute Weight x Lever = Moment]
    E --> F[Sum all Wi = Total Displacement]
    E --> G[Sum all Wi x Ki = Total Moment]
    F --> H[Apply KG = Total Moment / Total Displacement]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Obtain KG value in metres above keel]
    I --> J{Compare KG with KG_max}
    J -->|KG <= KG_max| K[Loading Condition SAFE]
    J -->|KG > KG_max| L[REJECT Loading - Reduce KG]
    L --> M[Re-distribute cargo, add ballast, or unload]
    M --> D
    K --> N[Compute GM = KM - KG]
    N --> O[GM must be > Critical GM]
    O --> P[Stability Criteria Satisfied]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Four Reference Points of Transverse Stability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reference_Points
        direction TB
        A[Keel K - Baseline 0 m] --> B[Centre of Buoyancy B - KB above keel]
        B --> C[Centre of Gravity G - KG above keel]
        C --> D[Metacentre M - KM above keel]
    end
    F[Weight W acts DOWN through G] -.-> C
    G1[Buoyancy Delta acts UP through B] -.-> B
    D --> E[GM = KM - KG = Metacentric Height]
    C --> E
    B --> H[KB from Bonjean Curves]
    D --> I[BM = I_T / V_waterplane]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Weight Added/Removed Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Reference Condition: W1, KG1] --> OpType{Operation Type?}
    OpType -->|Add Weight w| Add1[New W = W1 + w]
    Add1 --> Add2[New Moment = W1*KG1 + w*kg]
    Add2 --> Add3[New KG = New Moment / New W]
    OpType -->|Remove Weight w| Rem1[New W = W1 - w]
    Rem1 --> Rem2[New Moment = W1*KG1 - w*kg]
    Rem2 --> Rem3[New KG = New Moment / New W]
    OpType -->|Shift Weight w by dv| Sh1[Weight unchanged]
    Sh1 --> Sh2[Delta KG = w * dv / W]
    Sh2 --> Sh3[New KG = Old KG +/- Delta KG]
    Add3 --> Result[Final KG]
    Rem3 --> Result
    Sh3 --> Result
```

## 4.4 Schematic: The Effect of High vs Low G

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HIGH_G_High
        direction TB
        H1[Weight concentrated near top of ship]
        H2[Examples: Top-heavy containers, light cargo high in tween decks]
        H3[Ship is Tender, rolls easily]
        H4[Large rolling angles in beam seas]
        H5[Possible capsize if G > M]
    end
    subgraph LOW_G_Stable
        direction TB
        L1[Weight concentrated near bottom of ship]
        L2[Examples: Double bottom ballast, heavy cargo in lower holds]
        L3[Ship is Stiff, has small roll]
        L4[May cause uncomfortable rapid motions]
        L5[Safely resists overturning]
    end
```

## 4.5 Coordinate System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin: Keel at midships CL] -->|+X axis| X[Longitudinal direction: LCG]
    O -->|+Y axis| Y[Transverse direction: TCG]
    O -->|+Z axis| Z[Vertical direction: KG or VCG]
    X --> X1[+ve forward toward bow]
    X --> X2[-ve aft toward stern]
    Y --> Y1[+ve starboard]
    Y --> Y2[-ve port]
    Z --> Z1[+ve upward toward main deck]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `Start`, `Add1`) and labels contain only plain uppercase/lowercase text with spaces and hyphens — no markdown bold/italic tags, no HTML, and no vertical pipes that would break the rendering engine.
