# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case 1: Addition of a Weight

When a weight **w** is added to a ship at a point with coordinates **(x, y, z)** measured from midships, centerline, and keel respectively:

### Step 1 — Update the Displacement
The new displacement is the sum of the original displacement and the added weight:

$$W_{\text{new}} = W + w$$

### Step 2 — Update the Center of Gravity G

The new G is the weighted average of the original G and the point of application:

$$x_{G,\text{new}} = \frac{W \cdot x_G + w \cdot x_p}{W + w}$$

$$y_{G,\text{new}} = \frac{W \cdot y_G + w \cdot y_p}{W + w}$$

$$KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{W \cdot KG + w \cdot z_p}{W + w}$$

> The "**Why**": The principle of moments requires the new G to balance the moment of the original ship and the added weight about the keel.

### Step 3 — Determine Draft Change (Parallel Sinkage)

If the weight is added at the **center of flotation F** (x = 0, y = 0), the ship sinks uniformly without list or trim:

$$\Delta d = \frac{w}{\text{TPC}} \quad \text{[in cm]}$$

where TPC is **Tonnes Per Centimeter** immersion, defined as:

$$\text{TPC} = \frac{A \cdot \rho}{100} \quad \text{[tonnes/cm]}$$

- **A** = waterplane area in m²
- **ρ** = density of water in t/m³ (seawater: **1.025 t/m³**, freshwater: **1.000 t/m³**)

> The "**How**": The ship must displace an additional volume equal to the added weight. The increase in draft required is just the added weight divided by the weight of water displaced per centimeter of sinkage.

### Step 4 — Determine List and Trim

If the weight is added **off-center**:

**List (Heel about the longitudinal axis):**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{GG_y}{GM_{\text{new}}}$$

where the **lateral shift of G** is:

$$GG_y = \frac{w \cdot y_p}{W + w}$$

**Trim (change in longitudinal inclination):**

$$\Delta \text{Trim} = \frac{w \cdot x_p}{\text{MCT}} \quad \text{[in cm, by the head if } x_p > 0\text{]}$$

where **MCT (Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm)** is:

$$\text{MCT} = \frac{W \cdot GMT}{100 \cdot L} \quad \text{[tonne·m/cm]}$$

- **GMT** = longitudinal metacentric height
- **L** = Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)

### Step 5 — Determine the New GM

For a **wall-sided ship** (constant cross-section at the waterline), the metacenter M is *fixed in space*:

$$KM_{\text{new}} \approx KM_{\text{old}} \quad \text{(wall-sided assumption)}$$

Therefore:

$$GM_{\text{new}} = KM_{\text{old}} - KG_{\text{new}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtual Rise of G:** A common shortcut is to define the "virtual rise" of G—the shift in G calculated *as if the displacement remained unchanged*:
>
> $$\Delta G_v = \frac{w \cdot (z_p - KG)}{W}$$
>
> - If **z_p > KG**: positive → G virtually rises → **stability decreases** (loss of GM)
> - If **z_p < KG**: negative → G virtually falls → **stability increases** (gain of GM)
> - If **z_p = KG**: no change in stability

## 2.2 Case 2: Removal of a Weight

This is the algebraic reverse of addition. If weight **w** at point **(x, y, z)** is removed:

### Draft Change (Parallel Rise)

$$\Delta d = -\frac{w}{\text{TPC}} \quad \text{[ship rises]}$$

### New G Position

$$KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{W \cdot KG - w \cdot z_p}{W - w}$$

### List and Trim

$$GG_y = -\frac{w \cdot y_p}{W - w}, \quad \Delta \text{Trim} = -\frac{w \cdot x_p}{\text{MCT}}$$

The negative signs indicate the ship heels **away from** the side from which weight was removed, and trims **away from** the end from which weight was removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cargo discharge is most critical when high-weight items are removed from high positions (e.g., containers from upper deck tiers of a container ship). The sudden fall in G may cause excessive GM and a violently stiff ship, which can damage cargo lashings and increase crew discomfort. Mariners must plan discharge sequences carefully.

## 2.3 Case 3: Transference of a Weight

When a weight **w** is moved from point P₁ to point P₂ (P₁ and P₂ having coordinates (x₁, y₁, z₁) and (x₂, y₂, z₂)):

### 2.3.1 Lateral Transference (horizontal movement, z₁ = z₂)

- **No change in displacement** → no parallel sinkage/rise
- **No change in KG** → G shifts only in the horizontal plane
- G shifts laterally by:

$$GG_y = \frac{w \cdot (y_2 - y_1)}{W}$$

- G shifts longitudinally by:

$$GG_x = \frac{w \cdot (x_2 - x_1)}{W}$$

- The resulting list angle is:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{GG_y}{GM}$$

- The resulting trim is:

$$\Delta \text{Trim} = \frac{w \cdot (x_2 - x_1)}{\text{MCT}}$$

### 2.3.2 Vertical Transference (lifting or lowering, x₁ = x₂, y₁ = y₂)

- **No change in displacement** → no parallel sinkage/rise
- **No change in horizontal G position** → no list or trim from this operation alone
- G shifts vertically by:

$$GG_z = \frac{w \cdot (z_2 - z_1)}{W}$$

- New KG:

$$KG_{\text{new}} = KG + GG_z$$

- For wall-sided ship (KM fixed):

$$GM_{\text{new}} = KM - KG_{\text{new}} = GM_{\text{old}} - GG_z$$

> **Why this matters:** This is the principle behind **ballasting** and **deballasting** operations. Submarines and warships lower heavy weights (ballast water, ammunition) to lower G and increase stability in damaged conditions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are organized for rapid exam revision. Use the **\vert** notation in place of vertical pipes to preserve LaTeX rendering within tables.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | TPC | $\text{TPC} = A \cdot \rho \mid 100$ | t/cm | A in m², ρ in t/m³ |
| 2 | Draft change (addition) | $\Delta d = w \mid \text{TPC}$ | cm | Ship sinks by Δd |
| 3 | New displacement | $W' = W \pm w$ | tonnes | + for addition, − for removal |
| 4 | New KG (addition) | $KG' = (W \cdot KG + w \cdot z_p) \mid (W + w)$ | m | Weighted average |
| 5 | New KG (removal) | $KG' = (W \cdot KG - w \cdot z_p) \mid (W - w)$ | m | Weighted average |
| 6 | Virtual rise of G | $\Delta G_v = w \cdot (z_p - KG) \mid W$ | m | Positive = G rises |
| 7 | Lateral shift of G | $GG_y = w \cdot y_p \mid (W + w)$ | m | For addition |
| 8 | Heel angle | $\tan\theta = GG_y \mid GM'$ | dimensionless | θ in radians |
| 9 | MCT | $\text{MCT} = W \cdot GMT \mid (100 \cdot L)$ | t·m/cm | L is LBP |
| 10 | Trim change | $\Delta \text{Trim} = w \cdot x_p \mid \text{MCT}$ | cm | +ve = by head |
| 11 | New GM (wall-sided) | $GM' = KM - KG'$ | m | M is fixed |
| 12 | Vertical transfer effect | $\Delta G_z = w \cdot h \mid W$ | m | h = height moved |
| 13 | Lateral transfer effect | $GG_y = w \cdot (y_2 - y_1) \mid W$ | m | No draft change |
| 14 | Free-surface correction | $\Delta GM_{fs} = -\rho \cdot i \mid \Delta$ | m | For liquid in tank |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Discipline:** In all numerical KTU problems, **tonnes** are used for weights, **metres** for lengths, and **centimetres** for draft. The factor of **100** converts metres to centimetres in the TPC and MCT derivations.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

These principles are used in:

1. **Loading computer software** on cargo ships (LOGS, MASTERLOAD, CLOAD) to predict stability at every stage of a voyage
2. **Submarine operations** for ballasting to control depth
3. **Warship damage control** — flooding and counter-flooding decisions
4. **Heavy-lift operations** — coordinating crane lifts to avoid excessive heel during cargo transfer
5. **Offshore platforms** — ballasting for tow-out and up-ending operations
6. **Port state control** — verifying ship stability certificates (e.g., Grain Loading Code, IMSBC Code)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Draft Change from Added Weight (Parallel Sinkage)

**Statement:** When a weight **w** is added at the center of flotation, the ship sinks by a depth Δd such that the additional buoyancy equals w.

**Starting from Archimedes' principle:**

The ship must displace an additional volume **ΔV** of water whose weight equals the added weight **w**:

$$w = \rho \cdot g \cdot \Delta V \quad \text{(in SI, mass terms: } w = \rho \cdot \Delta V\text{)}$$

For a small parallel sinkage **Δd**, the additional displaced volume is:

$$\Delta V = A \cdot \Delta d$$

where **A** is the waterplane area. Substituting:

$$w = \rho \cdot A \cdot \Delta d \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta d = \frac{w}{\rho \cdot A}$$

**To convert to cm and tonnes (practical shipboard units):**

Let **w** be in tonnes, **A** in m², **ρ** in t/m³, and **Δd** in cm:

$$w = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot \Delta d}{100}$$

Solving for **Δd** in cm:

$$\Delta d = \frac{w \cdot 100}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{w}{\text{TPC}}$$

**Defining TPC explicitly:**

$$\boxed{\text{TPC} = \frac{\rho \cdot A}{100} \quad \text{[tonnes per cm]}}$$

> **Logic of the conversion:** We divide by 100 because 1 m³ = 100 cm × 100 cm × 100 cm → 1 m² × 1 cm = 0.01 m³. So the mass per cm of sinkage for 1 m² of waterplane = ρ × 0.01 = ρ/100 tonnes.

## 3.2 Derivation: Virtual Rise of G

**Statement:** When weight **w** is added at height **z** above the keel, the change in KG can be expressed as a "virtual" rise referenced to the original displacement W.

**Starting from the parallel-axis moment formula:**

$$KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{W \cdot KG + w \cdot z}{W + w}$$

**Rewriting:**

$$KG_{\text{new}} - KG = \frac{W \cdot KG + w \cdot z - KG \cdot (W + w)}{W + w}$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$W \cdot KG + w \cdot z - W \cdot KG - w \cdot KG = w \cdot (z - KG)$$

Therefore:

$$KG_{\text{new}} - KG = \frac{w \cdot (z - KG)}{W + w}$$

**Defining the virtual rise** (calculated as if W remained unchanged):

$$\boxed{\Delta G_v = \frac{w \cdot (z - KG)}{W}}$$

> **Note:** For small w ≪ W, the denominator (W + w) ≈ W, so the virtual rise ΔG_v ≈ the actual change in KG.

## 3.3 Derivation: Heel Angle from Off-Center Added Weight

**Statement:** The angle of list θ is found by balancing the heeling moment caused by the off-center weight with the righting moment of the ship at that angle.

**Step 1 — Compute the lateral shift of G:**

$$GG_y = \frac{w \cdot y_p}{W + w}$$

**Step 2 — Heeling moment = w · y_p** (moment of the added weight about the centerline at the waterline level; for small y, approximated as the heeling arm about G).

**Step 3 — At equilibrium list, heeling moment = righting moment:**

For a wall-sided ship, the righting moment at small heel θ is:

$$\text{Righting Moment} = W \cdot GM' \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot GM' \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4 — Equating heeling and righting moments:**

$$w \cdot y_p = (W + w) \cdot GM' \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small angles, sinθ ≈ tanθ:

$$\sin\theta \approx \frac{w \cdot y_p}{(W + w) \cdot GM'}$$

Since **GG_y = w · y_p / (W + w)**, we can also write:

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \frac{GG_y}{GM'}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm (MCT)

**Statement:** MCT is the moment (in t·m) required to change the trim by 1 cm.

**Step 1 — Trim T** is defined as the difference between forward and aft drafts:

$$T = d_F - d_A$$

A change in trim **ΔT** corresponds to the ship rotating about its center of flotation by an angle **φ**:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\Delta T}{L}$$

**Step 2 — The moment to change trim by 1 cm is related to longitudinal stability:**

For small angles:

$$MCT = \frac{W \cdot GMT}{100 \cdot L}$$

where the **100** converts the trim from cm to m. Derivation sketch:

The ship's resistance to trim is analogous to its resistance to heel, but in the longitudinal plane. By substituting the longitudinal second moment of waterplane area and using the same small-angle approximation, one arrives at the same form as the transverse formula:

$$\text{MCT} = \frac{\Delta \cdot GMT}{100 \cdot L}$$

> **Memory aid:** The longitudinal GM (GMT) is much larger than transverse GM (typically 100–300 m for cargo ships vs. 0.5–2 m transverse), so MCT values are large (typically 50–500 t·m/cm for cargo ships).

## 3.5 Derivation: Effect of Vertical Transference on GM

**Statement:** When weight **w** is moved vertically by height **h** (positive upward), G shifts by ΔG_z = w·h / W.

**Step 1 — Original moment of G about keel** = W · KG.

**Step 2 — After transfer, the moment of the moved weight about the keel changes by w · h**, so the new moment is:

$$W \cdot KG_{\text{new}} = W \cdot KG + w \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Solving for new KG:**

$$KG_{\text{new}} = KG + \frac{w \cdot h}{W}$$

**Step 4 — For a wall-sided ship (KM constant), the change in GM is:**

$$GM_{\text{new}} = KM - KG_{\text{new}} = KM - KG - \frac{w \cdot h}{W} = GM_{\text{old}} - \frac{w \cdot h}{W}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta GM = -\frac{w \cdot h}{W}}$$

> **Sign convention:** If the weight is moved **upward** (h > 0), ΔGM is **negative** (stability decreases). If moved **downward** (h < 0), ΔGM is **positive** (stability increases).

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example A — Loading at Center of Flotation

**Given:** A ship of displacement W = 8000 t, original KG = 6.2 m, TPC = 20, KM = 7.5 m. Cargo of 100 t is loaded at the center of flotation, 4 m above the keel.

**Find:** New draft, new KG, new GM.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta d = \frac{100}{20} = 5 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(ship sinks 5 cm)}$$

$$KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{8000 \times 6.2 + 100 \times 4}{8000 + 100} = \frac{49600 + 400}{8100} = \frac{50000}{8100} \approx 6.173 \text{ m}$$

$$GM_{\text{new}} = 7.5 - 6.173 = 1.327 \text{ m}$$

**Check using virtual rise:**

$$\Delta G_v = \frac{100 \times (4 - 6.2)}{8000} = \frac{-220}{8000} = -0.0275 \text{ m}$$

Negative → G falls → GM increases. Consistent with calculation.

### Example B — Loading Off-Center (Lateral)

**Given:** Same ship, but cargo is loaded 3 m to port (y = −3 m).

**Find:** List angle.

**Solution:**

$$GG_y = \frac{100 \times (-3)}{8100} = -0.0370 \text{ m} \quad \text{(G shifts to port)}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{-0.0370}{1.327} = -0.0279 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta \approx -1.6° \text{ (list to port)}$$

### Example C — Vertical Transference

**Given:** A ship of W = 5000 t, KG = 5.0 m, KM = 6.0 m (so GM = 1.0 m). A 200 t weight originally at z = 8 m is lowered to z = 2 m.

**Find:** Change in GM.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta G_z = \frac{200 \times (2 - 8)}{5000} = \frac{-1200}{5000} = -0.24 \text{ m}$$

$$KG_{\text{new}} = 5.0 + (-0.24) = 4.76 \text{ m}$$

$$GM_{\text{new}} = 6.0 - 4.76 = 1.24 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta GM = +0.24 \text{ m} \quad \text{(stability improved)}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: Effect of Weight Change on a Ship

The following block-level functional architecture diagram summarizes the logical flow of how addition, removal, and transference of weight affect the ship's draft, stability, and attitude.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WEIGHT OPERATION ON SHIP] --> B[ADDITION OF WEIGHT W]
    A --> C[REMOVAL OF WEIGHT W]
    A --> D[TRANSFERENCE OF WEIGHT W]
    
    B --> B1[NEW DISPLACEMENT W PLUS W]
    B --> B2[DRAFT CHANGE W DIVIDED BY TPC]
    B --> B3[NEW KG BY WEIGHTED AVERAGE]
    B --> B4[LIST OR TRIM IF OFF CENTER]
    
    B1 --> E[PARALLEL SINKAGE OCCURS]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> F[NEW GM EQUALS KM MINUS NEW KG]
    B4 --> G[HEEL ANGLE TANT EQUALS GG_Y BY GM]
    B4 --> H[TRIM CHANGE W TIMES X DIVIDED BY MCT]
    
    C --> C1[NEW DISPLACEMENT W MINUS W]
    C --> C2[DRAFT RISE NEGATIVE W BY TPC]
    C --> C3[NEW KG BY WEIGHTED AVERAGE]
    C --> C4[LIST OR TRIM AWAY FROM REMOVAL]
    
    C1 --> I[PARALLEL RISE OCCURS]
    C2 --> I
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> G
    C4 --> H
    
    D --> D1[LATERAL TRANSFER Y CHANGES]
    D --> D2[VERTICAL TRANSFER Z CHANGES]
    D --> D3[LONGITUDINAL TRANSFER X CHANGES]
    
    D1 --> J[NO DRAFT CHANGE SHIFTS G]
    D2 --> K[DIRECTLY CHANGES GM STABILITY]
    D3 --> L[CHANGES TRIM ONLY]
    
    F --> M[FINAL STABILITY GM ASSESSMENT]
    G --> M
    H --> N[FINAL ATTITUDE LIST AND TRIM]
    K --> M
    J --> M
    L --> N
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Virtual Rise / Fall of G Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[WEIGHT W AT HEIGHT Z] --> Q{IS Z GREATER THAN KG}
    Q -->|YES| R[GG VIRTUAL POSITIVE G RISES]
    Q -->|NO| S[GG VIRTUAL NEGATIVE G FALLS]
    Q -->|EQUAL| T[NO CHANGE IN G]
    
    R --> U[GM DECREASES LOSS OF STABILITY]
    S --> V[GM INCREASES GAIN OF STABILITY]
    T --> W[GM UNCHANGED]
    
    U --> X[EXAMPLES DECK CARGO UPPER STACKS]
    V --> Y[EXAMPLES BALLAST DOUBLE BOTTOM TANK]
    W --> Z[EXAMPLES WEIGHT AT ORIGINAL G HEIGHT]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block: Addition vs. Removal vs. Transference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ADD
        A1[WEIGHT ADDED AT POINT X Y Z] --> A2[SINKAGE PLUS LIST PLUS TRIM]
        A2 --> A3[NEW KG BY MOMENT BALANCE]
        A3 --> A4[NEW GM EQUALS KM MINUS KG]
    end
    
    subgraph REM
        R1[WEIGHT REMOVED FROM POINT X Y Z] --> R2[RISE PLUS LIST PLUS TRIM]
        R2 --> R3[NEW KG BY MOMENT BALANCE]
        R3 --> R4[NEW GM EQUALS KM MINUS KG]
    end
    
    subgraph TRANS
        T1[WEIGHT MOVED FROM P1 TO P2] --> T2{CHANGES}
        T2 --> T3[LATERAL CAUSES LIST ONLY]
        T2 --> T4[VERTICAL CHANGES GM ONLY]
        T2 --> T5[LONGITUDINAL CAUSES TRIM ONLY]
    end
    
    ADD --> COMP[COMPARATIVE EFFECT]
    REM --> COMP
    TRANS --> COMP
    COMP --> OUT[CRITICAL FOR LOADING COMPUTER STABILITY ASSESSMENT]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Virtual Rise of G". When does it lead to a loss of stability?**

**Model Answer:**

The virtual rise of G is the change in the position of the ship's center of gravity G, expressed as if the original displacement W remained unchanged, when a weight w is added at a height z above the keel.

$$\text{Virtual Rise of G} = \frac{w \cdot (z - KG)}{W}$$

It is **positive** (G effectively rises) when the added weight is placed at a height **above** the original G (z > KG). A positive virtual rise causes a **loss of GM and hence loss of initial transverse stability**, because:

$$GM_{\text{new}} = GM_{\text{old}} - \text{Virtual Rise of G}$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**A ship of 6000 t displacement has a TPC of 18. Find the change in draft if 90 t of cargo is loaded at the center of flotation.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\Delta d = \frac{w}{\text{TPC}} = \frac{90}{18} = 5 \text{ cm}$$

The ship sinks by **5 cm** in even-keel condition, since the cargo is loaded at the center of flotation F (no list or trim is induced).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Discipline (Most Common Error):** Students lose 2–3 marks by mixing up positive/negative signs for direction of heel/trim, or for upward/downward transfer. Always draw a sketch with axes marked.
> 2. **Units Confusion:** TPC is in **t/cm** but Δd comes out in **cm**. MCT is in **t·m/cm** but trim comes out in **cm**. Do not convert to metres prematurely.
> 3. **Wall-Sided Assumption:** When the problem says "assume ship is wall-sided", you MUST take KM' = KM. Otherwise, full credit is lost.
> 4. **Virtual vs. Actual Rise:** For small weights, virtual rise ≈ actual rise. For large weights (e.g., w > 10% of W), use the exact formula: ΔKG = w(z − KG)/(W + w).
> 5. **TPC and Density:** Forgetting to use ρ = 1.025 for seawater vs. 1.000 for freshwater can change the answer by 2.5%.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A vessel of displacement 12 000 t, original KG = 7.2 m, and KM = 8.4 m is floating at a draft of 8.5 m. The vessel has TPC = 24 and MCT = 250 t·m/cm. The length between perpendiculars is 150 m.**

**(a) 300 t of cargo is loaded at a point 2 m to starboard, 10 m above the keel, and 6 m forward of midships. Determine the new draft, the new GM, the list angle, and the trim.**

**(b) Subsequently, 100 t of cargo is transferred from a position 12 m above the keel to a position 3 m above the keel (at the same longitudinal and transverse position). Find the change in GM and comment on the stability of the vessel.**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: New displacement and parallel sinkage**

$$W' = 12000 + 300 = 12300 \text{ t}$$

$$\Delta d_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{300}{24} = 12.5 \text{ cm}$$

The ship sinks by 12.5 cm in still water (no list/trim component from this).

**Step 2: New KG (vertical position of G)**

$$KG' = \frac{12000 \times 7.2 + 300 \times 10}{12300} = \frac{86400 + 3000}{12300} = \frac{89400}{12300} \approx 7.268 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: New GM (wall-sided ship: KM is fixed at 8.4 m)**

$$GM' = KM - KG' = 8.4 - 7.268 = 1.132 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: List angle (lateral shift of G)**

$$GG_y = \frac{300 \times 2}{12300} = 0.0488 \text{ m}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{GG_y}{GM'} = \frac{0.0488}{1.132} = 0.0431$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0431) \approx 2.47° \text{ list to starboard}$$

**Step 5: Trim (longitudinal moment and MCT)**

$$\Delta \text{Trim} = \frac{w \cdot x_p}{\text{MCT}} = \frac{300 \times 6}{250} = 7.2 \text{ cm by the head}$$

> **Final answer (a):** Sinkage 12.5 cm, new GM = 1.132 m, list = 2.47° to starboard, trim = 7.2 cm by the head.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Identify the operation as a vertical transfer**

Weight moved: w = 100 t
Height moved: h = 3 − 12 = −9 m (moved **down** by 9 m)

**Step 2: Compute the change in KG**

$$\Delta G_z = \frac{w \cdot h}{W'} = \frac{100 \times (-9)}{12300} = -0.0732 \text{ m}$$

G falls by 7.32 cm.

**Step 3: New KG and new GM**

$$KG'' = KG' + \Delta G_z = 7.268 - 0.0732 = 7.195 \text{ m}$$

$$GM'' = 8.4 - 7.195 = 1.205 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Change in GM**

$$\Delta GM = GM'' - GM' = 1.205 - 1.132 = +0.0732 \text{ m} \approx +7.32 \text{ cm}$$

**Stability Comment:** Since the weight was lowered (moved down), G falls and GM **increases** by 7.32 cm. The vessel becomes **more stable** (more stiff). However, excessively high GM causes jerky, uncomfortable rolling motion, so the increase here is generally favourable and within acceptable limits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**A bulk carrier of displacement 18 000 t, KG = 8.5 m, KM = 9.3 m, TPC = 28, is floating at even keel. Its length LBP is 160 m and MCT is 320 t·m/cm.**

**(a) 400 t of cargo is loaded at a point 4 m to port, 3 m aft of midships, and 6 m above the keel. Find the change in draft, new GM, list, and trim.**

**(b) The same cargo (400 t) is then discharged and instead 200 t is moved from the upper deck (10 m above keel) to the double bottom tank (2 m above keel), all at the same transverse and longitudinal position. Find the change in GM and comment on stability.**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: New displacement and sinkage**

$$W' = 18000 + 400 = 18400 \text{ t}$$

$$\Delta d = \frac{400}{28} = 14.29 \text{ cm} \approx 14.3 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: New KG**

$$KG' = \frac{18000 \times 8.5 + 400 \times 6}{18400} = \frac{153000 + 2400}{18400} = \frac{155400}{18400} \approx 8.446 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: New GM**

$$GM' = 9.3 - 8.446 = 0.854 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: List (port = y = −4 m)**

$$GG_y = \frac{400 \times (-4)}{18400} = -0.0870 \text{ m}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{-0.0870}{0.854} = -0.1019$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(-0.1019) \approx -5.82° \text{ list to port}$$

> Note: This is a substantial list — the ship may be approaching operational limits. Real loading computers would flag this.

**Step 5: Trim (x = −3 m, i.e., 3 m aft)**

$$\Delta \text{Trim} = \frac{400 \times (-3)}{320} = -3.75 \text{ cm}$$

Negative → trim **by the stern** of 3.75 cm. The stern sinks further and the bow rises.

> **Final answer (a):** Sinkage 14.3 cm, new GM = 0.854 m, list = 5.82° to port, trim = 3.75 cm by the stern.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Vertical transfer of 200 t through h = 2 − 10 = −8 m (moved down by 8 m)**

Displacement unchanged at W = 18 000 t.

**Step 2: Change in KG**

$$\Delta G_z = \frac{200 \times (-8)}{18000} = -0.0889 \text{ m}$$

G falls by 8.89 cm.

**Step 3: New KG and GM**

$$KG'' = 8.5 + (-0.0889) = 8.411 \text{ m}$$

$$GM'' = 9.3 - 8.411 = 0.889 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Change in GM**

$$\Delta GM = 0.889 - 0.800 = +0.089 \text{ m} = +8.9 \text{ cm}$$

Wait — the *original* GM was 9.3 − 8.5 = 0.8 m. So:

$$\Delta GM = 0.889 - 0.8 = +0.089 \text{ m}$$

**Stability Comment:** The vertical lowering of 200 t over 8 m increases GM by 8.9 cm. Stability **improves** — the ship becomes stiffer. The double-bottom tank ballasting is a classic shipboard method of improving stability in damaged or partially-loaded conditions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- ✅ **Three Operations:** Addition, Removal, Transference (lateral / vertical / longitudinal).
- ✅ **Addition → sinkage; Removal → rise.** Both follow Δd = ±w/TPC.
- ✅ **TPC** = ρ · A / 100 [tonnes per cm immersion]. Seawater ρ = 1.025 t/m³.
- ✅ **New KG** = weighted average: $KG' = (W \cdot KG \pm w \cdot z) / (W \pm w)$.
- ✅ **Virtual Rise of G** = w · (z − KG) / W. Positive = loss of stability; negative = gain.
- ✅ **Wall-Sided Ship:** KM is constant; M is fixed in space.
- ✅ **New GM** = KM − KG' (for wall-sided ships).
- ✅ **List angle** = tan⁻¹ (GG_y / GM') where GG_y = w · y_p / (W + w).
- ✅ **MCT** = W · GMT / (100 · L) [t·m/cm]. GMT is longitudinal metacentric height.
- ✅ **Trim** = w · x_p / MCT. Positive = by the head, negative = by the stern.
- ✅ **Lateral transfer:** No draft change; G shifts horizontally; induces list or trim.
- ✅ **Vertical transfer:** No draft change; G shifts vertically; ΔGM = −w · h / W.
- ✅ **Stability rule of thumb:** Heavy weights **low** = stable; heavy weights **high** = unstable.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** x positive forward, y positive starboard, z positive upward.
- ✅ **Free surface effect** (advanced): adds a negative correction ΔGM_fs = −ρ · i / Δ for any liquid in a partly-filled tank.
- ✅ **Real-world link:** Loading computers on modern ships automate all of these calculations for every stage of a voyage.

> **Final note for KTU 2024 students:** When solving any problem, **always start with a free-body sketch** showing W, w, the point of application (x, y, z), and the original G. This eliminates 90% of sign errors that cost marks. Use the **virtual rise of G** as a sanity check on your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Suspended Weights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Suspended Weights

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Suspended Weight** in naval architecture is defined as a mass that is supported by a structure of the ship at a point vertically separated from the ship's center of gravity (**G**), creating a **virtual shift in G** even though no actual mass has been added at a new location on the ship. The most common scenario occurs when a weight is suspended from a point located **above G** using shipboard cranes, derricks, davits, helicopter decks, or transfer rigs.

Mathematically, when a weight **w** is suspended at a vertical distance **h** from the ship's center of gravity **G**, the supporting point and the weight itself form an **internal couple** that is statically equivalent to the weight being located at G plus a virtual upward shift of G itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Suspended weights are a critical cause of unexpected loss of intact stability. The KTU Board routinely tests this concept because it directly impacts **SOLAS Chapter II-1** intact stability criteria and forms the basis of cargo operation safety protocols.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners

Imagine you are standing on a weighing scale on a perfectly stable wooden plank over water. Your **true** center of gravity is at your chest.

- **Case 1:** You hold a heavy dumbbell **at chest level** → The scale reads your weight + dumbbell weight, but the plank barely tilts because the extra weight is right at your center of gravity.
- **Case 2:** You raise the same dumbbell **above your head on a pole** → Suddenly the plank tips dangerously! The plank doesn't know you are tall; it only "feels" that something is pulling the top of the system sideways relative to the base.

In this analogy, the plank is the **ship**, your body is the **ship's structure**, and the dumbbell on the pole is the **suspended weight**. The plank's stability reduces dramatically because the effective center of gravity has **virtually risen**, reducing the metacentric height (GM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A weight suspended high above the ship behaves exactly like adding mass to a tall, top-heavy ship. The "virtual rise" of G is independent of the actual location of the ship's hard mass — it is a **dynamic equilibrium effect** caused by the support couple.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics Used in This Topic

The following standard units and symbols are used universally in KTU board examinations:

- **W** = Ship's Displacement (in **tonnes** or **kN**, displacement weight)
- **w** = Suspended Weight (in **tonnes**)
- **h** = Vertical distance from G to the point of suspension P (in **meters**)
- **GG'** = Virtual rise of the center of gravity (in **meters**)
- **GM** = Original metacentric height of the ship (in **meters**)
- **Effective GM** = New metacentric height after the suspended weight is in place (in **meters**)
- **θ** = Angle of heel/list caused by the suspended weight moment (in **degrees**)
- **GMT** = Transverse metacentric height (used in this context)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Mechanics — Why Does a Suspended Weight Affect G?

The phenomenon can be broken down into a strict cause-and-effect chain:

1. **A weight `w` is supported by a structure** (e.g., crane, davit, hook) attached to the ship at point **P**.
2. **The point P experiences an upward reaction force** equal to `w` (assuming the weight is in static equilibrium).
3. **The center of mass of `w` itself is at a lower position** than P (it cannot coincide with the hook).
4. **These two equal and opposite forces, separated by a vertical distance `h`, form a couple** of moment `M = w × h`.
5. **This couple, when transferred to the ship's center of gravity G**, is equivalent to the weight `w` acting at G plus an **upward virtual displacement** of G.
6. **The virtual shift GG' is calculated** by taking moments of the original couple about the new (raised) G.
7. **The metacentric height reduces by exactly GG'**, leaving a new effective stability margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why behind the formula:** A couple can be translated to any point on a rigid body without changing its external effect. By translating the couple to the new virtual G, we find that G must rise by `GG' = w·h / W` to maintain moment equilibrium.

## 2.2 Conditions Affecting the Magnitude of the Effect

The severity of the stability reduction depends on three primary factors:

- **Magnitude of the suspended weight (`w`):** Larger `w` → larger `GG'`.
- **Height of the point of suspension above G (`h`):** Greater `h` → larger `GG'`. This is the most dangerous factor because cargo operations often involve very high cranes (10–30 m).
- **Ship's displacement (`W`):** Smaller `W` → larger `GG'`. Smaller vessels are disproportionately affected.

## 2.3 Special Case — Weight Suspended Below G

In rare situations (e.g., a heavy keel block being lowered, an underwater cable), a weight may be suspended **below G**. In this case:

- The couple is **reversed** in direction.
- The virtual shift is **downward** (apparent fall of G).
- The metacentric height **increases** (stability improves).
- This is the principle behind **external keels** and **drogues** used to enhance stability.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Suspended weight analysis is critical for:

- **Heavy-lift vessel operations** (semi-submersibles, jack-up rigs)
- **Helicopter-deck ships** (especially during landing with rotors engaged)
- **Lifeboat launch and recovery** (davit operation)
- **Crane barge stability** during offshore construction
- **Diving support vessels (DSVs)** with bells lowered over the side
- **Ship-to-ship (STS) transfer operations** at sea
- **Naval replenishment-at-sea (RAS)** operations

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Condition / Notes** |
|:-----:|:-------------|:-----------:|:---------:|:-----------------------|
| 1 | Virtual rise of G | $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W}$ | meters | Suspended weight above G |
| 2 | Virtual fall of G | $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W}$ | meters | Suspended weight below G (sign convention) |
| 3 | New effective GM | $GM_{new} = GM - GG'$ | meters | Standard condition (weight above G) |
| 4 | Stability criterion | $GM_{new} > 0$ | meters | Required for positive stability |
| 5 | Couple moment due to suspension | $M_{susp} = w \cdot h$ | tonne·m | Internal couple of ship |
| 6 | Heel angle from off-center suspension | $\tan \theta = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot GMT}$ | radians | `d` = transverse distance of P from CL |
| 7 | Trim moment from longitudinal suspension | $MT = w \cdot l$ | tonne·m | `l` = longitudinal distance from midships |
| 8 | Change in trim due to suspension | $\Delta Trim = \dfrac{MT}{MCT \cdot L}$ | meters | Use ship's Moment to Change Trim (MCT) |
| 9 | Equivalent free surface correction | $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W}$ | meters | Conceptually similar to FS effect |
| 10 | Critical suspended weight for zero GM | $w_{crit} = \dfrac{GM \cdot W}{h}$ | tonnes | Threshold above which ship capsizes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always convert the final heel angle from radians to degrees by multiplying by `180/π ≈ 57.2958`. Examiners deduct marks for missing unit conversions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Virtual Rise of G (GG' = w·h / W)

### Step 1 — Setup of the Physical System

Consider a ship floating at rest in still water with a total displacement **W** and a center of gravity at **G**. A weight **w** is suspended from a point **P** located at a vertical distance **h** above G on the ship's centerline.

### Step 2 — Identify the Force Couple

The support structure exerts an **upward force of magnitude w** at point P. The weight **w** itself acts **downward** at its own center of mass, which is below P. The two forces are:

- **Upward force at P:** magnitude `w`, direction `+z`
- **Downward force at CG of weight:** magnitude `w`, direction `-z`
- **Vertical separation between them:** `h`
- **Moment of the couple:** $M = w \times h$

### Step 3 — Translate the Couple to the New Center of Gravity G'

A pure couple can be translated to any point on a rigid body without changing the net force or moment. To translate this couple to the ship's center of gravity, we define a **new point G'** such that the moment of the original couple about G' equals the moment produced by adding weight `w` at G'.

### Step 4 — Take Moments About G'

Let G' be displaced **upward** by a distance `x` (= GG') from the original G. Taking moments about G' of the original couple:

$$M_{about\,G'} = w \times (h - x) - w \times (-x) = w \times h - w \times x + w \times x = w \times h$$

Wait — this is the original couple. We need to interpret it as the *original* G being raised by `x`. To balance the system, the equivalent weight `w` placed at the **new G'** must produce the same moment as the original system.

### Step 5 — Equate the Moments

For static equilibrium, the moment of the original couple about the new G' must equal zero when expressed in terms of the equivalent system (weight `w` placed at the new G'). Therefore:

$$w \cdot h = W \cdot x + w \cdot x$$

Neglecting the small term `w·x` (since `w << W`):

$$w \cdot h = W \cdot x$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the Virtual Rise x

$$\begin{aligned}
W \cdot x &= w \cdot h \\
x &= \frac{w \cdot h}{W} \\
\therefore GG' &= \frac{w \cdot h}{W}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The ship's center of gravity virtually rises by `w·h/W` meters. This means the metacenter `M` stays fixed (since W and B remain the same), but G moves up to G', reducing the righting lever arm `GZ` for any given angle of heel.

## 3.2 Derivation of the New Effective Metacentric Height

The original transverse metacentric height is:

$$GMT_{original} = KM - KG$$

After the suspended weight is added, the new KG becomes:

$$KG_{new} = KG_{original} + GG' = KG_{original} + \frac{w \cdot h}{W}$$

Since KM is unchanged (the underwater form of the hull is the same), the new metacentric height is:

$$\begin{aligned}
GMT_{new} &= KM - KG_{new} \\
&= KM - \left( KG_{original} + \frac{w \cdot h}{W} \right) \\
&= (KM - KG_{original}) - \frac{w \cdot h}{W} \\
GMT_{new} &= GMT_{original} - \frac{w \cdot h}{W}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cases of Suspended Weights

| **Case** | **Configuration** | **Direction of GG'** | **Effect on GM** | **Stability Impact** |
|:--------:|:------------------|:--------------------:|:----------------:|:---------------------|
| 1 | Weight `w` suspended above G on centerline | Upward (+GG') | Reduces | **Dangerous** — most common failure case |
| 2 | Weight `w` suspended below G on centerline | Downward (-GG') | Increases | **Beneficial** — rarely engineered intentionally |
| 3 | Weight `w` at level of G (h = 0) | Zero (GG' = 0) | No change | Neutral |
| 4 | Weight `w` off-centerline, above G | Upward + lateral | Reduces GM + causes list | **Most dangerous** — combines both effects |
| 5 | Weight `w` off-centerline, below G | Downward + lateral | Increases GM + causes list | Stability improved, but list remains |
| 6 | Weight `w` at G with horizontal offset | Zero GG', heel only | No change in GM | Causes static list only |
| 7 | Swinging suspended weight (pendulum motion) | Variable | Variable (oscillates) | Can induce **parametric roll** and resonance capsizing |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1 — Standard Reduction in GM

**Given:**
- Displacement `W = 12,000` tonnes
- Original `KG = 6.5` m, `KM = 7.2` m → `GMT = 0.7` m
- Suspended weight `w = 80` tonnes
- Point of suspension at height `15` m above the keel

**Find:** New effective GMT and the virtual rise of G.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
GG' &= \frac{w \cdot h}{W} = \frac{80 \times (15 - 6.5)}{12{,}000} \\
GG' &= \frac{80 \times 8.5}{12{,}000} = \frac{680}{12{,}000} \\
GG' &= 0.0567 \text{ m} \\
GMT_{new} &= 0.7 - 0.0567 = 0.6433 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** GMT reduces from 0.70 m to 0.643 m — a **8.1% reduction** in stability.

### Example 2 — Critical Suspended Weight (Cap-Size Threshold)

**Given:**
- Displacement `W = 3,000` tonnes
- Original `GMT = 0.15` m
- Suspended weight `w = 25` tonnes
- Point of suspension at height `h = 12` m above G

**Find:** Whether the ship remains stable.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
GG' &= \frac{w \cdot h}{W} = \frac{25 \times 12}{3{,}000} = \frac{300}{3{,}000} = 0.10 \text{ m} \\
GMT_{new} &= 0.15 - 0.10 = +0.05 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The ship is still **marginally stable** with GMT = 0.05 m — operation is unsafe and should be suspended.

### Example 3 — Capsize Case (Negative GMT)

**Given:**
- Displacement `W = 2,500` tonnes
- Original `GMT = 0.08` m
- Suspended weight `w = 30` tonnes
- Point of suspension at height `h = 10` m above G

**Find:** Critical condition.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
GG' &= \frac{w \cdot h}{W} = \frac{30 \times 10}{2{,}500} = \frac{300}{2{,}500} = 0.12 \text{ m} \\
GMT_{new} &= 0.08 - 0.12 = -0.04 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The ship becomes **NEGATIVELY stable** (GMT = -0.04 m). It will capsize or list dangerously. The cargo operation must be aborted immediately.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanics of Suspended Weight Effect

The following block diagram illustrates the complete cause-and-effect chain from the physical setup to the final stability outcome.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship Floating at Rest Displacement W] --> B[Suspended Weight w at Point P]
    B --> C[Upward Support Reaction at P]
    B --> D[Downward Force at CG of Weight w]
    C --> E[Internal Couple Formation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Couple Magnitude M equals w times h]
    F --> G[Virtual Rise of G by w times h divided by W]
    G --> H[New KG increases by GG prime]
    H --> I[KM unchanged by form stability]
    I --> J[New GMT equals Old GMT minus GG prime]
    J --> K{Is New GMT greater than zero?}
    K --> L[YES Stable Ship Reduced Margin]
    K --> M[NO Negative GMT CAPSIZE RISK]
    M --> N[Abort Heavy Lift Operation]
    L --> O[Continue Operation with Caution]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Suspended Weight Operations

This block diagram maps the engineering decision-making process before and during a heavy-lift operation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Start: Plan Heavy Lift Operation] --> S2[Determine Ship Displacement W]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Original GMT from Stability Booklet]
    S3 --> S4[Identify Suspended Weight w from Cargo Manifest]
    S4 --> S5[Determine Height h of Suspension Point above G]
    S5 --> S6[Calculate Virtual Rise GG prime]
    S6 --> S7{Is GMT minus GG prime positive?}
    S7 --> S8[YES Safe to Proceed]
    S7 --> S9[NO Review Alternatives]
    S9 --> S10[Option 1 Reduce Lift Height]
    S9 --> S11[Option 2 Increase Ballast to Raise W]
    S9 --> S12[Option 3 Use Shore Crane]
    S9 --> S13[Option 4 Use Tandem Lift]
    S10 --> S5
    S11 --> S5
    S12 --> S8
    S13 --> S8
    S8 --> S14[Monitor Heel and Trim Continuously]
    S14 --> S15[Complete Operation]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparative Effect of Suspended Weights vs. Free Surface Effect

This diagram maps the conceptual similarity between suspended weights and the free surface effect — a frequently tested comparison in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Suspended Weight Effect] --> B1[Virtual Rise of G]
    A2[Free Surface Effect] --> B2[Virtual Rise of G]
    B1 --> C1[Reduces GMT]
    B2 --> C2[Reduces GMT]
    C1 --> D1[Loss of Stability]
    C2 --> D2[Loss of Stability]
    A1 --> E1[Caused by Support Couple w times h]
    A2 --> E2[Caused by Shifting Liquid Moment]
    E1 --> F1[GG prime equals w times h over W]
    E2 --> F2[GG prime equals rho times i over W]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic Description

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualization Note (since physical drawings are not feasible in Mermaid):**
>
> The physical setup can be visualized as a vertical column of forces:
>
> ```
>         ↑ w  (Reaction at suspension point P, height h above G)
>         |
>         |
>         |  ← Vertical distance h
>         |
>         ↓ w  (Weight w at its own CG, typically near original G)
>         |
>         |
>         • G  (Original ship center of gravity)
>         |
>         |
>         ↑ W  (Total buoyancy force at center of buoyancy B)
>         |
>         • B  (Center of buoyancy on centerline)
>         |
>         ↓ W  (Total weight of ship, assumed at G)
>         |
>         |
>        ~~~ ~~~ ~~~  (Waterline)
> ```
>
> The two opposing `w` forces (upward at P and downward at CG of `w`) form the couple. The metacenter **M** lies on the centerline at height **KM** above the keel. The righting arm **GZ** at any heel angle θ is the lever that produces a restoring moment when **G is below M**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "suspended weight" as applicable to ship stability. Explain briefly how it affects the metacentric height of a ship.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **suspended weight** is a load that is supported by a structure of the ship (such as a crane, derrick, or davit) at a point vertically separated from the ship's center of gravity G, such that the supporting point and the weight itself form an internal couple. **(1 mark)**

This couple causes a **virtual rise of the center of gravity** by an amount $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W}$, where `w` is the suspended weight, `h` is the vertical distance from G to the point of suspension, and `W` is the ship's displacement. **(1 mark)**

The effective metacentric height reduces to $GMT_{new} = GMT - GG'$. If the suspended weight is too large or hung too high, the effective GMT may become negative, leading to loss of stability and potential capsize. **(1 mark)**

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare the effect of a suspended weight on stability with the free surface effect. Mention any one similarity and one difference.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Similarity (1 Mark):** Both suspended weights and free surface effect cause a **virtual rise of the center of gravity G**, thereby reducing the effective metacentric height GMT of the ship. Both are transient and disappear when the load is removed or the slack tank is pressed up.

**Difference (2 Marks):** A suspended weight arises from an **internal couple formed by a weight hanging below its support point**, and is governed by the formula $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W}$. The free surface effect, in contrast, arises from the **shifting moment of a liquid with a free surface** in a partially filled compartment, governed by the formula $GG' = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i}{W}$ where `i` is the transverse moment of inertia of the free surface. The physical mechanisms are different — one is structural suspension, the other is fluid dynamics.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1 Modified]**

**A general cargo vessel of displacement 8,500 tonnes has a center of gravity G at 5.8 m above the keel and a metacenter M at 6.5 m above the keel. During cargo operations at port, a heavy lift of 60 tonnes is suspended from a shipboard crane with the hook point 18 m above the keel.**

**(a) Calculate the virtual rise of the ship's center of gravity. (7 marks)**

**(b) Determine the new effective metacentric height. Comment on whether the cargo operation is safe to continue. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify given data (1 mark):**
- W = 8,500 tonnes
- KG = 5.8 m
- KM = 6.5 m
- w = 60 tonnes
- Height of suspension point above keel = 18 m
- Hence, height of suspension point above G = 18 − 5.8 = 12.2 m

**Step 2 — Apply the suspended weight formula (2 marks):**
The vertical distance from G to the point of suspension is:

$$h = 18 - 5.8 = 12.2 \text{ m}$$

The virtual rise of G is given by:

$$GG' = \frac{w \cdot h}{W}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and calculate (2 marks):**

$$GG' = \frac{60 \times 12.2}{8{,}500} = \frac{732}{8{,}500} = 0.0861 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Present final answer with units and interpretation (2 marks):**
The ship's center of gravity virtually rises by **GG' = 0.0861 m** (or approximately 86 mm). This is a significant virtual rise for a small vessel, and must be considered in conjunction with the original GMT.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Calculate the original GMT (1 mark):**

$$GMT_{original} = KM - KG = 6.5 - 5.8 = 0.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the modified GMT formula (2 marks):**

$$GMT_{new} = GMT_{original} - GG' = 0.7 - 0.0861 = 0.6139 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate safety (2 marks):**
The new GMT = **0.6139 m** is still positive, so the ship remains stable. However, the stability margin has reduced by:

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{0.0861}{0.7} \times 100 = 12.3\%$$

This is a substantial reduction. According to SOLAS intact stability criteria, the operation should be continued only if:
- The vessel has adequate reserve GMT (typically ≥ 0.15 m for general cargo).
- Weather and sea conditions are calm.
- The crane operator is experienced.
- Continuous monitoring of heel and draft is in place.

**Step 4 — Final recommendation (2 marks):**
The operation **can be continued with strict precautions**, including real-time monitoring of the stability information display, ensuring all ballast operations are coordinated, and aborting the lift if heel exceeds 2° or trim exceeds 0.5°.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2 Modified]**

**A small offshore support vessel of displacement 2,800 tonnes has its center of gravity G at 4.2 m above the keel. The transverse metacentric height (GMT) is 0.20 m. During a routine operation, a 40-tonne submersible is being lowered over the side using the ship's A-frame crane, with the suspension point 14 m above G.**

**(a) Determine the new effective GMT. State with reasons whether the operation is safe. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the GMT must remain at least 0.05 m for safe operation, calculate the maximum permissible suspended weight for the given suspension height of 14 m above G. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify data (1 mark):**
- W = 2,800 tonnes
- GMT_original = 0.20 m
- w = 40 tonnes
- h = 14 m (above G)

**Step 2 — Compute virtual rise (2 marks):**

$$GG' = \frac{w \cdot h}{W} = \frac{40 \times 14}{2{,}800} = \frac{560}{2{,}800} = 0.20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute new GMT (2 marks):**

$$GMT_{new} = GMT_{original} - GG' = 0.20 - 0.20 = 0.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result (2 marks):**
The new GMT is **exactly zero** — this is a critically dangerous condition. The ship is on the verge of neutral stability; any slight disturbance (wave action, crew movement, off-center load) can cause it to list and potentially capsize.

**Conclusion:** The operation is **NOT SAFE** and must be **aborted immediately**. The submersible should be either (i) supported by a separate vessel, (ii) the ship should take on additional ballast to increase W, or (iii) a different launch method should be used.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — State the safety condition (1 mark):**
For safe operation, $GMT_{new} \geq 0.05$ m.

$$GMT_{original} - \frac{w \cdot h}{W} \geq 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for w (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{w \cdot h}{W} &\leq GMT_{original} - 0.05 \\
\frac{w \cdot h}{W} &\leq 0.20 - 0.05 = 0.15 \text{ m} \\
w &\leq \frac{0.15 \times W}{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values (2 marks):**

$$w_{max} = \frac{0.15 \times 2{,}800}{14} = \frac{420}{14} = 30 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude and recommend (2 marks):**
The maximum permissible suspended weight is **30 tonnes** for the given configuration. Since the planned 40-tonne load exceeds this limit, the operator must either:
- Reduce the load to ≤ 30 tonnes,
- Lower the suspension point,
- Add ballast to increase W,
- Postpone the operation until conditions improve.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract KG from the hook height** — Many students use the hook height above the keel as `h`, but the correct `h` is the height **above G**, not the keel. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing up units** — Using kg instead of tonnes in the displacement leads to wrong answers by a factor of 1000. Always check units first. **(−1 to −3 marks)**
> 3. **Not stating the conclusion** — Numerical answers without a written safety comment (Safe/Not Safe) lose up to 2 marks in the final part.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert heel angle** — If heel angle is computed in radians, examiners expect either a conversion to degrees or an explicit "radians" unit. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Ignoring the `w << W` approximation** — In most KTU problems, the approximation $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W}$ is acceptable, but in extreme cases (e.g., very heavy lifts on small vessels), the more accurate formula $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W + w}$ should be used. Failing to mention this assumption can cost 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing the direction of GG'** — A weight suspended **above G** raises G (reduces stability). A weight suspended **below G** lowers G (increases stability). Sign errors are heavily penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 1, Effect of Suspended Weights:**
>
> - **Definition:** A weight supported by the ship at a point vertically separated from G, creating an internal couple.
> - **Core Formula:** $GG' = \dfrac{w \cdot h}{W}$ (where `h` is the vertical distance from G to suspension point).
> - **Effect on Stability:** $GMT_{new} = GMT_{original} - GG'$ when the weight is **above G**.
> - **Below G Case:** The effect is reversed — apparent fall of G, increase in GMT (used in rare stabilizing applications).
> - **Critical Suspended Weight:** $w_{crit} = \dfrac{GMT \cdot W}{h}$ — the threshold beyond which the ship becomes unstable.
> - **Three Danger Multipliers:** Large `w`, large `h`, small `W`.
> - **Most Common Real-World Scenario:** Heavy-lift cargo operation using a shipboard crane with the hook well above the deck.
> - **Heel Contribution:** If the suspension point is off-centerline at a transverse distance `d`, an additional list is induced: $\tan \theta = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot GMT}$.
> - **Trim Contribution:** If the suspension point is at a longitudinal distance `l` from midships, a trim moment of `w·l` tonne·m is induced.
> - **Comparison with Free Surface Effect:** Both reduce GMT by a virtual rise of G, but the physical mechanism differs (suspension couple vs. shifting liquid moment).
> - **SOLAS Implication:** Ships must ensure intact stability under all operational conditions, including cargo operations with suspended loads.
> - **Common Mistake:** Using hook height above keel instead of above G for `h`.
> - **Sign Convention:** Always define `h` as positive for above-G suspension and negative for below-G.
> - **Practical Safety Rule:** A good rule of thumb is to keep the effective GMT ≥ 0.15 m during all transient cargo operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Surface Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Free Surface Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Surface Effect (FSE):** A phenomenon in naval architecture wherein any liquid cargo, ballast, or water contained in a partially filled shipboard tank possesses a free surface (a surface open to atmospheric pressure). When the ship rolls or heels, this liquid shifts across the tank, and the resulting shift in the liquid's center of gravity produces a virtual rise in the vessel's overall center of gravity (G), thereby **reducing the effective metacentric height (GM)** and degrading the ship's transverse stability.

In formal KTU/IMO notation, this is referred to as the **Free Surface Correction (FSC)** applied to the intact stability criteria.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are carrying a tray with a half-filled glass of water. When you walk steadily, the water stays put and the tray feels balanced. Now imagine the same tray tilts slightly to one side (the ship heels). The water **sloshes** toward the lower edge of the glass, and the tray suddenly feels **tippy** — it seems to want to tip over more. That "extra tippiness" you feel is exactly what a ship experiences: the shifting liquid acts as a **destabilizing moment**, virtually pulling the ship's weight toward the lower side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant Highlight:** The destabilizing effect is **independent** of the actual weight (mass) of the liquid, but **directly proportional** to the **transverse moment of inertia of the free surface area** of the liquid. This is the single most important fact about FSE.

### 1.3 Why FSE Matters in Ship Design

- **Tankers** (oil, chemical, LNG) — most susceptible.
- **Bulk carriers** with partially filled holds.
- **Ships with slack ballast tanks.**
- **Warships** with fuel compensation or corrective tanks.

A ship that appears stable when upright (positive GM) may become **unstable (negative GM)** only after the free surface correction is applied. Hence, **FSE is non-negotiable** in stability booklets and loading manuals.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

1. **Ship is upright.** Liquid in a partially filled tank lies flat. The center of gravity of the liquid coincides with the centroid of the free surface.
2. **Ship heels by a small angle θ.** Gravity pulls the liquid toward the lower side; the liquid surface remains horizontal (due to gravity), but the tank is now inclined.
3. **The liquid's center of gravity shifts** laterally by a distance $\overline{gg'}$ toward the low side.
4. **The shift acts as a virtual rise in G** of the whole ship by an amount $\overline{GG'}$ parallel to the new vertical.
5. **The metacentric radius BM is unchanged** (it depends on ship displacement and waterplane area), but **GM is reduced**:
   
   $$GM_{\text{effective}} = GM - \overline{GG'}$$

6. **If $\overline{GG'} > GM$**, the ship becomes dynamically unstable (negative effective GM).

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- The liquid is **free to move**, so it always re-orients itself horizontally — this is the source of the shifting weight.
- The moment of inertia of the free surface ($i$ or $I_t$) quantifies **how much the liquid resists re-orientation** (or rather, how much lateral shift occurs for a given heel).
- A **wide, shallow tank** has a huge $i$ — terrible for stability.
- A **long, narrow tank** (longitudinal) has a small transverse $i$ — manageable for stability.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam favorites. Memorize the symbols, units, and applicability.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{i \cdot v}{V \cdot \rho_s}$ (or $\dfrac{i}{V} \cdot \rho_l$ for water) | Virtual rise of G due to free surface | $\overline{GG'}$ in m, $i$ in m⁴, $v$ in m³, $V$ in m³ |
| 2 | $i = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Transverse moment of inertia of a **rectangular** free surface | $L$ = length along ship, $B$ = athwartships width; both in m |
| 3 | $i = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$ | Transverse moment of inertia of a **circular** free surface | $D$ = diameter of circular tank |
| 4 | $\Delta \cdot \overline{GG'} = \rho_l \cdot i$ (when liquid is seawater) | Simplified form using displacement $\Delta$ | $\Delta$ in tonnes, $i$ in m⁴, gives $\overline{GG'}$ in m |
| 5 | $GM_{\text{eff}} = KM - KG - \overline{GG'}$ | Effective GM after FSE correction | All in metres |
| 6 | $T_{FS} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot i}{\Delta}$ | Free Surface Correction (FSC) per unit displacement | Non-dimensional form |
| 7 | $\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{\sum (\rho_l \cdot i)}{W}$ | Multiple tanks case (summation over all partially filled tanks) | $W$ = total ship displacement |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Loading Computer Inputs:** Every onboard stability computer (e.g., MARCAS, NOVACHECK) continuously computes FSC and warns the officer if $GM_{\text{eff}}$ falls below the regulatory minimum (typically **0.15 m** for passenger ships, varies for cargo).
- **Code Compliance:** SOLAS Chapter II-1, IMO A.749(18), and classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) all mandate FSE inclusion in damage stability and intact stability calculations.
- **Design Optimization:** Tank designers minimize $i$ by using **longitudinal subdivision bulkheads** (sloshing bulkheads) that effectively break a wide free surface into narrow strips.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Virtual Rise of G ($\overline{GG'}$) for a Single Rectangular Tank

**Given:**
- A rectangular tank of length $L$ (fore-aft) and breadth $B$ (athwartships), partially filled with liquid of density $\rho_l$.
- Ship displacement volume (or mass) $V$ (or $\Delta$).
- Ship heels by a small angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal centerline.

**Step 1 — Geometry of liquid shift.**

When the ship heels, the liquid surface remains horizontal, while the tank rotates. The wedge of liquid moves from the high side to the low side.

**Step 2 — Center of gravity shift of the liquid alone.**

The lateral shift of the liquid's center of gravity from the tank center is:

$$g g' = \dfrac{i \cdot \tan\theta}{v}$$

where $i$ is the **transverse moment of inertia of the free surface** about the ship's centerline, and $v$ is the **volume of liquid** in the tank.

> *Derivation note:* $i \cdot \tan\theta$ is the first moment of the shifted wedge volume about the centerline; dividing by $v$ gives the centroid shift.

**Step 3 — Convert to virtual rise of ship G.**

The moment created by the shift of liquid weight about the ship's centerline must equal an equivalent shift of the entire ship's G:

$$\Delta \cdot \overline{GG'} = \rho_l \cdot v \cdot g g'$$

Substituting $g g'$:

$$\Delta \cdot \overline{GG'} = \rho_l \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{i \cdot \tan\theta}{v} = \rho_l \cdot i \cdot \tan\theta$$

For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta \approx \theta$, and for an infinitesimal heel we treat the ratio:

$$\boxed{\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot i}{\Delta}}$$

**Step 4 — Rectangular tank substitution.**

For a rectangular free surface of length $L$ and breadth $B$:

$$i = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot \Delta}$$

**Step 5 — Multiple tanks (analytical matrix form).**

For $n$ partially filled tanks:

$$\overline{GG'_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \rho_{l,k} \cdot i_k$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** $\overline{GG'}$ is **independent of the depth of liquid** in the tank, and **independent of the heel angle** (for small angles). It depends only on the **geometry of the free surface** and the **ship's displacement**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A ship of displacement $\Delta = 10{,}000$ tonnes has a partially filled rectangular double-bottom tank. The tank has length $L = 12$ m, breadth $B = 8$ m, and is filled with seawater to a depth of 0.6 m. Calculate the loss of GM due to free surface effect.

**Step 1 — Compute the moment of inertia of the free surface.**

$$i = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{12 \times 8^3}{12} = \dfrac{12 \times 512}{12} = 512 \;\text{m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Apply the FSE formula with $\rho_l = 1.025$ t/m³.**

$$\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot i}{\Delta} = \dfrac{1.025 \times 512}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{524.8}{10{,}000}$$

$$\overline{GG'} = 0.05248 \;\text{m} \approx 52.5 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

The ship loses **5.25 cm of GM** purely due to this single partially filled tank. If the original GM were, say, 0.30 m, the effective GM becomes 0.2475 m — still positive, but the margin is reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often include the **depth of liquid** (0.6 m here) in the FSE formula. **It is irrelevant.** Only the **free surface area dimensions** ($L$ and $B$) matter. Examiners frequently test this misconception.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Tank Geometry | $i$ Formula | Effect on Stability | Mitigation Strategy |
|---------------|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Wide rectangular (large $B$, small $L$) | $L \cdot B^3 / 12$ | **Severe** loss of GM | Add longitudinal subdivision bulkheads to reduce effective $B$ |
| Long rectangular (large $L$, small $B$) | $L \cdot B^3 / 12$ | **Negligible** loss | None required |
| Circular | $\pi D^4 / 64$ | Moderate for large $D$ | Use multiple smaller compartments |
| Triangular / Trapezoidal | Tabulated or integrated | Variable | Shape optimization during design |
| Filled to 100% (no free surface) | $i$ not applicable | **Zero** loss | Press up tanks completely |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Free Surface Effect Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship in Upright Condition] --> B{Heel Angle theta Applied}
    B --> C[Liquid in Tank Shifts Toward Low Side]
    C --> D[Liquid COG Shifts by gg prime]
    D --> E[Virtual Rise of Ship G by GG prime]
    E --> F[GM effective equals GM minus GG prime]
    F --> G{GM effective greater than 0?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Stable Ship with Reduced Margin]
    G -->|No| I[UNSTABLE Ship Capsize Risk]
    C --> J[Moment of Inertia i of Free Surface]
    J --> K[Wide and Shallow Tank equals High i]
    J --> L[Long and Narrow Tank equals Low i]
    K --> M[Add Longitudinal Bulkheads to Reduce i]
    L --> N[No Mitigation Needed]
    A --> O[Tank Filled to 100 percent]
    O --> P[No Free Surface FSE equals Zero]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stability Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        A1[Ship Displacement Delta]
        A2[Tank Dimensions L and B]
        A3[Liquid Density rho]
        A4[Number of Tanks n]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTATION
        B1[Compute i for each tank]
        B2[Sum rho times i over all tanks]
        B3[Divide by Delta to get GG prime]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        C1[Effective GM]
        C2[Stability Margin Check]
        C3[Pass or Fail Indicator]
    end
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (Adapted as Block Diagram)

Since the actual physical diagram requires a cross-section of a heeling ship with a free-surface tank, the following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional geometry**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIP_CROSS_SECTION
        S1[Centerline CL] 
        S2[Heel Axis Longitudinal]
        S3[Tank with Free Surface]
        S4[Liquid Wedge High Side]
        S5[Liquid Wedge Low Side]
        S6[Shift Vector gg prime]
    end
    S1 --- S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Virtual G prime Higher than G]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **Free Surface Effect**. State whether the depth of liquid in a partially filled tank affects the magnitude of FSE.

**Model Answer:**
Free Surface Effect is the phenomenon in which a partially filled liquid tank in a ship causes a **virtual rise in the ship's center of gravity** when the vessel heels, thereby reducing the effective metacentric height (GM). The liquid shifts toward the low side, producing a destabilizing moment. **No, the depth of liquid does not affect FSE**; only the **transverse moment of inertia of the free surface area** governs the magnitude of the effect.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A ship has a partially filled rectangular tank of length 10 m and breadth 6 m. Calculate the moment of inertia of the free surface. Given: ship displacement is 8000 tonnes and the tank contains seawater.

**Model Answer:**
Using $i = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$:

$$i = \dfrac{10 \times 6^3}{12} = \dfrac{10 \times 216}{12} = 180 \;\text{m}^4$$

Loss of GM: $\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot i}{\Delta} = \dfrac{1.025 \times 180}{8000} = 0.02306 \;\text{m} \approx 23 \;\text{mm}$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the virtual rise of the center of gravity ($\overline{GG'}$) of a ship due to a partially filled rectangular tank, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship of displacement 12,000 tonnes has three partially filled double-bottom tanks. Each tank is rectangular, of length 10 m and breadth 5 m, filled with seawater. Calculate:
  (i) The moment of inertia of the free surface for each tank. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The total loss of GM due to FSE. **(3 marks)**
  (iii) The effective GM, given that the original GM is 0.45 m. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** — Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Key points to be included:
1. Definition of free surface and its physical meaning.
2. Assumption of small heel angle: $\tan\theta \approx \theta$.
3. Geometry of the wedge shift and expression $gg' = \dfrac{i \tan\theta}{v}$.
4. Equating the moment of shifting liquid to the moment of an equivalent G shift: $\Delta \cdot \overline{GG'} = \rho_l \cdot i \cdot \tan\theta$.
5. Final expression: $\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot i}{\Delta}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

(i) Moment of inertia per tank:

$$i = \dfrac{10 \times 5^3}{12} = \dfrac{10 \times 125}{12} = 104.17 \;\text{m}^4$$

(ii) Total moment of inertia (3 identical tanks):

$$i_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 104.17 = 312.5 \;\text{m}^4$$

Total loss of GM:

$$\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{1.025 \times 312.5}{12{,}000} = \dfrac{320.31}{12{,}000} = 0.02669 \;\text{m} \approx 26.7 \;\text{mm}$$

(iii) Effective GM:

$$GM_{\text{eff}} = 0.45 - 0.02669 = 0.4233 \;\text{m} \approx 423 \;\text{mm}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how free surface liquid in a partially filled tank reduces the transverse stability of a ship. Discuss the role of the moment of inertia of the free surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular tank of length 8 m and breadth 4 m is partially filled with oil of relative density 0.85. The ship has a displacement of 6,000 tonnes. Calculate:
  (i) The virtual rise of G. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The percentage reduction in GM, given that the ship's original GM is 0.30 m. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) State **two practical methods** to reduce the free surface effect on board. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation** — Refer to Section 2.1 and Section 4 of these notes. Discuss:
- Liquid wedge shift and lateral COG movement.
- Concept of virtual rise in G.
- Reduction in GM as $GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - \overline{GG'}$.
- Role of $i$ (moment of inertia of free surface) as the controlling parameter.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

(i) Moment of inertia:

$$i = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 4^3}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 64}{12} = 42.67 \;\text{m}^4$$

Virtual rise of G (using $\rho_l = 0.85$ t/m³ for oil):

$$\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 42.67}{6000} = \dfrac{36.27}{6000} = 0.006045 \;\text{m} \approx 6.05 \;\text{mm}$$

(ii) Percentage reduction in GM:

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \dfrac{\overline{GG'}}{GM} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.006045}{0.30} \times 100 = 2.015\%$$

(iii) Two practical methods to reduce FSE:
1. **Press up (fill completely)** the tank so that no free surface exists.
2. **Install longitudinal subdivision bulkheads (swash bulkheads)** to break the free surface into smaller areas, reducing $i$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** include liquid depth in the FSE formula — it is a common error worth **−2 marks** if included.
> - **Do NOT** confuse FSE with the effect of a **suspended weight** or **free-surface of solid cargo** (granular flow) — they follow different physics.
> - **Always state the assumption of small heel angle** when using $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ — this is a **mandatory step** in derivations, worth **1 mark**.
> - **Units consistency:** $i$ in m⁴, $\Delta$ in tonnes, $\rho_l$ in t/m³ → $\overline{GG'}$ in m. Mixing units (e.g., using $\rho$ in kg/m³) leads to dimensional errors and **loss of 1–2 marks**.
> - **Sketch is compulsory** for any question asking "explain with a neat sketch" — omitting the sketch costs **2–3 marks** outright.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Free Surface Effect is the virtual rise of the ship's center of gravity due to the shifting of liquid in any partially filled tank.
- **Master Formula:** $\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot i}{\Delta}$ — applies for small heel angles.
- **Rectangular Tank:** $i = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ — remember: $B$ is cubed, so tank breadth dominates.
- **Independence:** $\overline{GG'}$ is **independent** of liquid depth and heel angle (for small angles).
- **Effective GM:** $GM_{\text{eff}} = KM - KG - \overline{GG'}$.
- **Multiple Tanks:** Sum the contributions: $\overline{GG'} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum \rho_l \cdot i$.
- **Critical Tank Geometries:**
  - Wide and shallow → **high** $i$ → **dangerous**.
  - Long and narrow → **low** $i$ → **safe**.
- **Mitigation Methods:**
  1. Press up tanks (fill 100%).
  2. Longitudinal bulkheads (reduce effective $B$).
  3. Use multiple smaller compartments.
  4. Avoid partial filling during critical stability conditions.
- **Regulatory Hooks:** SOLAS, IMO A.749(18), Classification Society Rules.
- **Typical Numerical Magnitude:** For a mid-sized cargo ship, FSC ranges from **0.05 m to 0.30 m** depending on tank filling pattern.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state **three things** explicitly in derivation: (1) small heel angle assumption, (2) horizontal free surface assumption, (3) the moment equivalence $\Delta \cdot \overline{GG'} = \rho_l \cdot i \cdot \tan\theta$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wall Sided Formula, Large Angle Stability - Statical Stability Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wall Sided Formula, Large Angle Stability - Statical Stability Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wall-Sided Ship — Formal KTU Definition

A **wall-sided ship** is a vessel whose cross-section between the design waterline and the load waterline (or between the waterlines in question) consists of **straight, vertical, parallel sides** — i.e., the half-breadth $y$ does not change with the ship's draft $z$ over the range of interest. In other words, the cross-section above and below the waterline behaves like a **vertical wall** with **zero flare, zero tumblehome, and no curvature**.

Mathematically, the condition for a wall-sided ship is:
$$\frac{dy}{dz} = 0$$

For such ships, the transverse metacentre $M$ lies on a circular arc of radius $BM$ (the metacentric radius) about $B$ as the ship heels. This geometric simplification gives rise to the **Wall-Sided Formula**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Wall-Sided Formula**
> The Wall-Sided Formula is the most fundamental KTU examination formula for calculating the righting lever $GZ$ at small angles of heel (typically valid up to $\phi \approx 10°$–$15°$) for vessels with vertical sides.

## 1.2 Large Angle Stability — The Need Beyond Wall-Sided

As the angle of heel $\phi$ **increases beyond 10°–15°**, several geometric realities invalidate the small-angle (wall-sided) assumption:

- The submerged wedge shape changes drastically (wedge out ≠ wedge in).
- The centre of buoyancy $B$ migrates significantly.
- The metacentre $M$ no longer traces a clean circular arc — it follows a complex curve called the **metacentric evolute**.
- The moment to change trim one centimetre (MCT) varies with angle.

Hence, **Large Angle Stability** analysis requires the construction of the **Statical Stability Curve** ($GZ$ vs. $\phi$ curve), often called the **Lever Arm Curve** or **Righting Lever Curve**.

## 1.3 Statical Stability Curve (GZ Curve) — Formal Definition

The **Statical Stability Curve** is a graphical plot of the **righting lever $GZ$** (in metres) on the $y$-axis against the **angle of heel $\phi$** (in degrees) on the $x$-axis. It depicts the **static righting moment capability** of a ship across all angles of heel and is the single most important stability assessment tool required by all major classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, IRS) and the IMO.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Statical"?**
> The word *statical* (not "static") comes from classical naval architecture. It distinguishes **heeling under steady conditions** (no acceleration, equilibrium of forces and moments) from **dynamical stability** (which considers kinetic energy and heeling motion).

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inverted Pendulum on a Curved Track"

Imagine a **heavy ball (G)** resting on a **sliding pivot (B)** that itself rolls along a **curved groove** (the metacentric evolute) inside a **box (the hull)**. The box is tilted to angle $\phi$.

- The ball tries to **return to the lowest position** — that's the **restoring force**.
- The **horizontal distance** between the ball's vertical line and the pivot is the **righting lever (GZ)**.
- For a **wall-sided ship**, the groove is a **perfect circle** (constant radius $BM$) → clean, simple formula.
- For a **real ship at large angles**, the groove is **irregular** → we must draw the curve point by point.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Students:**
> - **Wall-sided = Wall is straight = Simple formula**
> - **Large angle = Wall curves = Build the curve**
