# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Wall-Sided Formula — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a wall-sided ship heeled to an angle $\phi$ (see Section 4 for the full diagram). Let:

- $G$ = centre of gravity (fixed in body — ship-fixed coordinates)
- $B$ = centre of buoyancy (new position after heel)
- $M$ = transverse metacentre (intersection of vertical through new $B$ with ship centreline)
- $N$ = point where the vertical through heeled $B$ meets the original vertical (centreline)
- $KN$ = perpendicular distance from keel to $N$ along the new vertical

### 2.1.2 Derivation Logic (from First Principles)

The righting lever is, by definition, the **perpendicular distance from $G$ to the vertical through the new $B$**:

$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin\phi$$

This is the **fundamental KTU high-yield expression**. It is valid for **any ship shape**, but the difficulty lies in computing $KN$.

For a **wall-sided ship**, the metacentre $M$ traces a **circular arc of radius $BM$ about $B$**. Therefore, the perpendicular from $K$ to the new vertical through $B$ is:

$$KN = KM_{\phi} \cdot \sin\phi$$

And $KM_{\phi}$ for a wall-sided ship can be shown to equal:

$$KM_{\phi} = KM - \frac{1}{2} \cdot BM \cdot \tan\phi$$

where $KM = KB + BM$ is the initial transverse metacentric height.

### 2.1.3 Final Wall-Sided Formula (Two Forms)

**Form 1 — Lever form:**
$$GZ = \sin\phi \cdot \left( KM - \frac{1}{2} \cdot BM \cdot \tan\phi \right) - KG \cdot \sin\phi$$

Simplifying using $KM - KG = GM$:

$$\boxed{GZ = \sin\phi \cdot \left( GM - \frac{1}{2} \cdot BM \cdot \tan\phi \right)}$$

**Form 2 — Direct perpendicular form:**
$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin\phi$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $BM \cdot \tan\phi / 2$ term is the **"wall-sided correction"** and represents the **loss of stability** as the ship heels. At $\phi = 0$, $\tan 0 = 0$ and the formula reduces to the small-angle $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\phi \approx GM \cdot \phi$ (in radians).

## 2.2 Why the Formula Breaks Down at Large Angles

The wall-sided assumption fails for three cumulative reasons:

1. **Wedge Inequality:** At small angles, the emerged wedge volume $\approx$ immersed wedge volume, and both are triangles. At large angles, the emerged wedge is no longer a triangle, and the **centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts non-linearly**.
2. **Metacentric Evolute:** The locus of $M$ is not a circle for non-wall-sided ships, and even for wall-sided ships, the $BM$ value changes (since $I_T$ changes with heel).
3. **Submerged Volume Asymmetry:** The volume of the ship below water changes because parts of the deck or superstructure may immerse at large angles.

Hence, we must abandon closed-form solutions and build the **$GZ$ curve point by point**.

## 2.3 Statical Stability Curve — Construction Methods

There are **three classical methods** to construct the $GZ$ curve, all KTU-relevant:

| Method | Principle | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Cross-Curves of Stability (CCS)** | Plot $KN$ vs. displacement for various angles, then subtract $KG \cdot \sin\phi$ | Most accurate; used in design offices |
| **Wall-Sided Formula** | Apply $GZ = \sin\phi (GM - 0.5 \cdot BM \cdot \tan\phi)$ | Quick check; valid up to ~10°–15° |
| **Direct Integration (Simpson's Rule)** | Use Bonjean curves + offsets to compute $KN$ directly by integration of $y^3 \,dz$ | Used historically; rarely in KTU but conceptually important |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorise These**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $GZ = \sin\phi \left( GM - \dfrac{1}{2} BM \tan\phi \right)$ | Righting lever for wall-sided ship | $\phi \leq 15°$ approx. |
| 2 | $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$ | Universal righting lever definition | All $\phi$ |
| 3 | $GZ \approx GM \cdot \phi$ (radians) | Small-angle approximation | $\phi \leq 5°$ |
| 4 | $KN = \sin\phi \left( KM - \dfrac{1}{2} BM \tan\phi \right)$ | Wall-sided $KN$ | $\phi \leq 15°$ |
| 5 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Metacentric radius | Universal |
| 6 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | Universal |
| 7 | $KM = KB + BM$ | Total height | Universal |
| 8 | $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ (rectangle) | Transverse 2nd moment for rectangular waterplane | Special case |
| 9 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{BM \cdot \tan\phi}{2 \cdot GM}$ | Angle of maximum $GZ$ (wall-sided) | Wall-sided only |
| 10 | $GZ_{max} = \dfrac{GM \cdot \sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = GM \cdot \tan\theta$ | Maximum righting lever (wall-sided) | Wall-sided only |
| 11 | $GZ_{max} = 0.53 \cdot BM$ (approx.) | Empirical peak for wall-sided | Wall-sided only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Discipline:**
> - All lengths in **metres**.
> - All angles in **radians** for the small-angle formula, **degrees** for $\tan$ (since $\tan$ is dimensionless, it doesn't matter, but $\sin$ and $\cos$ are evaluated in whatever unit the calculator is set to).
> - Volumes in **m³**.
> - Displacement $\nabla$ and $W$ in **tonnes** (1 tonne = 1 m³ of seawater).

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Statical Stability Curves** are **mandatory deliverables** in every ship's **Trim & Stability Booklet** required by IMO SOLAS and the Classification Society rules.
- The **angle of maximum GZ** dictates the **worst-case roll angle** a ship should survive (e.g., container ships aim for max GZ at 25°–30°).
- The **angle of vanishing stability** (where $GZ$ returns to zero at large angles) is the **capsize angle** — a critical safety metric.
- The **range of stability** (from $\phi = 0$ to angle of vanishing stability) determines **roll safety** in storm conditions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Wall-Sided Formula

We will derive $GZ = \sin\phi (GM - 0.5 \, BM \tan\phi)$ from the most basic geometry, step by step.

### Step 1: Define the geometry after heeling

Let the ship be heeled to angle $\phi$. The new centre of buoyancy is $B_1$ (we drop the subscript and call it $B$). The metacentre $M$ is defined as the point on the ship centreline such that the **vertical through $B$ (after heel) passes through $M$**. The line $GZ$ is the horizontal distance from $G$ to this vertical.

### Step 2: Identify the right triangle

From the heeled geometry:
- $KN$ is the perpendicular from $K$ to the vertical through $B$.
- $GN$ is the perpendicular from $G$ to the vertical through $B$.
- Triangle $KGN$ is a right triangle at $N$ (in the body-axis projection).
- Therefore: $GZ = KN - GN$

### Step 3: Evaluate $GN$

In triangle $KGN$, the side $GN$ is the projection of $KG$ on the new vertical direction:
$$GN = KG \cdot \sin\phi$$

Thus:
$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin\phi \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Step 4: Find $KN$ for a wall-sided ship

For a wall-sided ship, the new metacentre $M$ is at the same height $KM$ above $K$ as the original $M$ (since $BM$ is constant, and $B$ is at the same distance $KB$ from $K$). So $M$ lies on a circle of radius $BM$ around $B$, and $KM = KB + BM = $ constant (where $KM$ is the metacentric height measured from keel).

The point $M$ is connected to $K$ by a fixed distance $KM$, but the new vertical through $B$ makes angle $\phi$ with the original centreline. Hence in the right triangle formed by $K$, the new $M$, and the foot of the perpendicular from $K$ to the new vertical:

$$KN = KM \cdot \sin\phi \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 5: Account for the metacentric shift

Wait — for a wall-sided ship, $M$ is NOT at the same height above $K$ after heeling! The metacentric radius $BM$ stays the same, but the **direction of $BM$** is no longer vertical. We need the perpendicular from $K$ to the new vertical, which is $KM_{\phi}$ (the slanted distance from $K$ to $M$).

The actual relationship (from the geometry of a wall-sided ship) is:
$$KM_{\phi} = KM - \frac{1}{2} \cdot BM \cdot \tan\phi$$

This can be derived by considering the change in the second moment of the waterplane area as the ship heels. For a wall-sided ship, the new waterplane is the same shape (parallel translation), but the heel reduces the effective transverse second moment. The classical result (proved in most naval architecture texts) is:

$$KN = \sin\phi \cdot \left( KM - \frac{1}{2} \cdot BM \cdot \tan\phi \right) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

### Step 6: Substitute (iii) into (i)

$$GZ = \sin\phi \left( KM - \frac{1}{2} \cdot BM \cdot \tan\phi \right) - KG \sin\phi$$

Factor out $\sin\phi$:
$$GZ = \sin\phi \left[ KM - \frac{1}{2} \cdot BM \tan\phi - KG \right]$$

Use $KM - KG = GM$:
$$GZ = \sin\phi \left( GM - \frac{1}{2} BM \tan\phi \right)$$

$$\boxed{\therefore GZ = \sin\phi \left( GM - \frac{1}{2} BM \tan\phi \right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Angle of Maximum GZ (Wall-Sided)

The angle $\theta$ at which $GZ$ is maximum is found by $\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi} = 0$.

Starting from:
$$GZ(\phi) = GM \sin\phi - \frac{1}{2} BM \sin\phi \tan\phi$$

Differentiate with respect to $\phi$ (using $\dfrac{d}{d\phi}(\sin\phi) = \cos\phi$ and $\dfrac{d}{d\phi}(\sin\phi \tan\phi) = \sin\phi \sec^2\phi + \cos\phi \tan\phi$):

$$\frac{dGZ}{d\phi} = GM \cos\phi - \frac{1}{2} BM \left[ \sin\phi \sec^2\phi + \cos\phi \tan\phi \right]$$

Setting $\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi} = 0$ and solving yields:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{BM}{2 \cdot GM}$$

This is a celebrated result: **the angle of maximum GZ depends only on the ratio $BM / GM$**.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Given:**
- Displacement $\nabla = 12{,}000$ tonnes
- $KG = 7.5$ m
- $KB = 3.0$ m
- $BM = 5.0$ m
- $GM = KM - KG = (3.0 + 5.0) - 7.5 = 0.5$ m

**Task:** Find $GZ$ at $\phi = 15°$ using the Wall-Sided Formula, and also find the angle of maximum GZ and its value.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Apply wall-sided formula.
$$GZ = \sin 15° \left( 0.5 - \frac{1}{2} \times 5.0 \times \tan 15° \right)$$

Step 2: Compute trigonometric values.
$$\sin 15° = 0.2588, \quad \tan 15° = 0.2679$$

Step 3: Compute the bracketed term.
$$0.5 - \frac{1}{2} \times 5.0 \times 0.2679 = 0.5 - 0.6698 = -0.1698 \text{ m}$$

Step 4: Final GZ.
$$GZ = 0.2588 \times (-0.1698) = -0.0439 \text{ m}$$

Since $GZ$ is **negative**, the ship has **no positive righting lever at 15°** → it is past its maximum GZ and will continue to capsize. This is a classic case of **insufficient GM**.

Step 5: Angle of maximum GZ.
$$\tan\theta = \frac{BM}{2 \cdot GM} = \frac{5.0}{2 \times 0.5} = 5.0$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(5.0) = 78.69° \quad \text{(physically unreasonable — confirms instability)}$$

Step 6: Engineering conclusion.
> With $GM = 0.5$ m, the ship is dangerously stable-deficient. The angle of maximum GZ is unrealistically large, indicating the wall-sided formula is giving non-physical results — the ship needs **ballast redistribution** to lower $G$ (i.e., increase $GM$).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Wall-Sided Heel Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ship at Even Keel phi equals 0] --> B[Ship Heels to Angle phi]
    B --> C[New Centre of Buoyancy B1]
    C --> D[New Metacentre M1 on Curved Locus]
    D --> E{Is Ship Wall Sided?}
    E -- YES --> F[Use Wall Sided Formula GZ equals sin phi times GM minus half BM tan phi]
    E -- NO --> G[Use Cross Curves of Stability Method]
    F --> H[Compute GZ for each phi value]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Plot GZ vs phi to get Statical Stability Curve]
    I --> J[Read off GZ max and Angle of Vanishing Stability]
    J --> K[Compare with IMO and Class Criteria]
```

```mermaid
    end

    B2 --> D1

    A2 --> C1

```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — GZ Curve Features

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Origin phi equals 0 GZ equals 0] --> B[Rising Limb Positive Stability]
    B --> C[Angle of Maximum GZ phi max]
    C --> D[Descending Limb Still Positive but Decreasing]
    D --> E[Angle of Vanishing Stability phi v where GZ equals 0]
    E --> F[Negative GZ Region Capsize]

    subgraph KEYFEATURES [KEY POINTS]
        P1[GZ MAX equals Maximum Righting Lever]
        P2[Range of Stability equals 0 to phi v]

    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Angle of Heel phi] --> B{phi less than 10 degrees}
    B -- YES --> C[Use Simple GZ equals GM sin phi]
    B -- NO --> D{Is hull wall sided}
    D -- YES --> E[Use Wall Sided Formula valid up to 15 degrees]
    D -- NO --> F[Use Cross Curves of Stability or Numerical Integration]
    E --> G{phi greater than 15 degrees}
    F --> G
    G -- YES --> H[Use CCS Method]
    G -- NO --> I[Use Wall Sided with caution]
    H --> J[Plot GZ Curve]
    I --> J
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a "wall-sided ship". State the condition under which the wall-sided formula is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **wall-sided ship** is one whose transverse cross-section, between the relevant waterlines, has **straight, vertical, parallel sides** — i.e., the half-breadth $y$ does not change with the draft $z$ (i.e., $dy/dz = 0$). The wall-sided formula $GZ = \sin\phi (GM - 0.5 \, BM \tan\phi)$ is **valid for angles of heel up to approximately 10°–15°** beyond which the geometric simplification breaks down and the formula gives inaccurate results. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a statical stability curve? Name any two important features that can be obtained from it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **statical stability curve** is a plot of the righting lever $GZ$ (m) on the $y$-axis versus the angle of heel $\phi$ (degrees) on the $x$-axis, showing the static righting capability of a ship at various heel angles.

**Two important features:**
1. **Maximum righting lever (GZ max)** — the peak of the curve; occurs at the angle of maximum stability.
2. **Range of stability** — the angle at which $GZ$ returns to zero (angle of vanishing stability), beyond which the ship capsizes. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Internal Choice:** Students answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the wall-sided formula for the righting lever $GZ$ in terms of $GM$, $BM$, and the angle of heel $\phi$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship has the following particulars in a given loading condition: $W = 8{,}000$ tonnes, $KG = 6.0$ m, $KB = 2.8$ m, $BM = 4.5$ m, $T = 5.2$ m. Find:
  (i) The angle of maximum $GZ$ using the wall-sided formula.
  (ii) The maximum righting lever $GZ_{max}$.
  (iii) The $GZ$ at $\phi = 20°$. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):

**Step 1:** Let ship heel to angle $\phi$. From geometry, $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** For a wall-sided ship, $M$ traces a circular arc of radius $BM$ about $B$. The new metacentre height $KM_{\phi}$ satisfies: $KM_{\phi} = KM - 0.5 \cdot BM \tan\phi$. (2 marks)

**Step 3:** The perpendicular from $K$ to the new vertical is: $KN = KM_{\phi} \cdot \sin\phi$. (1 mark)

**Step 4:** Substituting: $GZ = \sin\phi (KM - 0.5 \cdot BM \tan\phi) - KG \sin\phi = \sin\phi (GM - 0.5 \cdot BM \tan\phi)$. (3 marks)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):

**Given:** $W = 8000$ t, $KG = 6.0$ m, $KB = 2.8$ m, $BM = 4.5$ m.

**Step 1:** $KM = KB + BM = 2.8 + 4.5 = 7.3$ m. (0.5 mark)

**Step 2:** $GM = KM - KG = 7.3 - 6.0 = 1.3$ m. (0.5 mark)

**Step 3 (i) Angle of maximum GZ:**
$$\tan\theta = \frac{BM}{2 \cdot GM} = \frac{4.5}{2 \times 1.3} = \frac{4.5}{2.6} = 1.7308$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(1.7308) = 60°$$ (rounded). (1.5 marks)

**Step 4 (ii) Maximum righting lever:**
$$GZ_{max} = GM \cdot \tan\theta = 1.3 \times 1.7308 = 2.25 \text{ m}$$
(Alternatively: $GZ_{max} = 0.53 \cdot BM = 0.53 \times 4.5 = 2.385$ m, but the formula-derived value is 2.25 m.) (1.5 marks)

**Step 5 (iii) GZ at 20°:**
$$\sin 20° = 0.342, \quad \tan 20° = 0.364$$
$$GZ = 0.342 \times (1.3 - 0.5 \times 4.5 \times 0.364)$$
$$= 0.342 \times (1.3 - 0.819) = 0.342 \times 0.481 = 0.164 \text{ m}$$ (2 marks)

**Step 6: Comment:** At 20°, $GZ = 0.164$ m. The angle of maximum GZ ($\approx 60°$) is unreasonably large — this indicates that the **wall-sided assumption is breaking down** at 20°, and **Cross-Curves of Stability (CCS)** should be used for more accurate results. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the construction and use of the **Statical Stability Curve**. List the criteria for positive stability obtained from the curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wall-sided ship has $W = 5{,}000$ tonnes, $KG = 5.5$ m, $BM = 3.2$ m, $KB = 2.5$ m. Plot the $GZ$ values at $\phi = 0°, 5°, 10°, 15°, 20°, 30°$ using the wall-sided formula and find:
  (i) The angle of maximum $GZ$
  (ii) The maximum $GZ$ **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. For a given loading condition, calculate $GZ$ at several heel angles (typically $0°, 10°, 20°, 30°, 40°, 60°, 90°$).
2. Plot $GZ$ on $y$-axis vs. $\phi$ on $x$-axis.
3. The $GZ$ values are computed using either the **wall-sided formula** (small angles) or **Cross-Curves of Stability** (large angles, more accurate).

**Use (2 marks):**
- Determines **maximum righting lever** (peak of curve).
- Determines **range of stability** (angle of vanishing stability).
- Calculates **dynamical stability** = area under the curve.
- Verifies **IMO weather criterion** and **class requirements**.

**Criteria for positive stability (2 marks):**
1. The area under the $GZ$ curve up to the angle of flooding must exceed a minimum value (e.g., 0.055 m·rad for passenger ships).
2. $GZ_{max}$ must occur at an angle $\geq 25°$–$30°$ (varies by ship type).
3. The initial metacentric height $GM \geq 0.15$ m (for passenger ships per IMO).

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):

**Given:** $W = 5000$ t, $KG = 5.5$ m, $KB = 2.5$ m, $BM = 3.2$ m.

**Step 1:** $KM = 2.5 + 3.2 = 5.7$ m. $GM = 5.7 - 5.5 = 0.2$ m. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Compute $GZ$ at various angles:
| $\phi$ | $\sin\phi$ | $\tan\phi$ | $GZ = \sin\phi(0.2 - 0.5 \times 3.2 \times \tan\phi)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | 0.000 | 0.000 | $0.000$ m |
| 5° | 0.0872 | 0.0875 | $0.0872(0.2 - 0.140) = 0.0052$ m |
| 10° | 0.1736 | 0.1763 | $0.1736(0.2 - 0.282) = -0.0142$ m |
| 15° | 0.2588 | 0.2679 | $0.2588(0.2 - 0.429) = -0.0593$ m |

(2 marks for the table)

**Step 3:** Since $GM = 0.2$ m is very small, the bracket $(GM - 0.5 \cdot BM \tan\phi)$ becomes negative very quickly, making $GZ$ negative for all $\phi > 5°$. The ship has **very poor stability**. (1 mark)

**Step 4 (i) Angle of maximum GZ:**
$$\tan\theta = \frac{BM}{2 \cdot GM} = \frac{3.2}{0.4} = 8.0$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(8.0) = 82.87°$$
(Physically meaningless — wall-sided formula is invalid here.) (1.5 marks)

**Step 5 (ii) Maximum GZ:**
$$GZ_{max} = GM \cdot \tan\theta = 0.2 \times 8.0 = 1.6 \text{ m}$$
(But this is non-physical; the ship actually loses stability by ~6°.) (1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $0.5 \cdot BM \tan\phi$ correction term** — losing 2–3 marks in Part (a) derivations.
> 2. **Mixing units**: $\sin$ and $\tan$ use degrees, but the small-angle formula needs radians. Mixing yields numerical errors.
> 3. **Applying wall-sided formula beyond 20°**: This is a frequent trap. Examiners specifically test whether the student recognises when the formula is invalid.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in the statical stability curve question — at least 1–2 marks are reserved for the sketch in Part (a) of long-answer questions.
> 5. **Confusing $KN$ and $GZ$** — many students write $GZ = KN$ without subtracting $KG \sin\phi$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Wall Sided Formula & GZ Curve**

- ✅ **Wall-Sided Condition:** $dy/dz = 0$ (vertical, parallel sides).
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $GZ = \sin\phi (GM - 0.5 \cdot BM \tan\phi)$.
- ✅ **Universal Identity:** $GZ = KN - KG \sin\phi$ (always true, any ship, any angle).
- ✅ **Small-Angle Limit:** As $\phi \to 0$, $GZ \to GM \cdot \sin\phi \approx GM \cdot \phi$ (radians).
- ✅ **Angle of Maximum GZ:** $\tan\theta = BM / (2 \cdot GM)$ — **wall-sided only**.
- ✅ **Maximum GZ Value:** $GZ_{max} = GM \cdot \tan\theta = 0.53 \cdot BM$ (empirical).
- ✅ **Validity Range:** Wall-sided formula accurate up to **~15°** of heel.
- ✅ **Beyond 15°:** Use **Cross-Curves of Stability (CCS)** for accuracy.
- ✅ **Statical Stability Curve:** Plot of $GZ$ (m) vs. $\phi$ (°).
- ✅ **Key Curve Features:** $GZ_{max}$, angle of vanishing stability, range of stability, area under curve (= dynamical stability).
- ✅ **IMO Positive Stability Criteria:** (1) Area to 30° $\geq 0.055$ m·rad, (2) Area 30°–40° $\geq 0.030$ m·rad, (3) Area to 40° $\geq 0.090$ m·rad, (4) $GZ_{max} \geq 0.20$ m, (5) $\theta_{max} \geq 25°$.
- ✅ **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** $GM \geq 0.15$ m (passenger ships), $GM \geq 0.05$ m (cargo ships).
- ✅ **Unit Discipline:** Lengths in metres, $W$ in tonnes, angles consistent.
- ✅ **Why the formula breaks at large angles:** Non-linear wedge shape, non-circular metacentric evolute, waterplane second moment varies with heel.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Positive $GZ$ = righting; negative $GZ$ = capsizing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of GZ Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of GZ Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **GZ Curve**, also known as the **Curve of Statical Stability** or the **Righting Lever Curve**, is a graphical representation of the ship's righting lever (GZ) plotted as a function of the angle of heel (φ). It is the most fundamental and widely used tool in naval architecture for assessing a vessel's transverse stability at all angles of inclination, from the upright position to the angle of vanishing stability.

**Righting Lever (GZ):** The horizontal distance between the ship's centre of gravity (G) and the vertical line of action of buoyancy (through B or B') passing through the centre of buoyancy, measured at any given angle of heel φ. The corresponding moment is called the **Righting Moment (RM)** and is given by RM = W × GZ, where **W = 12,000 tonnes** (typical displacement, varies per vessel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** A clear understanding of the GZ curve and its characteristic points is mandatory for the End Semester Examination. Students are expected to identify the following features: initial slope, angle of maximum GZ, maximum GZ value, angle of vanishing stability, range of positive stability, and the area representing dynamic stability.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are holding a tall bottle upright on a table. The bottle is stable. If you tilt it slightly, it tries to right itself because the weight of the liquid (acting downward at the centre of gravity G) and the upward push from the table (acting through the centre of buoyancy B) create a *couple* that tries to restore it to upright. The horizontal distance between these two vertical forces is the "righting lever" — exactly the **GZ** of a ship.

Now tilt the bottle further. At first the restoring effect grows, reaches a maximum, then begins to decrease. If you push it past a critical angle (the deck-edge immersion point), the bottle's geometry changes — the "buoyancy point" shifts drastically — and the bottle capsizes. This progressive behaviour of the restoring force with tilt angle is what the GZ curve captures for a ship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Statical vs Dynamical Stability:**
> - **Statical Stability (GZ):** The instantaneous righting lever at a given angle — a *force/moment* measure.
> - **Dynamical Stability (Area under GZ curve):** The total work done by the righting moment in returning the ship from an angle of heel to the upright — an *energy* measure.

### 1.3 Key Nomenclature

- **G** — Centre of gravity of the ship (fixed, does not shift with small angles of heel).
- **B** — Centre of buoyancy in the upright condition.
- **B'** — Centre of buoyancy when inclined at angle φ.
- **K** — Keel point (intersection of the vertical centreline with the baseline).
- **KB** — Vertical height of B above K.
- **KG** — Vertical height of G above K.
- **KN** — Horizontal distance from K to the intersection point N of the vertical through B' with the upright centreline.
- **GZ** — Righting lever (the horizontal arm).
- **φ (phi)** — Angle of heel in degrees or radians.
- **W (Δ)** — Weight/displacement of the ship.
- **GM** — Metacentric height (transverse).
- **BM_T** — Transverse metacentric radius at the inclined waterline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

When a ship heels by a small angle φ, the shape of the underwater volume changes. The centre of buoyancy shifts from B to B', tracing a curve known as the **metacentric evolute**. The position of the metacentre M is found by drawing the normal to the evolute at B' and extending it to intersect the upright centreline.

The geometry of the GZ curve is built on three foundational steps:

**Step 1 — Identify the Lever Arm Components:**
At any angle of heel φ, the righting lever GZ is the difference between two horizontal distances measured from the ship's centreline:
- **KN:** distance from K to the vertical through B' (found using Bonjean or direct integration).
- **KG × sin(φ):** the horizontal shift of G from the centreline when the ship tilts.

**Step 2 — Establish the Master Equation:**

$$
GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

This is the **universal form** of the GZ equation. The term KN accounts for the shift in the centre of buoyancy, and KG·sin(φ) accounts for the geometric shift of G from the centreline.

**Step 3 — Apply Simplifications for Specific Regimes:**
- For small angles (φ < 10°–15°), the metacentric theory applies: GZ ≈ GM·sin(φ) ≈ GM·φ (in radians).
- For larger angles, the **Wall-Sided Formula** is used when the ship has parallel side walls (typical of box-shaped hulls).
- For general cases, KN is obtained from **Cross Curves of Stability** (curves of KN vs. displacement for fixed angles).

### 2.2 Physical Interpretation — Why GZ Rises and Falls

The GZ curve is not monotonic. It has a characteristic shape because:

1. As φ increases from 0°, the righting arm GZ grows because the shift in B (and hence KN) increases faster than the KG·sin(φ) component.
2. A **maximum GZ** is reached at the **angle of maximum righting moment** (typically 25°–40° for well-designed ships), where the rate of shift of B' begins to slow down.
3. Beyond this maximum, the deck edge begins to immerse, dramatically altering the waterplane area and the position of B'. GZ starts to fall.
4. GZ reaches zero at the **angle of vanishing stability (φ_v)**. Beyond φ_v, the ship is **dynamically unstable** and will capsize unless external forces act to bring her back.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Master GZ Equation | $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ | GZ in m, KG in m, φ in rad/deg | Universal — valid for all angles |
| Small Angle GZ | $GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin(\phi)$ | GM in m, φ in rad | Valid for φ < 10°–15° |
| Small Angle (Radian Approx.) | $GZ \approx GM \cdot \phi$ | φ in radians only | Used in theoretical derivations |
| Wall-Sided Formula | $GZ = \sin(\phi) \cdot \left[GM + \dfrac{BM_T}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi)\right]$ | All in m, φ in rad | When ship sides are vertical & parallel |
| Righting Moment | $RM = W \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot GZ$ | W in tonnes, GZ in m → RM in tonne·m | RM = W·GZ |
| Righting Couple | $RM = W \cdot GZ$ | — | Same as righting moment |
| Dynamical Stability (Work) | $DN = W \cdot \int_{0}^{\phi} GZ \, d\phi$ | W in tonnes, GZ in m, φ in rad | Area under GZ curve × W |
| Dynamical Stability (Unit Displ.) | $dn = \int_{0}^{\phi} GZ \, d\phi$ | GZ in m, φ in rad | Energy per unit displacement |
| Stability Criterion (Statical) | $GZ > 0$ | — | Positive for stable equilibrium |
| Stability Criterion (Initial) | $GM > 0$ | GM in m | Required for initial stability |
| Vanishing Stability | $GZ = 0 \implies \phi = \phi_v$ | φ_v in degrees | Angle of deck edge immersion |
| Maximum GZ Condition | $\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi} = 0$ | — | Defines angle of maximum GZ |
| Area under GZ (Simpson's Rule) | $A \approx \dfrac{h}{3}\left[y_0 + 4(y_1+y_3+...) + 2(y_2+y_4+...) + y_n\right]$ | h = common angle interval | Numerical integration of GZ curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Conversion Reminder:** When computing area under the GZ curve for dynamical stability, φ **must be in radians** (since we are integrating with respect to an angle). If the GZ is given in metres and the abscissa is in degrees, multiply the area (in m·deg) by **π/180** to convert to m·rad.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The GZ curve is not merely an academic exercise — it is a **statutory requirement** under the **IMO Code on Intact Stability (IS Code, 2008)** and the **SOLAS Convention**. Naval architects use the GZ curve to:
- Verify compliance with the **Weather Criterion** (IMO 2008).
- Assess the **Severe Wind and Rolling Criterion (SWRC)**.
- Ensure adequate **range of stability** (φ_v − φ_max ≥ 60° for passenger ships per IMO).
- Calculate **downflooding angles** and required **freeboard**.
- Design **damage stability** survivability curves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master GZ Equation

We start from the geometric configuration of a ship heeled at angle φ, with G, B', K, N, and M as defined above.

**Starting Configuration:**
- Ship is heeled through angle φ.
- The new centre of buoyancy is B' (on the inclined centreline).
- The vertical through B' meets the original vertical centreline at point N.
- The metacentre M is at a distance BM above B' (in the inclined position).
- The original centre of gravity is G, lying on the vertical through B' offset by KG·sin(φ) from the centreline.

**Geometric Construction of GZ:**

From the geometry of the inclined ship, the horizontal distance GZ (the righting lever) is the perpendicular distance between the line of action of the ship's weight (vertical through G) and the line of action of buoyancy (vertical through B').

By projecting KN horizontally and subtracting the horizontal displacement of G from the centreline:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= KN - \text{(horizontal shift of G from centreline)} \\
GZ &= KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Master GZ Equation** — universally valid for all angles of heel where the ship's underwater form can be represented by KN.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation is the **single most important formula** in Module 1 of Ship Stability. It is the foundation on which the GZ curve, dynamical stability, and stability criteria are built. Examiners will always start a problem from this equation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Small-Angle GZ Formula

For very small angles of heel, we apply **metacentric theory**, valid when M can be considered a fixed point (i.e., the shift of B is along a circular arc centred at M).

**Step 1:** From the geometry, when M is fixed:
$$
KN \approx KM \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting into the master equation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi) \\
GZ &= KM \cdot \sin(\phi) - KG \cdot \sin(\phi) \\
GZ &= (KM - KG) \cdot \sin(\phi) \\
GZ &= GM \cdot \sin(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For very small angles (in radians), $\sin(\phi) \approx \phi$:
$$
GZ \approx GM \cdot \phi
$$

This is the famous **"Initial Stability Formula"** that every naval architecture student must memorize.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Wall-Sided Formula

The **Wall-Sided Formula** is the KTU-favourite derivation. It applies when the ship has vertical parallel side walls between the upright and inclined waterlines. Assume a ship is heeled by angle φ and the centre of buoyancy has shifted to B'. The waterplane area in the inclined position has a particular geometry.

**Assumptions:**
1. The ship's sides are vertical (wall-sided) between the original and inclined waterlines.
2. The shift of the centre of buoyancy is in two components: an **inward shift** $\delta_{in}$ and an **upward shift** $\delta_{up}$.
3. The waterplane area is $A_w$ and the second moment of area is $I_T$.

**Step 1 — Standard small-angle KN expression:**
At any angle φ, using the integration of the underwater volume:
$$
KN = KM \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Expressing KM at an angle:**
The transverse BM at an angle φ is:
$$
BM_T(\phi) = \dfrac{I_T(\phi)}{V(\phi)}
$$
where $I_T(\phi)$ is the transverse second moment of the inclined waterplane and $V(\phi)$ is the displaced volume.

**Step 3 — Taylor Series Expansion of BM_T:**
For a wall-sided ship, $I_T$ increases proportionally to $1/\cos(\phi)$ (waterplane widens) and $V$ is approximately constant. The result of this analysis yields:
$$
BM_T(\phi) \approx BM \cdot \sec(\phi)
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting and simplifying:**
After substitution, the wall-sided formula emerges as:
$$
GZ = \sin(\phi) \cdot \left[GM + \dfrac{BM_T}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi)\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Expanding for very small angles:**
For $\phi$ in radians and small, $\sin(\phi) \approx \tan(\phi) \approx \phi$, giving:
$$
GZ \approx GM \cdot \phi + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot BM_T \cdot \phi^2
$$
which is the small-angle expansion showing the GZ curve's quadratic departure from linearity at larger angles.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of GZ Curve Features

| Feature of GZ Curve | Mathematical Condition | Physical Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Origin (0, 0) | $\phi = 0, GZ = 0$ | Ship upright; no righting moment | Reference point of the curve |
| Initial Slope | $\left.\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi}\right\_{\phi=0} = GM$ | Slope equals metacentric height | Determines initial stability |
| GZ increasing | $GZ > 0$ and $\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi} > 0$ | Ship is statically stable; righting moment grows | Region of increasing righting |
| Maximum GZ | $\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi} = 0$ at $\phi = \phi\_{max}$ | Peak of the righting lever | Critical stability point |
| GZ decreasing | $GZ > 0$ and $\dfrac{dGZ}{d\phi} < 0$ | Stability reduces; deck edge immersion begins | Region of decreasing righting |
| Vanishing Stability | $GZ = 0$ at $\phi = \phi_v$ | Righting moment becomes zero | Ship on verge of capsizing |
| Range of Stability | $\phi_v$ | Maximum angle before capsize | IMO requires $\geq 60°$ for some ships |
| Dynamical Stability | $\int_0^{\phi} GZ \, d\phi$ | Work done by the righting moment | Energy to resist capsize by wind/waves |
| Residual GZ | GZ value at a specific large angle (e.g., 40°) | Safety margin against knockdown | Used in damage stability |
| Downflooding Angle | $\phi$ at which any opening immerses | Critical operational limit | Must be < $\phi_v$ |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Verifying the GZ Curve

**Problem:** A ship has $W = 20{,}000$ tonnes, $KG = 6.5$ m, $GM = 0.85$ m. The KN values at various angles are:

| $\phi$ (°) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| KN (m) | 0 | 1.85 | 3.60 | 5.10 | 6.20 | 6.85 | 7.00 | 6.70 | 5.80 |

Calculate GZ at each angle and identify the maximum GZ and angle of vanishing stability.

**Step 1:** Compute $KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ for each angle.

| $\phi$ (°) | $\sin(\phi)$ | $KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.000 |
| 10 | 0.1736 | 1.128 |
| 20 | 0.3420 | 2.223 |
| 30 | 0.5000 | 3.250 |
| 40 | 0.6428 | 4.178 |
| 50 | 0.7660 | 4.979 |
| 60 | 0.8660 | 5.629 |
| 70 | 0.9397 | 6.108 |
| 80 | 0.9848 | 6.401 |

**Step 2:** Compute $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$.

| $\phi$ (°) | KN (m) | $KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ (m) | GZ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.000 | **0.000** |
| 10 | 1.85 | 1.128 | **0.722** |
| 20 | 3.60 | 2.223 | **1.377** |
| 30 | 5.10 | 3.250 | **1.850** |
| 40 | 6.20 | 4.178 | **2.022** |
| 50 | 6.85 | 4.979 | **1.871** |
| 60 | 7.00 | 5.629 | **1.371** |
| 70 | 6.70 | 6.108 | **0.592** |
| 80 | 5.80 | 6.401 | **−0.601** |

**Step 3:** Interpret the results.

- **Maximum GZ = 2.022 m** at $\phi \approx 40°$ → **angle of maximum righting moment.**
- **Vanishing stability** occurs between 70° and 80°. By linear interpolation:

$$
\phi_v = 70° + \dfrac{0.592}{0.592 - (-0.601)} \times 10° = 70° + 4.96° \approx 74.96°
$$

- **Range of stability ≈ 75°**, which exceeds the IMO minimum of 60° — the ship passes the basic stability criterion.
- **Dynamical Stability (area under GZ curve)** can be computed by Simpson's Rule or trapezoidal rule on the GZ values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Anatomy of the GZ Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Upright - phi = 0 deg] --> B[Begin Heeling due to External Moment]
    B --> C[Centre of Buoyancy B shifts to B-prime]
    C --> D[Vertical through B-prime meets Centreline at N]
    D --> E[Compute KN from Cross Curves or Integration]
    E --> F[Compute Horizontal Shift = KG x sin phi]
    F --> G[Master Equation: GZ = KN - KG sin phi]
    G --> H{Is GZ greater than 0?}
    H -- Yes, increasing --> I[Stable Region - Righting Moment Growing]
    I --> J[Reach Maximum GZ at phi max]
    J --> K[Deck Edge Immersion Begins]
    K --> L[GZ begins to decrease]
    L --> M{GZ = 0?}
    M -- No --> N[Ship remains stable but with reduced righting]
    M -- Yes --> P[Angle of Vanishing Stability phi v]
    P --> Q[Beyond phi v - Ship Capsizes]
    H -- No --> R[Unstable - Ship Heels Further]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of GZ Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data]
        I1[Ship Displacement W]
        I2[Vertical Centre of Gravity KG]
        I3[KN Table from Bonjean / Cross Curves]
        I4[Angle of Heel phi]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Engine]
        C1[Lookup KN at phi]
        C2[Compute sin phi]
        C3[Compute KG sin phi]
        C4[Apply Master GZ Equation]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Parameters]
        O1[Righting Lever GZ]
        O2[Righting Moment RM = W x GZ]
        O3[Plot Point on GZ Curve]
    end

    I3 --> C1
    I4 --> C2
    I2 --> C3
    I4 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> O1
    I1 --> O2
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Characteristic Points on the GZ Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GZ Curve Plot with GZ vs phi] --> B[Origin: 0,0 - Ship Upright]
    A --> C[Initial Slope equals GM]
    A --> D[Maximum GZ Point - Peak of Curve]
    A --> E[Angle of Vanishing Stability - phi v]
    A --> F[Range of Stability equals phi v]
    A --> G[Area under GZ equals Dynamical Stability]

    B --> B1[No Righting Moment at phi=0]
    C --> C1[Determines Initial Stability - large GM means steeper slope]
    D --> D1[Maximum Righting Moment occurs here - ideal between 30 to 40 deg]
    E --> E1[Ship on verge of capsizing - end of positive stability]
    F --> F1[IMO requires phi v to be greater than or equal to 60 deg]
    G --> G1[Energy stored in ship to resist external heeling moments]
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — GZ Curve Shape and Key Points

```
       GZ (m)
         |
   2.5 - +                  ____
         |                /        \
   2.0 - +              /            \
         |             /                \
   1.5 - +            /                  \
         |           /                    \
   1.0 - +          /                      \
         |         /                        \
   0.5 - +        /                          \
         |       /                            \
   0.0 - +------+------------------------------+------>  phi (deg)
         | 0   10   20   30  40  50  60  70   80
         |
         |        [phi_max]   [phi_v]
         |          ≈40°       ≈75°
         |
   _____|______________________________________________
         |              (Negative Region)
   -0.5 -+
         |
   -1.0 -+
         |
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The curve rises from origin (0, 0), increases with decreasing slope, reaches a **peak** at $\phi_{max} \approx 40°$ where GZ is maximum, then descends and crosses the horizontal axis at $\phi_v \approx 75°$. Beyond this angle, GZ is negative — the ship will capsize if held at that angle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the term **Righting Lever (GZ)**. Explain its significance in determining the stability of a ship at various angles of heel.

**Model Answer:**

The **Righting Lever (GZ)** is the horizontal distance between the vertical line of action of the ship's weight (passing through the centre of gravity G) and the vertical line of action of the buoyancy force (passing through the centre of buoyancy B') when the ship is inclined at an angle φ. The corresponding **righting moment** is given by RM = W × GZ.

**Significance:**
1. It is a direct measure of the ship's ability to right itself from a given angle of heel.
2. When GZ > 0, the ship experiences a restoring (righting) moment; when GZ = 0, the ship is at the angle of vanishing stability; when GZ < 0, the ship capsizes.
3. The plot of GZ vs φ gives the **GZ curve**, from which maximum GZ, range of stability, and dynamical stability (area under curve) can be determined.
4. It is used to verify compliance with IMO stability criteria.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

State the **master equation** for the righting lever GZ. What is the significance of the term **KN** in this equation?

**Model Answer:**

The master equation for the righting lever is:

$$
GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

**Significance of KN:**
- **KN** is the horizontal distance from the keel point K to the intersection N of the vertical line through the inclined centre of buoyancy B' with the original (upright) centreline.
- It represents the geometric shift of the centre of buoyancy due to the change in the underwater volume when the ship heels.
- KN is obtained either by direct integration of the underwater volume or from the **Cross Curves of Stability** (curves of KN vs. displacement for fixed angles).
- It is the only term in the GZ equation that depends on the hull form; the other term, KG·sin(φ), depends only on the position of G and the angle of heel.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **characteristics of the GZ curve**. List the important points and their physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a ship of displacement **W = 15,000 tonnes**, KG = **7.2 m**, and GM = **0.95 m**, the KN values at various angles of heel are given below. Calculate the GZ at each angle, determine the **angle of maximum GZ** and the **angle of vanishing stability**, and compute the **dynamical stability** up to 40° of heel using Simpson's Rule. **(7 Marks)**

| $\phi$ (°) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| KN (m) | 0 | 1.65 | 3.30 | 4.75 | 5.85 | 6.40 | 6.50 | 6.10 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Characteristics of the GZ Curve:**

[Refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.4 and the schematic in Section 4.4 for the explanation.]

The GZ curve is a plot of righting lever GZ (in metres) versus angle of heel φ (in degrees). Its key characteristics are:

1. **Origin (0, 0):** At φ = 0, GZ = 0. The ship is upright and there is no righting moment.
2. **Initial Slope = GM:** For small angles, the slope of the GZ curve equals the metacentric height GM. A larger GM means a steeper initial slope and better initial stability.
3. **Maximum GZ Point:** The peak of the curve, where dGZ/dφ = 0. At this angle, the righting moment is maximum (RM_max = W × GZ_max). For well-designed ships, this angle lies between 30° and 40°.
4. **Angle of Vanishing Stability ($\phi_v$):** The angle at which the curve crosses the horizontal axis (GZ = 0). Beyond this angle, the ship is dynamically unstable.
5. **Range of Stability:** Equals $\phi_v$ — the range of angles through which the ship can be heeled and still return to upright.
6. **Area under the Curve:** Represents the **dynamical stability** — the work done by the righting moment in returning the ship to upright.
7. **Negative Region:** For φ > $\phi_v$, GZ is negative — the ship experiences a heeling moment and will capsize.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ using $KG = 7.2$ m.

| $\phi$ (°) | $\sin(\phi)$ | $KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.000 |
| 10 | 0.1736 | 1.250 |
| 20 | 0.3420 | 2.462 |
| 30 | 0.5000 | 3.600 |
| 40 | 0.6428 | 4.628 |
| 50 | 0.7660 | 5.515 |
| 60 | 0.8660 | 6.235 |
| 70 | 0.9397 | 6.766 |

**Step 2:** Compute $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$.

| $\phi$ (°) | KN (m) | $KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ (m) | GZ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.000 | **0.000** |
| 10 | 1.65 | 1.250 | **0.400** |
| 20 | 3.30 | 2.462 | **0.838** |
| 30 | 4.75 | 3.600 | **1.150** |
| 40 | 5.85 | 4.628 | **1.222** |
| 50 | 6.40 | 5.515 | **0.885** |
| 60 | 6.50 | 6.235 | **0.265** |
| 70 | 6.10 | 6.766 | **−0.666** |

**Step 3:** Identify Key Points.

- **Maximum GZ = 1.222 m** at $\phi \approx 40°$.
- **Vanishing stability** is between 60° and 70°. By linear interpolation:
$$
\phi_v = 60° + \dfrac{0.265}{0.265 - (-0.666)} \times 10° = 60° + 2.85° \approx 62.85°
$$
- **Range of Stability ≈ 62.85°** (passes IMO 60° requirement).

**Step 4:** Compute Dynamical Stability up to 40° using Simpson's Rule (convert degrees to radians).

- Convert each angle: $\phi$ in rad = $\phi \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$.

| $\phi$ (°) | $\phi$ (rad) | GZ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.000 |
| 10 | 0.1745 | 0.400 |
| 20 | 0.3491 | 0.838 |
| 30 | 0.5236 | 1.150 |
| 40 | 0.6981 | 1.222 |

- Common interval $h = 0.1745$ rad, $n = 4$ (even number of intervals — Simpson's 1/3 rule applies).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{40°} GZ \, d\phi &\approx \dfrac{h}{3}\left[y_0 + y_4 + 4(y_1 + y_3) + 2(y_2)\right] \\
&\approx \dfrac{0.1745}{3}\left[0.000 + 1.222 + 4(0.400 + 1.150) + 2(0.838)\right] \\
&\approx \dfrac{0.1745}{3}\left[1.222 + 6.200 + 1.676\right] \\
&\approx \dfrac{0.1745}{3} \times 9.098 \\
&\approx 0.5291 \text{ m·rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to dynamical stability in tonne·m units.

$$
DN = W \times \int_0^{40°} GZ \, d\phi = 15{,}000 \times 0.5291 \approx 7936.5 \text{ tonne·m}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Maximum GZ = **1.222 m** at φ = 40°.
- Angle of vanishing stability ≈ **62.85°**.
- Dynamical stability up to 40° ≈ **0.529 m·rad** (or **7936.5 tonne·m**).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **wall-sided formula** for the righting lever GZ. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship has GM = **0.8 m**, BM_T = **4.5 m** at 30° heel. Using the wall-sided formula, calculate the GZ at **20° and 40°** of heel. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Wall-Sided Formula:**

[Refer to Section 3.3 for the full derivation.]

**Assumptions:**
1. The ship has **vertical parallel side walls** (wall-sided) between the upright and inclined waterlines.
2. The waterplane area $A_w$ remains approximately constant with small changes in angle of heel.
3. The transverse BM at angle φ is related to the upright BM by $BM_T(\phi) = BM \cdot \sec(\phi)$.
4. The displaced volume V is approximately constant.
5. The metacentre M is at a finite, calculable height above K.

**Derivation Summary:**

For a wall-sided ship, the centre of buoyancy shifts as the ship heels. The horizontal lever KN can be expressed as:

$$
KN = KM \cdot \sin(\phi) = (KB + BM_T(\phi)) \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

Substituting $BM_T(\phi) = BM \cdot \sec(\phi)$:

$$
KN = \sin(\phi) \cdot KB + \sin(\phi) \cdot BM \cdot \sec(\phi)
$$

Using the master equation $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ and $KM = KG + GM$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GZ &= \sin(\phi) \cdot (KG + GM) - KG \cdot \sin(\phi) + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot BM \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \tan(\phi) \\
GZ &= \sin(\phi) \cdot GM + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot BM_T \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \tan(\phi) \\
GZ &= \sin(\phi) \cdot \left[GM + \dfrac{BM_T}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **wall-sided formula** for GZ.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $GM = 0.8$ m, $BM_T = 4.5$ m (at 30° heel, taken as the value to use in the formula).

**At $\phi = 20°$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(20°) &= 0.3420, \quad \tan(20°) = 0.3640 \\
GZ &= \sin(20°) \cdot \left[GM + \dfrac{BM_T}{2} \cdot \tan(20°)\right] \\
GZ &= 0.3420 \cdot \left[0.8 + \dfrac{4.5}{2} \cdot 0.3640\right] \\
GZ &= 0.3420 \cdot \left[0.8 + 0.819\right] \\
GZ &= 0.3420 \cdot 1.619 \\
GZ &\approx 0.554 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At $\phi = 40°$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(40°) &= 0.6428, \quad \tan(40°) = 0.8391 \\
GZ &= \sin(40°) \cdot \left[GM + \dfrac{BM_T}{2} \cdot \tan(40°)\right] \\
GZ &= 0.6428 \cdot \left[0.8 + \dfrac{4.5}{2} \cdot 0.8391\right] \\
GZ &= 0.6428 \cdot \left[0.8 + 1.888\right] \\
GZ &= 0.6428 \cdot 2.688 \\
GZ &\approx 1.728 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Results:**

1. GZ at 40° (1.728 m) is significantly greater than GZ at 20° (0.554 m), confirming that the righting lever increases with angle of heel (in this range).
2. The contribution of the second term $\left(\dfrac{BM_T}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi)\right)$ becomes more significant at larger angles. At 20°, it contributes ~50% of the bracketed term; at 40°, it contributes ~70%. This shows the **non-linear** (quadratic-like) nature of GZ at larger angles — purely metacentric theory would underestimate it.
3. The wall-sided formula is a **better approximation than the simple GM·sin(φ)** formula for angles beyond ~15°.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in GZ Curve Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion for Dynamical Stability:** When using Simpson's Rule, the angle must be in **radians**, not degrees. Forgetting to convert (multiply each angle by π/180) before applying the formula is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in KTU answer sheets.
> 2. **Confusing BM with BM_T:** BM is the **metacentric radius** (transverse BM) and is always positive. GM = KM − KG. Do not confuse BM with GZ.
> 3. **Sign Convention for GZ:** Negative GZ means the ship is in the **capsizing region** — do not take its absolute value. The area under only the **positive** portion of the GZ curve is meaningful for dynamical stability.
> 4. **Mistaking φ_max for φ_v:** $\phi_{max}$ is the angle of **maximum GZ** (peak of the curve). $\phi_v$ is the angle of **vanishing stability** (where the curve crosses zero). These are two different angles and must not be interchanged.
> 5. **Forgetting to Subtract KG·sin(φ):** When the GZ table is computed from KN, students often forget the second term and use GZ = KN, which is **wrong** unless KG is at the keel.
> 6. **Drawing the GZ Curve as a Straight Line:** A GZ curve is **not linear**. The small-angle approximation $GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\phi)$ is valid only for φ < 10°–15°. Sketching it as a straight line through the origin will cost full marks in "with neat sketch" questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Characteristics of GZ Curve**
> - **Master Equation:** $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ — **memorize this verbatim**.
> - **Small-Angle Formula:** $GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\phi) \approx GM \cdot \phi$ (radians).
> - **Wall-Sided Formula:** $GZ = \sin(\phi) \cdot \left[GM + \dfrac{BM_T}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi)\right]$.
> - **Initial slope of GZ curve = GM.**
> - **Maximum GZ** occurs at $\phi_{max}$ (typically 30°–40°) — peak of curve.
> - **Angle of Vanishing Stability $\phi_v$** is where GZ = 0.
> - **Range of Stability = $\phi_v$** — IMO requires ≥ 60° for many ship types.
> - **Dynamical Stability = $W \cdot \int GZ \, d\phi$** (use **radians** for angle).
> - **Stability Criterion:** GZ > 0 ⇒ stable; GZ = 0 ⇒ neutral; GZ < 0 ⇒ unstable.
> - **Residual GZ:** value at a specific angle (e.g., 40° or 50°) used as a safety check.
> - **KN values** are obtained from **Cross Curves of Stability** (KN vs. displacement for fixed angles).
> - The GZ curve **rises, peaks, falls, and crosses zero** — it is a single-peaked curve for an intact ship.
> - **Units:** W in tonnes, GZ in m, RM in tonne·m, $\phi$ in rad for integration.
> - For numerical integration: **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** needs an even number of intervals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Changes in Ship's Breadth, Freeboard and Superstructure on GZ Curve, Cross Curves of Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Changes in Ship's Breadth, Freeboard and Superstructure on GZ Curve, Cross Curves of Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

The **Righting Lever (GZ curve)** is the graphical representation of the **Righting Lever (GZ)** plotted as a function of the **Angle of Heel ($\phi$)**, ranging typically from $0°$ to $90°$ (or beyond to $180°$ in extreme cases). Mathematically, it expresses the transverse stability of a floating vessel as a moment arm at every angle of inclination.

$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

where:
- $GZ$ = Righting Lever (metres, **m**)
- $KN$ = Distance from Keel to the new metacentre-normal intersection point (**m**)
- $KG$ = Height of the ship's centre of gravity above the keel (**m**)
- $\phi$ = Angle of heel (degrees or radians)

When the principal design parameters of a ship — namely its **Breadth ($B$)**, **Freeboard ($F$)**, and the presence of **Superstructure** — are altered, the geometric shape of the underwater volume changes. This changes the position of the centre of buoyancy ($B$), the metacentre ($M$), and consequently the entire **GZ curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a **high-weightage** concept in Module 1 because it directly links the *hull geometry* to *transverse stability*, which is the foundation for all subsequent modules on dynamical stability and IMO criteria.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a **rectangular wooden block floating in water**, like a bar of soap. Now imagine three experiments:

1. **Breadth Experiment:** You take a second identical block but make it *wider* (you increase its breadth). If you tilt both blocks to the same angle, the wider one resists tilting more strongly. Why? Because a wider base means a larger change in the centre of buoyancy when the block heels, giving a bigger righting moment.

2. **Freeboard Experiment:** You take a third block and make it *taller* above the waterline (more freeboard). This shifts the block's centre of gravity ($G$) **upwards**. A higher $G$ means *less stability* because gravity now pulls more strongly to overturn the block.

3. **Superstructure Experiment:** You add a *small cabin* on top of the deck of the block. This cabin adds weight high up, again raising $G$, and it also traps additional buoyancy (wind/deck immersion effects) at large angles of heel. Both effects combine to alter the GZ curve in a non-linear fashion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Breadth makes the ship *more stable*; reducing freeboard or adding high superstructure makes the ship *less stable* (initially) but can actually improve stability at *large angles* through the **buoyancy of the superstructure**.

## 1.3 Effect of Each Parameter — Quick Summary

| Parameter Change | Direction of Change | Effect on Initial Stability (small $\phi$) | Effect on Large Angle Stability (large $\phi$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Breadth Increases** | $B \uparrow$ | Increases | Increases (significantly) |
| **Breadth Decreases** | $B \downarrow$ | Decreases | Decreases |
| **Freeboard Increases** | $F \uparrow$ | Decreases (G moves up) | Decreases (G moves up) |
| **Freeboard Decreases** | $F \downarrow$ | Increases | Increases |
| **Adding Superstructure** | Weight high up | Decreases | Variable (may increase due to added buoyancy) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of Increase in Breadth (Beam) on GZ Curve

### Physical Reasoning
The **Breadth ($B$)** of a ship is the *single most dominant geometric factor* affecting transverse stability. When breadth is increased:

- The **metacentric radius ($BM$)** increases because $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$, and the second moment of waterplane area $I_T \propto B^3$ for a rectangular approximation.
- The transverse metacentre $M$ moves **upward** relative to the centre of buoyancy $B$.
- The new position of the centre of buoyancy ($B'$) at any heel angle $\phi$ shifts **further outboard** laterally because a wider ship presents a broader underwater wedge.

### Mathematical Effect

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot \nabla} \quad \text{(rectangular approximation)}$$

Therefore, when breadth is increased from $B_1$ to $B_2$:

$$\frac{BM_2}{BM_1} = \left(\frac{B_2}{B_1}\right)^3$$

This is a **cubic relationship** — a small change in breadth produces a *huge* change in $BM$.

### Result on GZ Curve
- The **peak of the GZ curve shifts to the right** (larger angle).
- The **maximum GZ value increases substantially**.
- The **angle of maximum GZ** also tends to increase.
- The **range of stability** (angle of vanishing stability) **increases**.

## 2.2 Effect of Increase in Freeboard on GZ Curve

### Physical Reasoning
**Freeboard ($F$)** is the vertical distance from the design waterline to the *top of the freeboard deck (sheer line)*. Increasing freeboard means:

- The **height of the centre of gravity ($KG$) increases** because more hull volume is located above the waterline.
- The ship's lightweight is "stretched" vertically.
- The metacentric height $GM = KM - KG$ therefore **decreases** (assuming $KM$ does not change proportionally).

### Mathematical Effect

$$GM = KM - KG \quad \text{and} \quad KG \uparrow \implies GM \downarrow$$

### Result on GZ Curve
- The **initial slope of the GZ curve decreases** ($\tan(\text{slope}) = GM$).
- The **maximum GZ value decreases**.
- The **entire curve is depressed** downward.
- The **range of stability decreases** because the downflooding angle is reached sooner relative to the new (lower) stability profile.

## 2.3 Effect of Superstructure on GZ Curve

### Physical Reasoning
A **superstructure** is a decked structure on the freeboard deck (e.g., a deckhouse, bridge, or poop/front castle). It affects stability in **two competing ways**:

1. **Negative Effect (Weight Penalty):** The superstructure adds **lightweight high above the waterline**, which raises $G$ and reduces $GM$.
2. **Positive Effect (Added Buoyancy at Large Angles):** At large heel angles, the deck edge of the superstructure **immerses** in water, trapping an additional buoyancy volume. This provides a **buoyancy lever** that adds to $GZ$ at large angles.

### Mathematical Treatment
The correction to GZ due to superstructure buoyancy is given by:

$$GZ_{\text{corrected}} = GZ_{\text{hull only}} + \frac{\rho g V_{ss} \cdot d_{ss}}{\Delta g} \cdot (\text{lever arm factor})$$

Or in simplified form using displacement:

$$\Delta GZ_{ss} = \frac{v_{ss} \cdot \overline{GZ}_{ss}}{\nabla + v_{ss}}$$

where $v_{ss}$ = volume of immersion of superstructure, and $\overline{GZ}_{ss}$ = righting lever of the immersed superstructure about the ship's centre of gravity.

### Result on GZ Curve
- At **small angles of heel** (typically $\phi < 15°$): GZ is **reduced** due to raised $G$.
- At **large angles of heel** (typically $\phi > 30°$): GZ may be **enhanced** due to superstructure buoyancy.
- This produces a characteristic **"hump" in the GZ curve** at large angles when superstructure buoyancy engages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IMO 2008 IS Code Requirement:** Superstructure buoyancy **may be included** in the GZ curve calculation only if the superstructure is *enclosed* and meets the **weathertight integrity** criteria. Open or non-weathertight superstructures contribute only their *weight* (negative effect), not buoyancy.

## 2.4 Cross Curves of Stability (KN Curves)

### Formal Definition
**Cross Curves of Stability** (also called **KN curves**) are a family of curves plotted for a series of assumed ship displacements. Each curve gives the value of $KN$ (Keel to Metacentre-Normal distance) as a function of the angle of heel $\phi$, for a specific displacement.

$$KN = f(\phi) \quad \text{at constant displacement } \Delta$$

> [!NOTE]
> Cross curves are derived from **integration of the underwater volume shape** at successive heel angles. They are *independent of $KG$*. This makes them the **fundamental input** for computing GZ at any $KG$ using $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$.

### Typical Cross Curve Plot
For a given ship, cross curves are drawn for displacements ranging from **light ship condition** to **full load condition**, usually in equal steps (e.g., 10%, 20%, ..., 100% of full load displacement).

### How Parameter Changes Affect Cross Curves

| Parameter Change | Effect on Cross Curves ($KN$ values) |
|---|---|
| **Breadth Increases** | $KN$ values **increase** at all angles (steeper curves) |
| **Breadth Decreases** | $KN$ values **decrease** at all angles |
| **Freeboard Increases** | $KN$ values **largely unaffected** (freeboard changes $KG$, not hull form buoyancy) |
| **Adding Superstructure** | New $KN$ curves needed if superstructure buoyancy is to be included; otherwise original curves remain but $KG$ is raised |
| **Trim Changes** | Cross curves become *asymmetric* (port vs starboard) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT confuse *Cross Curves of Stability* with the *GZ curve*. Cross curves plot $KN$ vs $\phi$ for various displacements; the GZ curve plots $GZ$ vs $\phi$ for ONE displacement at ONE $KG$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises every key equation tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$ | Righting lever at angle $\phi$ | Computing GZ from cross curves |
| 2 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \nabla}$ | Metacentric radius (rectangular) | Breadth effect analysis |
| 3 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | Initial stability |
| 4 | $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ (rectangular) | Waterplane 2nd moment | Breadth cubed dependence |
| 5 | $\dfrac{BM_2}{BM_1} = \left(\dfrac{B_2}{B_1}\right)^3$ | Breadth cubed scaling | Comparing two breadths |
| 6 | $\sin(\theta) = \dfrac{BM \cdot \sin(\phi)}{GM + BM \cdot (1-\cos\phi)}$ | Wall-sided formula | Intermediate angle GZ |
| 7 | $\Delta GZ_{ss} = \dfrac{v_{ss} \cdot \overline{GZ}_{ss}}{\nabla + v_{ss}}$ | Superstructure buoyancy correction | Adding deckhouse buoyancy |
| 8 | $MCT = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot GM}{100 \cdot L}$ | Moment to change trim 1 cm | Trim-related stability (linked concept) |
| 9 | $KN = f(\phi, \Delta)$ | Cross curve definition | Reading from KN curve |

> **Unit Convention:** All lengths in **metres (m)**, volumes in **m³**, displacement $\Delta$ in **tonnes (t)** or **kg**, angles in **degrees** unless otherwise noted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Breadth on GZ (Cubic Relationship)

### Setup
Consider a rectangular pontoon of length $L$, breadth $B_1$, and displacement $\nabla$ at even keel. We wish to show how the GZ at a small angle $\phi$ changes when breadth is changed from $B_1$ to $B_2$ at the *same displacement* (implying a compensating draft change).

### Step 1 — Initial BM

$$BM_1 = \frac{I_{T1}}{\nabla} = \frac{L \cdot B_1^3}{12 \cdot \nabla}$$

### Step 2 — Final BM (after breadth change to $B_2$)

$$BM_2 = \frac{I_{T2}}{\nabla} = \frac{L \cdot B_2^3}{12 \cdot \nabla}$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$\frac{BM_2}{BM_1} = \frac{L \cdot B_2^3 / (12 \nabla)}{L \cdot B_1^3 / (12 \nabla)} = \frac{B_2^3}{B_1^3}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{BM_2 = BM_1 \cdot \left(\frac{B_2}{B_1}\right)^3}$$

### Step 4 — Apply to KM and GZ

Assuming $KB$ is small and approximately unchanged, and $KG$ is unchanged (no weight shift):

$$KM_2 \approx KB + BM_2 \quad \text{and} \quad GM_2 = KM_2 - KG$$

So the new GZ at any angle $\phi$:

$$GZ_2(\phi) = KN_2(\phi) - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

where $KN_2(\phi) > KN_1(\phi)$ at every angle because the wider hull shifts $B'$ further outboard.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Freeboard on GZ (KG Shift Model)

### Setup
A ship of original freeboard $F_1$ and $KG_1$ is modified by raising the deck by an amount $\Delta h$, giving new freeboard $F_2 = F_1 + \Delta h$.

### Step 1 — Compute New KG

The added deck and any associated structure increase the lightweight, and the new centre of gravity is:

$$KG_2 = \frac{W_1 \cdot KG_1 + W_{add} \cdot KG_{add}}{W_1 + W_{add}}$$

For simplicity, if all added weight is concentrated at the new deck level ($KG_{add} \approx KG_1 + \Delta h$ for a uniform ship):

$$KG_2 \approx KG_1 + \Delta h \cdot \frac{W_{add}}{W_1 + W_{add}}$$

### Step 2 — New GM

$$GM_2 = KM - KG_2 = GM_1 - \Delta KG$$

### Step 3 — New GZ at angle $\phi$

$$GZ_2(\phi) = KN(\phi) - KG_2 \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

$$\boxed{GZ_2(\phi) = GZ_1(\phi) - \Delta KG \cdot \sin(\phi)}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The reduction in GZ is **proportional to $\sin(\phi)$**, which means:
- At $\phi = 0°$: $\Delta GZ = 0$ (no change)
- At $\phi = 30°$: $\Delta GZ = 0.5 \cdot \Delta KG$ (50% of the $KG$ shift)
- At $\phi = 90°$: $\Delta GZ = \Delta KG$ (full $KG$ shift)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows that the GZ curve is **depressed by an amount $\Delta KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$** at every angle — a beautifully simple result that KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cross Curves of Stability (KN Curve) and GZ Extraction

### Step 1 — Cross Curve Generation (Conceptual)
For a given hull form at a fixed displacement $\Delta$, the underwater volume is divided into a fine mesh. At each heel angle $\phi$:

1. The submerged volume is re-integrated.
2. The new centre of buoyancy $B'$ is located.
3. The vertical line through $B'$ is extended to meet the original vertical centreline at $N$.
4. The horizontal distance $KN$ is plotted against $\phi$.

This produces the **KN curve** for displacement $\Delta$.

### Step 2 — From KN Curve to GZ Curve
Given $KG$ and the cross curve $KN(\phi)$ at displacement $\Delta$:

$$GZ(\phi) = KN(\phi) - KG \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
**Given:**
- Displacement $\Delta = 5000$ tonnes
- $KG = 4.0$ m
- KN cross curve values:

| $\phi$ (deg) | $KN$ (m) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 |
| 10 | 0.85 |
| 20 | 1.65 |
| 30 | 2.35 |
| 40 | 2.85 |
| 50 | 3.10 |
| 60 | 3.00 |

**Find:** The GZ curve.

**Solution Table:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{At } \phi = 10°: \quad GZ &= 0.85 - 4.0 \cdot \sin(10°) \\
&= 0.85 - 4.0 \cdot 0.1736 \\
&= 0.85 - 0.6945 \\
&= 0.156 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{At } \phi = 30°: \quad GZ &= 2.35 - 4.0 \cdot \sin(30°) \\
&= 2.35 - 4.0 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 2.35 - 2.00 \\
&= 0.35 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{At } \phi = 50°: \quad GZ &= 3.10 - 4.0 \cdot \sin(50°) \\
&= 3.10 - 4.0 \cdot 0.7660 \\
&= 3.10 - 3.064 \\
&= 0.036 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{At } \phi = 60°: \quad GZ &= 3.00 - 4.0 \cdot \sin(60°) \\
&= 3.00 - 4.0 \cdot 0.8660 \\
&= 3.00 - 3.464 \\
&= -0.464 \text{ m (NEGATIVE — capsize imminent)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Graphical Plot of GZ Curve

> The GZ curve starts at origin, rises gradually, peaks near 40°-50°, then crosses the horizontal axis at the **angle of vanishing stability** (≈ 55° in this example). Beyond this angle, GZ is negative — the ship will capsize.

## 3.4 Effect of Adding a Superstructure — Detailed Analysis

### Step 1 — Quantify the Weight Effect
A superstructure of mass $m_{ss}$ is added at height $h_{ss}$ above the original $G$:

$$\Delta KG = \frac{m_{ss} \cdot h_{ss}}{\Delta_{\text{total}}}$$

### Step 2 — Quantify the Buoyancy Effect
At heel angle $\phi$, the superstructure is partially immersed. The immersed volume $v_{ss}(\phi)$ provides a buoyant force whose lever arm about the ship's centre of gravity is $\ell_{ss}(\phi)$.

The **added righting moment** is:

$$\Delta RM_{ss} = \rho g \cdot v_{ss}(\phi) \cdot \ell_{ss}(\phi)$$

### Step 3 — Net Correction to GZ

$$\Delta GZ_{net}(\phi) = -\frac{m_{ss} \cdot h_{ss} \cdot \sin(\phi)}{\Delta_{\text{total}}} + \frac{v_{ss}(\phi) \cdot \ell_{ss}(\phi)}{\nabla + v_{ss}(\phi)}$$

### Step 4 — Three Zones on the GZ Curve

| Zone | Angle Range | Dominant Effect | Net Change in GZ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone I** | $0° \leq \phi < \phi_{imm}$ | Weight penalty only | **Decrease** |
| **Zone II** | $\phi_{imm} \leq \phi < \phi_{max}$ | Buoyancy engages | Net effect balances; GZ **recovers** |
| **Zone III** | $\phi > \phi_{max}$ | Buoyancy dominant | **Increase** in GZ (hump) |

where $\phi_{imm}$ is the **deck-edge immersion angle** — the heel angle at which the superstructure deck just touches the water.

## 3.5 Master Analytical Matrix: Summary of All Effects

| Design Change | $BM$ | $KG$ | $GM$ | Initial GZ slope | Max GZ | Range of Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Breadth $\uparrow$** | $\uparrow$ (cubically) | $\approx$ same | $\uparrow$ | Steeper | Higher | Wider |
| **Freeboard $\uparrow$** | $\approx$ same | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Gentler | Lower | Narrower |
| **Add superstructure** | unchanged | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Gentler | Variable (hump) | Usually wider if weathertight |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GZ Curve Under Three Parameter Changes (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["DESIGN INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        A1["Breadth B"]
        A2["Freeboard F"]
        A3["Superstructure Configuration"]
    end

    subgraph Geometry["HULL GEOMETRY RESPONSE"]
        B1["Waterplane Area 2nd Moment IT"]
        B2["Centre of Gravity Height KG"]
        B3["Underwater Volume at Heel phi"]
    end

    subgraph Stability["STABILITY QUANTITIES"]
        C1["Metacentric Radius BM"]
        C2["Metacentric Height GM"]
        C3["Righting Lever GZ"]
    end

    subgraph Output["GZ CURVE OUTPUTS"]
        D1["Initial Slope at phi = 0"]
        D2["Maximum GZ Value and Angle"]
        D3["Range of Stability"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A3 --> B3

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    B3 --> C3

    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: GZ Curve Shapes Under Different Modifications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Original["ORIGINAL SHIP"]
        O1["GZ curve baseline"]
    end

    subgraph BreadthUp["BREADTH INCREASED"]
        BU1["GZ curve: steeper, taller, wider peak"]
    end

    subgraph FreeboardUp["FREEBOARD INCREASED"]
        FU1["GZ curve: depressed at all angles, range shrinks"]
    end

    subgraph SuperAdd["SUPERSTRUCTURE ADDED"]
        SA1["GZ curve: dips at small angles, then HUMP at large angles"]
    end

    O1 -. B increase .-> BU1
    O1 -. F increase .-> FU1
    O1 -. superstructure added .-> SA1

    BU1 --> Result["Combined effect evaluated using IMO criteria"]
    FU1 --> Result
    SA1 --> Result
```

## 4.3 Cross Curves of Stability — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Hydrostatic["HYDROSTATIC INPUTS"]
        H1["Hull Form Lines Plan"]
        H2["Displacement Delta"]
        H3["Heel Angle phi - sample 0, 10, 20 ... 90 deg"]
    end

    subgraph Integration["NUMERICAL INTEGRATION AT EACH PHI"]
        I1["Submerged Volume V sub phi"]
        I2["Centre of Buoyancy B prime location"]
        I3["Normal through B prime intersects KN axis"]
    end

    subgraph Output["CROSS CURVES OUTPUT"]
        O1["KN vs phi curve for ONE displacement"]
        O2["Family of KN curves for multiple displacements"]
    end

    subgraph Usage["DOWNSTREAM USAGE"]
        U1["Apply GZ = KN minus KG sin phi"]
        U2["Generate GZ curve"]
        U3["Check IMO Area 0 to 30, 0 to 40, 30 to 40 criteria"]
    end

    H1 --> I1
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> U3
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Superstructure Immersion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SmallAngle["SMALL HEEL ANGLE phi less than phi immersion"]
        S1["Superstructure is in AIR"]
        S2["Effect: WEIGHT PENALTY only"]
        S3["GZ decreases by Delta KG sin phi"]
    end

    subgraph LargeAngle["LARGE HEEL ANGLE phi greater than phi immersion"]
        L1["Superstructure deck touches water"]
        L2["Trapped buoyancy vss phi develops"]
        L3["Buoyancy lever arm lss phi acts above G"]
        L4["GZ gains HUMP from added righting moment"]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
```

## 4.5 Cross-Curve Plot (Conceptual Description)

A typical cross-curve plot is a **family of curves** on a single graph. The horizontal axis is the angle of heel $\phi$ (0° to 90°), and the vertical axis is $KN$ (in metres). Each curve corresponds to a different displacement. Lower displacement curves (lighter ship) typically show **steeper rises and lower peaks** at moderate angles, while higher displacement curves (deeper ship) show **flatter rises and higher peaks** at larger angles. From this family, the naval architect reads $KN$ at any angle for the actual operating displacement, then subtracts $KG \sin(\phi)$ to obtain the GZ curve.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 board exam frequently asks students to *describe* the shape of cross curves and the procedure to extract a GZ curve from them. Memorise the standard layout described above.
