<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Pattern]**

### **Q1. Define floodable length. State the formula for computing the same.**

**Model Answer:**

> Floodable length is defined as the **maximum permissible length of a watertight compartment** which, when assumed to be completely flooded with water up to the margin line, will not submerge the ship below the margin line, assuming a standard flooding angle corresponding to deck-edge or bulkhead-deck immersion.
> 
> **Attwood's Formula:**
> $$l = \frac{W \cdot GM}{\mu \cdot w \cdot x} = \frac{V^2 \cdot GM}{\mu \cdot A \cdot I}$$
> 
> where $W$ = displacement, $GM$ = metacentric height, $\mu$ = permeability, $w$ = weight of water per unit length, $x$ = lever arm, $V$ = displacement volume, $A$ = waterplane area, $I$ = second moment of W.P.

---

### **Q2. What is permeability? How does it affect the floodable length of a compartment?**

**Model Answer:**

> Permeability $\mu$ is the ratio of the volume available for water entry within a compartment to the total (gross) volume of that compartment. It is a dimensionless quantity, always less than 1.
> 
> **Effect:** From Attwood's formula, $l \propto 1/\mu$. Therefore, **higher permeability → smaller floodable length** (because more water enters, causing greater upset). Conversely, compartments occupied by cargo, machinery, or stores have lower $\mu$, thus allowing a **larger** floodable length at the same position.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern Model]**

**(a)** Derive Attwood's formula for the floodable length of a compartment, clearly defining each variable used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship of displacement 18 000 t has a metacentric height of 0.75 m, waterplane area 1 500 m², and second moment of waterplane area 110 000 m⁴. A compartment located 25 m from the centroid of the waterplane has permeability 0.80. The deck-edge immersion angle at this position is 12°. Determine the floodable length of the compartment. **(7 marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Consider a compartment of length $l$ at distance $x$ from the centroid of the waterplane $L$, flooding angle $\theta$.

**Step 2:** Write the moment equilibrium:

$$W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = \mu \cdot w \cdot l \cdot x \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 3:** For a small flooding angle, $\cos\theta \approx 1$, so:

$$W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = \mu \cdot w \cdot l \cdot x$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $l$:

$$l = \frac{W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta}{\mu \cdot w \cdot x}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $w = \rho g A$ and $x = BM$:

$$l = \frac{W \cdot GM}{\mu \cdot \rho g \cdot A \cdot BM}$$

**Step 6:** Using $W = \rho g V$ and $BM = I/V$:

$$\boxed{\;l = \frac{V^2 \cdot GM}{\mu \cdot A \cdot I}\;}$$

This is the classical Attwood formula. The deck-edge immersion angle $\phi$ introduces a tangent correction:

$$l = \frac{V^2 \cdot GM \cdot \tan\phi}{\mu \cdot A \cdot I}$$

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for moment equation, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final form.)*

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $W = 18\,000$ t; $GM = 0.75$ m; $A = 1\,500$ m²; $I = 110\,000$ m⁴; $x = 25$ m; $\mu = 0.80$; $\phi = 12°$.

**Step 1 — Find volume of displacement:**

Take seawater density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³:

$$V = \frac{W}{\rho} = \frac{18\,000}{1.025} = 17\,561 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply Attwood's formula with deck-immersion angle:**

$$l = \frac{V^2 \cdot GM \cdot \tan\phi}{\mu \cdot A \cdot I}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$V^2 = (17\,561)^2 = 3.084 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^6$$

$$\tan 12° = 0.2126$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 3.084 \times 10^8 \times 0.75 \times 0.2126 = 4.917 \times 10^7$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$\mu \cdot A \cdot I = 0.80 \times 1\,500 \times 110\,000 = 1.32 \times 10^8$$

**Step 5 — Compute $l$:**

$$l = \frac{4.917 \times 10^7}{1.32 \times 10^8} = 0.3725 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $l \approx 0.37$ m. Any compartment longer than 0.37 m at this position cannot be flooded without submerging the margin line.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Attwood's curve. How is the curve constructed and used for the positioning of watertight bulkheads in a ship? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two adjacent compartments of a ship have lengths 25 m and 30 m and permeabilities 0.85 and 0.70 respectively. If the ship has displacement 10 000 t, $GM = 0.60$ m, and the weight of floodwater per unit length is 80 t/m for both compartments, determine the maximum permissible lever arm from the centroid of the waterplane for the combined compartment to remain within floodable length limits. Take flooding angle as 10°. **(7 marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Attwood's Curve:**

Attwood's curve is a **graphical representation** of the floodable length of a compartment as a function of its **longitudinal position** $x$ measured from the centroid of the waterplane (or midship). The curve is **U-shaped** (or sometimes more complex) and **symmetrical** about the centreline.

**Construction Steps:**
1. Compute ship form data: $W$, $V$, $A$, $I$, $BM$, $GM$.
2. For a series of longitudinal positions $x_i$, compute the **deck-edge immersion angle** $\phi_i$ using the half-beam and the local freeboard.
3. Apply Attwood's formula: $l_i = \dfrac{V^2 \cdot GM \cdot \tan\phi_i}{\mu \cdot A \cdot I}$
4. Plot $l_i$ vs $x_i$ → Attwood's curve.
5. Mark on the same graph the **actual lengths** of the proposed compartments as horizontal lines at their respective positions.
6. Where a compartment's actual length **exceeds** the Attwood curve value, the bulkhead arrangement must be revised.

**Use in Bulkhead Positioning:**
- The curve provides an **upper bound** on compartment length at every station.
- A bulkhead schedule is considered safe if every compartment's length lies **entirely within** the Attwood curve.
- Modern SOLAS probabilistic damage stability uses the **survival kit** approach that builds upon this classical concept.

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $l_1 = 25$ m, $\mu_1 = 0.85$
- $l_2 = 30$ m, $\mu_2 = 0.70$
- $W = 10\,000$ t, $GM = 0.60$ m
- $w = 80$ t/m, $\theta_f = 10°$

**Step 1 — Find weighted average permeability:**

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_1 l_1 + \mu_2 l_2}{l_1 + l_2} = \frac{0.85 \times 25 + 0.70 \times 30}{55} = \frac{21.25 + 21.0}{55} = \frac{42.25}{55} = 0.7682$$

**Step 2 — Total length:**

$$l_{total} = 25 + 30 = 55 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Attwood's formula and solve for $x$:**

$$x = \frac{W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta_f}{\mu_{eff} \cdot w \cdot l_{total} \cdot \cos\theta_f}$$

$$\sin 10° = 0.1736, \quad \cos 10° = 0.9848$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta_f = 10\,000 \times 0.60 \times 0.1736 = 1041.6$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$\mu_{eff} \cdot w \cdot l_{total} \cdot \cos\theta_f = 0.7682 \times 80 \times 55 \times 0.9848 = 3329.1$$

**Step 6 — Compute $x$:**

$$x = \frac{1041.6}{3329.1} = 0.3129 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** The combined compartment can be safely flooded only if its centre lies within 0.31 m of the centroid of the waterplane — meaning it must be **almost exactly at midship**. Such a tight constraint indicates that the flooding condition is critical; subdivision into smaller compartments is essential.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing lever arm $x$:** Many students take $x$ as the distance from midship, ignoring the location of the centroid of the waterplane. For non-symmetric load conditions (e.g., partly loaded), these are different.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tan\phi$ factor:** When the flooding angle is **not small**, the deck-edge immersion angle correction $\tan\phi$ MUST be included. A common mistake is to use $\sin\theta$ instead of $\tan\phi$.
> 
> 3. **Permeability of mixed compartments:** When two compartments are flooded together, students often use the permeability of one compartment and ignore the other. Always compute a **weighted average permeability** based on the lengths.
> 
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Ensure that $W$ is in tonnes, $V$ in m³, $A$ in m², and $I$ in m⁴. Inconsistency in units will lead to wrong answers.
> 
> 5. **Direction of the heeling moment:** The lever arm $x$ must be measured **perpendicular to the new vertical (shifted) centre line**, not along the original waterline. For small angles, this distinction is negligible, but in boundary cases, it matters.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- ✅ **Floodable length** = maximum length of a compartment that can be flooded without submerging the **margin line** (76 mm below bulkhead deck).
- ✅ **Attwood's formula (general):** $l = \dfrac{W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta}{\mu \cdot w \cdot x \cdot \cos\theta}$
- ✅ **Attwood's formula (volume form):** $l = \dfrac{V^2 \cdot GM \cdot \tan\phi}{\mu \cdot A \cdot I}$
- ✅ **Permeability** = ratio of floodable volume to total compartment volume; **higher μ → smaller l**.
- ✅ **Attwood's curve** is a U-shaped graph of $l$ vs. longitudinal position $x$.
- ✅ **Smallest floodable length** occurs at midship (for symmetric ships).
- ✅ **Largest floodable length** typically occurs some distance from midship, modulated by deck-immersion angle.
- ✅ **SOLAS** damage stability regulations are the modern application of the floodable length concept.
- ✅ **Margin line** is at 76 mm (3 in.) below the bulkhead deck — a universal safety buffer.
- ✅ **Flooding angle** is the heel at which the margin line is just submerged; assumed small in elementary derivations.
- ✅ **Weighted permeability** must be computed for multiple adjacent compartments.
- ✅ The formula is **inversely proportional to permeability and lever arm**, and **directly proportional to displacement and metacentric height**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factor of Subdivision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factor of Subdivision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **FACTOR OF SUBDIVISION (Fs) — SOLAS Chapter II-1, Reg. 7 Definition**
> The **Factor of Subdivision** is a dimensionless numerical measure of the standard of watertight subdivision achieved in a passenger ship. It is formally defined as the ratio of the **Required Subdivision Index (R)** to the **Attained Subdivision Index (A)**.

$$F_s = \frac{R}{A}$$

A ship is considered to have an **acceptable standard of subdivision** only when the **Attained Subdivision Index (A)** is **not less than** the **Required Subdivision Index (R)**, which mathematically means:

$$A \ge R \quad \iff \quad F_s \le 1.0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This concept is a **probabilistic approach** to damage stability introduced by the **1960 SOLAS Convention** and refined through **1988 Protocol / 2008 IS Code** for passenger ships. It shifts the philosophy from rigid deterministic criteria to a **risk-based survivability assessment**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **long, multi-storey passenger train** (think of a *cruise ship as a horizontal skyscraper*). The train is divided into **compartments** by **sliding fire-doors** and **bulkheads**.

Now ask:
> *"If water floods into one compartment, will the train still float upright, or will it capsize like a see-saw?"*

The **Factor of Subdivision** is essentially a **"safety score"** given to the ship designer. It answers:

- *How well did the naval architect slice up the ship's hull into small, isolated compartments?*
- *Are the bulkheads positioned smartly so that any *plausible* flooding scenario leaves the ship floating and stable?*

### 🍕 The Pizza Analogy

Think of the ship's hull as a **long pizza**. The Factor of Subdivision says:

1. **Slicing the pizza into MANY small slices** (many small compartments) → If a slice gets flooded, the rest of the pizza is still intact. **High subdivision, High survival.**
2. **Leaving the pizza in two giant halves** (only a few large compartments) → If one half is ruined, the ship may sink or capsize. **Low subdivision, poor survival.**

> *The naval architect's job is to find the sweet spot — enough slices for safety, but not so many that the ship becomes a "sieve" with too many doors and bulkheads (loss of usable space).*

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Regulatory Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Permeability (μ) — Passenger spaces** | **0.95** | SOLAS Reg. 7 |
| **Permeability (μ) — Crew spaces** | **0.70** | SOLAS Reg. 7 |
| **Permeability (μ) — Machinery spaces** | **0.85** | SOLAS Reg. 7 |
| **Permeability (μ) — Dry stores, voids** | **0.60** | SOLAS Reg. 7 |
| **Permeability (μ) — Liquid tanks** | **0 or 0.95** (depending on filling) | SOLAS Reg. 7 |
| **Maximum permissible Fs** | **1.000** | SOLAS Reg. 7.1 |
| **Survival probability (s) maximum** | **1.000** (intact) | SOLAS Reg. 7 |
| **Survival probability (s) minimum** | **0.000** (capsize) | SOLAS Reg. 7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permeability (μ)** is the ratio of the volume of flooded water inside a space to the total volume of that space. It is never **1.0** because furniture, equipment, and structure displace some volume.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Required Index (R) vs. Attained Index (A)

The Factor of Subdivision rests on **two independent probabilistic indices** that the naval architect must compute and compare.

### 2.1.1 Required Subdivision Index (R)

> The **Required Subdivision Index (R)** is a function of:
> - The **length (L)** of the ship
> - The **Number of Persons on Board (N)** (passengers + crew)
> - The **Service Class** (Class I, II, III based on voyage length)

It is obtained from a **standardised curve or table** provided in SOLAS Reg. 7.3, where:

$$R = f(L, N, \text{Service Class})$$

**Physical meaning:** *R* is the **minimum acceptable level of safety**. It grows as ships get longer and carry more people.

---

### 2.1.2 Attained Subdivision Index (A)

The **Attained Subdivision Index (A)** is **calculated** for the actual ship design:

$$A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (p_i \cdot s_i)$$

Where:
- $p_i$ = Probability that **only compartment *i* (or group *i*)** is flooded
- $s_i$ = Probability of **survival** after that flooding event
- The summation is over **all possible flooding scenarios** (single, double, triple … compartments)

**Physical meaning:** *A* is the **measured safety level** of the proposed design. It is the **weighted average** of survival probabilities across all credible damage scenarios.

---

## 2.2 Why This System Was Invented

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context — Why probabilistic and not just deterministic?**
> Before 1960, ship safety was evaluated using a **worst-case single-compartment flooding** rule. But the **Titanic (1912)** and **MV Herald of Free Enterprise (1987)** disasters showed that:
> - The "worst case" was not always predictable.
> - Some *double* compartment floodings were nearly as likely as single ones.
> - A ship with good subdivision but average bulkhead spacing might survive better than a ship with a single ideal compartment.

The IMO therefore adopted a **probabilistic framework** in 1960 (refined in 1974 SOLAS, 1988 Protocol, and 2008 IS Code) to give **credit for good design** in many partial flooding scenarios rather than only the most extreme.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Calculating "A"

1. **Divide the ship** into watertight compartments using the bulkhead arrangement.
2. **For each compartment *i***, compute the probability of flooding $p_i$. This uses the **flooding length** concept (the longitudinal extent of damage that can breach that compartment).
3. **For each flooding scenario**, determine the **survival factor** $s_i$ using the **s-curve** (a function of residual metacentric height, heel angle, and range of stability).
4. **Multiply and sum** over all scenarios.

---

## 2.4 The Survival Factor *s* — The Heart of the Calculation

The survival factor $s_i$ for a given damage case is:

$$s_i = s_{\text{heel}} \cdot s_{\text{trim}} \cdot s_{\text{reserve stability}}$$

In SOLAS, the simplified $s$ is determined from **standardised curves**:

- $s = 1.00$ if the final waterline is below the margin line and the residual GM is positive with adequate range of stability.
- $s = 0.50$ if the residual GM is barely positive.
- $s = 0.00$ if the ship capsizes, sinks, or the final waterline exceeds the margin line.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorise for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_s = \dfrac{R}{A}$ | Factor of Subdivision | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i$ | Attained Subdivision Index | Sum over all damage cases |
| 3 | $p_i = \dfrac{l_i}{L} \cdot \Delta l$ | Probability of flooding for case *i* | Based on damage length $l_i$ |
| 4 | $s_i = 0.5 \cdot (s_1 + s_2 + \cdots + s_n)$ | Average survival factor (some interpretations) | Range: 0 to 1 |
| 5 | $\mu = \dfrac{V_{\text{water}}}{V_{\text{space}}}$ | Permeability | Range: 0 to 0.95 |
| 6 | $A \ge R$ | Compliance criterion | Equivalent to $F_s \le 1.0$ |
| 7 | $s = 0$ if heel > 20° or trim > 10° | Capsize failure | SOLAS Reg. 7.2.3 |
| 8 | $s = 0$ if final WL above margin line | Margin line failure | SOLAS Reg. 7.2.1 |
| 9 | $F_s \le 1.0$ | Design acceptance | Hard regulatory limit |
| 10 | $\sum p_i = 1$ | Total probability over all cases | Normalisation check |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this concept matters in practice:**
> - **Passenger Ship Design:** Every cruise ship (e.g., *Symphony of the Seas*, *Diamond Princess*) must satisfy $A \ge R$ to obtain a **Passenger Ship Safety Certificate**.
> - **Bulkhead Position Optimisation:** Naval architects iterate bulkhead positions to **maximise *A*** (lowest $F_s$) without sacrificing cargo/passenger capacity.
> - **Class Society Verification:** Agencies like **Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas** perform these calculations during the **Approval in Principle (AIP)** stage.
> - **Conversion Projects:** When a ship is **lengthened, shortened, or re-purposed**, the Factor of Subdivision must be **re-calculated and re-certified**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attained Subdivision Index Formula

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Total Probability of Survival

The fundamental principle is the **law of total probability**. If we have *n* mutually exclusive flooding scenarios, and the probability of each scenario is $p_i$, with the conditional probability of survival being $s_i$, then the **overall probability of survival** is:

$$
P(\text{survival}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{survival} \mid \text{Case } i) \cdot P(\text{Case } i)
$$

### 3.1.2 Substituting Probabilistic Terms

By SOLAS convention:
- $P(\text{Case } i) \equiv p_i$ (probability of flooding case *i*)
- $P(\text{survival} \mid \text{Case } i) \equiv s_i$ (survival factor for case *i*)

Therefore:

$$
A = P(\text{survival}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i
$$

> *This is the **Attained Subdivision Index** — the overall expected probability that the ship will survive any credible flooding event.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Flooding Probability $p_i$

The probability of flooding a specific compartment or group is based on the **flooding length** — the longitudinal extent of damage that breaches it.

For a single compartment *i* bounded by bulkheads at positions $x_{i-1}$ and $x_i$:

$$
p_i = \frac{l_{\text{damage}}}{L_{\text{total}}}
$$

Where:
- $l_{\text{damage}}$ is the assumed damage length (typically a function of the **subdivision length**)
- $L_{\text{total}}$ is the ship's length

For **transverse bulkhead arrangements** at irregular intervals, the formula becomes:

$$
p_i = \frac{\Delta L_i}{L}
$$

Where $\Delta L_i$ is the **effective damage length** that can flood compartment *i*.

> **Textual explanation:** The longer the ship, the more likely a randomly positioned damage breach will hit a given compartment. Compartments located amidships have a **higher** $p_i$ than those at the ends (longer run of damage can include them).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A passenger ship has 4 watertight compartments. The flooding probabilities and survival factors for each compartment are given. Calculate the **Attained Subdivision Index (A)** and the **Factor of Subdivision (Fs)**, given the Required Index **R = 0.72**.

| Compartment *i* | $p_i$ | $s_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.18 | 0.90 |
| 2 | 0.27 | 0.75 |
| 3 | 0.32 | 0.60 |
| 4 | 0.23 | 0.85 |

**Verification of total probability:**

$$
\sum p_i = 0.18 + 0.27 + 0.32 + 0.23 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Compute the Attained Subdivision Index

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i \cdot s_i
$$

$$
A = (0.18 \times 0.90) + (0.27 \times 0.75) + (0.32 \times 0.60) + (0.23 \times 0.85)
$$

$$
A = 0.162 + 0.2025 + 0.192 + 0.1955
$$

$$
A = 0.752
$$

---

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Compute the Factor of Subdivision

$$
F_s = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{0.72}{0.752} = 0.9574
$$

---

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compliance Check

$$
F_s = 0.9574 \le 1.0 \quad \checkmark \quad \text{(COMPLIANT)}
$$

> *Since $A = 0.752 \ge R = 0.72$, the subdivision is acceptable. The ship has roughly a **4.3% safety margin**.*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Design Changes

> [!NOTE]
> **The following analytical matrix shows how a designer's choices shift $F_s$:**

| Design Choice | Effect on $A$ | Effect on $R$ | Net Effect on $F_s$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Add one extra bulkhead amidships** | ↑ Increases | → No change | ↓ Decreases (safer) |
| **Lengthen the ship** | → Slight ↑ | ↑↑ Strongly increases | ↑ Increases (worse) |
| **Increase passenger capacity (N)** | → No change | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (worse) |
| **Shift bulkheads toward mid-length** | ↑ Increases | → No change | ↓ Decreases (safer) |
| **Reduce compartment permeability** | ↑ Increases | → No change | ↓ Decreases (safer) |
| **Increase number of persons on board** | → No change | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (worse) |

> **Key Insight for KTU:** The designer can **only** influence $A$ — the regulation **fixes** $R$. So all optimisation effort focuses on **maximising $A$**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Subdivision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Passenger Ship Design] --> nodeB{Compute R from SOLAS curve L, N, Service}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 1: Identify all flooding cases]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 2: For each case, compute p_i]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 3: For each case, compute s_i from residual stability]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 4: Calculate A = sum of p_i times s_i]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Is A greater than or equal to R?}
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH[Design ACCEPTED - Fs less than or equal to 1.0]
    nodeG -->|No| nodeI[Design REJECTED]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Revise bulkhead positions]
    nodeJ --> nodeC
    nodeH --> nodeK[Issue Passenger Ship Safety Certificate]
    nodeK --> nodeL[End]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Survival Factor Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Flooding Case i Initiated] --> nodeB{Final waterline above margin line?}
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[s_i equals 0 - Capsize by Margin Line Failure]
    nodeB -->|No| nodeD{Heel angle exceeds 20 degrees?}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeC
    nodeD -->|No| nodeE{Trim angle exceeds 10 degrees?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeC
    nodeE -->|No| nodeF{Residual GM positive?}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeC
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG{Range of stability adequate?}
    nodeG -->|No| nodeC
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH[Compute s_i from standard s curve]
    nodeH --> nodeI[s_i value between 0 and 1]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Multiply by p_i to get contribution to A]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Architecture of a Subdivision Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        node1[Ship Length L]
        node2[Number of Persons N]
        node3[Service Class Type]
        node4[Bulkhead Positions]
        node5[Compartment Permeability]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Layer]
        node6[Module A: Required Index R from SOLAS table]
        node7[Module B: Flooding Probability p_i for each case]
        node8[Module C: Survival Factor s_i from damage stability]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        node9[Attained Subdivision Index A]
        node10[Factor of Subdivision Fs]
        node11{Compliance Check Fs less than or equal to 1.0}
    end
    node1 --> node6
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node10
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 -->|Yes| node12[APPROVED]
    node11 -->|No| node13[REDESIGN]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — The Pizza-Slice Analogy in Flowchart Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ship Hull as a Long Pizza] --> nodeB{Bulkhead Arrangement}
    nodeB -->|Few Slices| nodeC[Large Compartments - LOW subdivision]
    nodeB -->|Many Slices| nodeD[Small Compartments - HIGH subdivision]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Single flooding = huge p_i - high impact]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Single flooding = small p_i - low impact]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Low A, High Fs, NON-COMPLIANT]
    nodeF --> nodeH[High A, Low Fs, COMPLIANT]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Add more bulkheads - more slices]
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeH --> nodeJ[OPTIMAL DESIGN]
```

> **Reading Tip for KTU:** The "pizza" diagram is a useful **classroom-intuition** tool but examiners will expect the **formal SOLAS Reg. 7 framework** in any calculation.

---
