<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "Factor of Subdivision" and state the compliance condition as per SOLAS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Factor of Subdivision ($F_s$)** is defined as the ratio of the **Required Subdivision Index (R)** to the **Attained Subdivision Index (A)** of a passenger ship:
> 
> $$F_s = \frac{R}{A}$$
> 
> The compliance condition, as per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 7**, is that the **Attained Subdivision Index must not be less than the Required Subdivision Index**:
> 
> $$A \ge R \quad \iff \quad F_s \le 1.0$$
> 
> *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for compliance condition.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **What is permeability in the context of damage stability? List the standard permeability values for two spaces.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Permeability ($\mu$)** is defined as the ratio of the volume occupied by **flooded water** inside a space to the **total volume of that space**:
> 
> $$\mu = \frac{V_{\text{water}}}{V_{\text{space}}}$$
> 
> Standard SOLAS values:
> - **Passenger spaces:** $\mu = 0.95$
> - **Crew spaces:** $\mu = 0.70$
> - **Machinery spaces:** $\mu = 0.85$
> - **Dry cargo / voids:** $\mu = 0.60$
> 
> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any two values with units.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - Students **forget to normalise** the $p_i$ values. Always check $\sum p_i = 1$ before computing $A$.
> - Confusing $A$ with $R$ (mixing up required and attained).
> - Applying **cargo ship** rules (which use deterministic $F_s$ based on floodable length) to passenger ships (which use the probabilistic $A$ and $R$ framework).
> - Stating the compliance condition as $F_s < 1.0$ (strict inequality) — it is **$\le 1.0$**.
> - Forgetting to **interpolate** the value of $R$ from the SOLAS curve when $L$ does not match a table entry.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Attained Subdivision Index (A)** as used in SOLAS damage stability regulations. Derive the expression for $A$ and discuss the physical meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A passenger ship has the following data:

| Compartment *i* | $p_i$ | $s_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.15 | 0.80 |
| 2 | 0.25 | 0.65 |
| 3 | 0.35 | 0.55 |
| 4 | 0.25 | 0.90 |

If the Required Subdivision Index is **R = 0.65**, calculate the Attained Subdivision Index and the Factor of Subdivision. State whether the design complies with SOLAS. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Explanation of Attained Subdivision Index (A) [7 Marks]

The **Attained Subdivision Index (A)** is the **weighted average probability of survival** of a passenger ship after flooding, summed over **all credible damage scenarios**.

**Derivation:**

By the law of total probability, the overall probability of survival is:

$$
P(\text{survival}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{survival} \mid \text{Case } i) \cdot P(\text{Case } i)
$$

In SOLAS notation:

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where:
- $p_i$ = probability that compartment (or group) *i* alone is flooded.
- $s_i$ = conditional probability of survival after that flooding.
- $n$ = total number of damage cases considered.

**Physical meaning:**
- $p_i$ represents the **chance that a collision or grounding** will breach exactly the bulkheads defining compartment *i*.
- $s_i$ captures the **engineering survivability** — does the damaged ship remain upright and afloat?
- The product $p_i \cdot s_i$ is the **contribution** of case *i* to the overall safety.
- Summing gives the **total expected safety** of the design.

A high $A$ (close to 1.0) means the ship is well-protected against all plausible flooding cases.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Verify normalisation**

$$
\sum p_i = 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.35 + 0.25 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Compute A**

$$
A = (0.15 \times 0.80) + (0.25 \times 0.65) + (0.35 \times 0.55) + (0.25 \times 0.90)
$$

$$
A = 0.1200 + 0.1625 + 0.1925 + 0.2250
$$

$$
A = 0.7000
$$

**Step 3: Compute Fs**

$$
F_s = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{0.65}{0.70} = 0.9286
$$

**Step 4: Compliance check**

Since $F_s = 0.9286 \le 1.0$, the design **complies with SOLAS** with a safety margin of approximately **7.14%**.

> *Also valid: $A = 0.70 \ge R = 0.65 \quad \checkmark$*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **Required Subdivision Index (R)** and explain how it is determined. What factors influence its value? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A passenger ship has three flooding cases. The data is:

| Case *i* | $p_i$ | $s_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 (Single compartment) | 0.55 | 0.95 |
| 2 (Two compartments) | 0.30 | 0.60 |
| 3 (Three compartments) | 0.15 | 0.10 |

If the Required Subdivision Index is **R = 0.70**, determine the Attained Subdivision Index and the Factor of Subdivision. Comment on the design's survivability. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Required Subdivision Index (R) [7 Marks]

The **Required Subdivision Index (R)** is the **minimum acceptable value** of the Attained Subdivision Index that a passenger ship must achieve to comply with SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 7.

**Determination of R:**

$R$ is obtained from **standardised curves or tables** given in SOLAS based on:

1. **Length of the ship (L)** — As length increases, R increases (longer ships need finer subdivision).
2. **Number of persons on board (N)** — More people (typically passengers) demand higher safety, increasing R.
3. **Service Class:**
   - **Class I** — Short international voyages (highest R)
   - **Class II** — Intermediate voyages
   - **Class III** — Short voyages close to coast (lowest R)

**Form of the equation:**

$$
R = f(L, N, \text{Service Class})
$$

The functional form is provided in SOLAS tables. **R must always be less than or equal to A** for compliance.

**Factors that increase R:**
- Longer ship
- More passengers
- More rigorous service class (Class I > Class II > Class III)

**Factors the designer cannot control directly** (these are regulatory inputs) are what make R a **fixed target** that the designer must achieve by manipulating bulkhead positions.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Verify normalisation**

$$
\sum p_i = 0.55 + 0.30 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Compute A**

$$
A = (0.55 \times 0.95) + (0.30 \times 0.60) + (0.15 \times 0.10)
$$

$$
A = 0.5225 + 0.1800 + 0.0150
$$

$$
A = 0.7175
$$

**Step 3: Compute Fs**

$$
F_s = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{0.70}{0.7175} = 0.9756
$$

**Step 4: Compliance check**

Since $F_s = 0.9756 \le 1.0$, the design **complies** but with a **very thin safety margin** of only **2.44%**.

> *Critical Comment:* Although the design formally complies, the three-compartment flooding case has a survival factor of only **0.10** — meaning if three compartments are breached, the ship is almost certain to be lost. The designer should consider **adding additional transverse bulkheads** to either reduce $p_3$ or improve $s_3$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- ☐ **Definition:** $F_s = R / A$ — dimensionless compliance ratio for passenger ship subdivision.
- ☐ **Compliance criterion:** $A \ge R$ OR equivalently $F_s \le 1.0$ (NOT strictly less than).
- ☐ **R is read** from a SOLAS curve/table; it depends on L, N, and service class.
- ☐ **A is calculated** as a sum: $A = \sum p_i \cdot s_i$ over all damage cases.
- ☐ **Normalisation check:** $\sum p_i = 1.00$ — must always hold.
- ☐ **Permeability values to memorise:** 0.95 (passenger), 0.70 (crew), 0.85 (machinery), 0.60 (stores/voids).
- ☐ **Survival factor $s_i = 0$** if final waterline exceeds margin line, heel > 20°, trim > 10°, or residual GM is negative.
- ☐ **Survival factor $s_i = 1$** for an intact-condition or no-flooding baseline.
- ☐ **The designer can only influence A** (via bulkhead positions, permeability, and compartment size); R is regulatory.
- ☐ **More bulkheads = smaller compartments = better A = lower Fs** (safer).
- ☐ **Longer ships and more passengers = higher R** (stricter target).
- ☐ **Applicable only to passenger ships** — cargo ships use the older *floodable length* deterministic method.
- ☐ **Regulatory source:** SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 7 (1960, refined 1974 / 1988 / 2008 IS Code).
- ☐ **Verification agencies:** Classification societies (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV) certify the calculation.
- ☐ **Probability of flooding $p_i$** is proportional to the damage length that can breach compartment *i*.
- ☐ **Mid-body compartments** have higher $p_i$ than end compartments (longer damage runs include them more often).
- ☐ **Final exam trick:** If given "ship is not compliant," the question is asking how to **rearrange bulkheads** or **reduce permeability** to raise A.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permissible Length
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Permissible Length

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

**Permissible Length (L_p)** is the maximum length of a watertight compartment (or a group of consecutive compartments) that may be flooded by the sea, under prescribed damage conditions, without the resultant waterplane submerging the **margin line** of the ship. It is the cornerstone of the SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) damage-stability framework and is directly governed by the **Factor of Subdivision (s)** and the **Criterion of Service Number** applicable to the vessel.

In mathematical form, it is expressed as:

$$L_{p} \;=\; F \cdot L_{s}$$

where $F$ is the **permissible-length factor** (a non-dimensional multiplier derived from IMO curves) and $L_s$ is the **subdivision length** of the ship.

## Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Watertight Building**
> Imagine a long apartment block standing on a flood plain. To prevent total flooding, the architect inserts sealed firewalls dividing the block into cells. *Permissible Length* is simply the **maximum size of one such cell** that can be completely submerged without the building as a whole going under. If a single cell is too long, too much water enters and the structure tilts beyond recovery. The ship's bulkheads play the same role as these firewalls.

The longer the permissible length, the fewer bulkheads are required (cheaper, more cargo space). The shorter it is, the safer the ship (more bulkheads, more robust subdivision). The IMO sets the rules balancing safety and economics.

## Key Definitions (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Margin Line** — A line drawn on the side plating, parallel to the bulkhead deck, at a distance of **76 mm (3 inches)** below the uppermost continuous deck at side. A damaged ship must remain *above* this line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subdivision Length (L_s)** — The length of the ship measured at a waterline located at **0.85 D** (where $D$ is moulded depth) above the moulded baseline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Permeability (μ)** — The ratio of the volume of water a space can hold to its total internal volume. Typical values: passenger spaces 0.95, machinery 0.85, cargo (stores) 0.60.

> [!NOTE]
> **Factor of Subdivision (s)** — A non-dimensional index, between 0 and 1, representing the *level* of subdivision achieved. Lower $s$ → heavier subdivision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criterion of Service Number (C_n)** — A SOLAS standard (Criterion 1, 2, 3 …) prescribing how many adjacent compartments must survive flooding simultaneously without submerging the margin line.

## Syllabus Significance

This topic directly enables **CO3 (Apply)** and **CO4 (Analyse)** of Module 3 — students must be able to compute permissible length, compare it with actual compartment lengths, and recommend whether a subdivision scheme satisfies international regulations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Why Permissible Length Exists — The Underlying Logic

The IMO's philosophy is built on a **probability-consequence** model:

- **Probability** of a compartment being breached is roughly proportional to its length (longer compartments are hit more often).
- **Consequence** of flooding is proportional to the *floodable volume* = permeability × cross-sectional area × length.
- The product (probability × consequence) must remain bounded; hence, **long ships carrying many passengers demand finer subdivision (shorter permissible length)**.

A ship fails the regulatory check if any *actual* compartment length exceeds the calculated permissible length.

## Logical Procedure (Why and How)

1. **Establish the design waterline and moulded depth** $D$ of the vessel.
2. **Compute the subdivision length** $L_s$ at the $0.85D$ waterline.
3. **Identify the number of passengers** $N$ (for passenger ships) and the ship's length $L$.
4. **Look up the constant $K$** from the IMO standard table (a function of $L$).
5. **Calculate the Factor of Subdivision $s$** using the SOLAS formula.
6. **Enter the criterion curve** to read off the permissible-length factor $F$.
7. **Compute** $L_p = F \cdot L_s$ — this is the maximum permitted compartment length.
8. **Compare** with actual compartment lengths; subdivide further if necessary.

## Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Naval architects** use permissible length as the *first-pass check* on every new ship design.
- **Classification societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) verify permissible length before issuing a building certificate.
- **Damage-control teams** at sea use pre-calculated permissible-length diagrams for emergency flooding response.

## KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE questions**. Memorise the table and units.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $L_s = L \,\big\vert_{0.85D}$ | Subdivision length at 85 % moulded depth | m |
| 2 | $s_1 = \dfrac{K \cdot N}{N+1}$ | Factor of subdivision (Criterion 1) | — |
| 3 | $K = 0.6 - \dfrac{L-85}{1000}\cdot 0.2$ | Length-dependent constant (85 m $\leq L \leq$ 200 m) | — |
| 4 | $L_p = F \cdot L_s$ | Permissible length of compartment | m |
| 5 | $\mu = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{space}}$ | Permeability (0 to 1) | — |
| 6 | $GG' = \dfrac{\rho \cdot I_T}{\nabla}$ | Virtual loss of GMT due to flooding | m |
| 7 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\rho \cdot e}{GG'}$ | Angle of list after flooding | rad |
| 8 | $GZ_{res} = GZ - GG'\sin\phi$ | Residual righting lever | m |
| 9 | $A_{req} = 0.055\,\text{rad·m}$ | Required residual area (weather criterion) | rad·m |
| 10 | $n = s \cdot N + 1$ | Permissible number of passengers | persons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on LaTeX in tables** — the symbols $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used above to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax (the literal pipe character ` \vert ` would have been interpreted as a column separator).

## Underlying Theory — Factor of Subdivision (Detailed)

For a passenger ship complying with **Criterion 1** (any single compartment may be flooded):

$$s_1 \;=\; \frac{K \cdot N}{N+1}$$

For higher criteria (any $n$ consecutive compartments may be flooded):

$$s_n \;=\; s_1 + (n-1)\cdot \Delta s$$

where $\Delta s$ is the *increment* from the IMO standard table.

The factor $s$ is bounded by $0 < s \leq 1$. A smaller $s$ means a more subdivided (safer) ship. The *criterion number* is read from a curve as the largest $n$ for which the criterion is still satisfied by the existing bulkhead arrangement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Factor of Subdivision (Conceptual)

The SOLAS formula for the factor of subdivision is empirical, but its logic is derived from the trade-off between **subdivision cost** and **safety margin**.

Let $P_i$ be the probability that compartment $i$ is breached and $C_i$ the consequence (loss of buoyancy) if it is flooded.

- $P_i \propto \dfrac{L_i}{L_s}$ — probability scales with compartment length.
- $C_i \propto \mu_i \cdot A_i \cdot L_i$ — consequence scales with floodable volume.

The **acceptable risk** is:

$$\sum_{i} P_i \cdot C_i \;\leq\; \text{Allowable risk (a function of } N \text{)}$$

After substituting and normalising against a reference ship, the SOLAS committee arrived at the working equation:

$$s_1 \;=\; \frac{K \cdot N}{N+1}$$

The constant $K$ collapses all the geometric and probabilistic variables into a single length-dependent number that the design engineer can read off a regulatory table.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Permissible Length (Operational)

Given $s_1$ from §3.1, the IMO criterion curve provides the permissible-length factor $F$ such that the **criterion is satisfied if and only if**:

$$L_{p} \;\geq\; L_{\text{compartment}}$$

That is, the actual compartment length must not exceed $L_p$. Substituting:

$$L_{p} \;=\; F(s_1,\,L) \cdot L_s$$

In a typical KTU problem, the value of $F$ is *given* in the question (or read from a tabulated IMO curve), so the student only needs to:

1. Compute $K$ from $L$.
2. Compute $s_1$ from $K$ and $N$.
3. Look up / take $F$ from the curve.
4. Multiply by $L_s$.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — KTU 2024 Pattern (14-Mark Style)**

**Given Data**

A passenger vessel has the following particulars:

- Length between perpendiculars: $L = 150 \text{ m}$
- Moulded depth: $D = 14.0 \text{ m}$
- Moulded draft: $T = 9.0 \text{ m}$
- Number of passengers: $N = 400$
- Permeability of the side compartments: $\mu = 0.65$
- A central compartment of length $l = 38 \text{ m}$ at midship is to be assessed.
- From the IMO criterion curve at $s = 0.585$, the permissible-length factor is given as $F = 0.575$.

**Required**

1. Determine the subdivision length $L_s$.
2. Calculate the factor of subdivision $s_1$.
3. Compute the permissible length of the compartment.
4. Verify whether the actual 38 m compartment satisfies Criterion 1.

**Solution — Step by Step**

**Step 1 — Subdivision Length $L_s$**

The subdivision waterline is at $0.85 D$ above the baseline:

$$z = 0.85 \times D = 0.85 \times 14.0 = 11.90 \text{ m above baseline}$$

Since the design draft $T = 9.0 \text{ m} < 11.90 \text{ m}$, the ship is *under-filled* at this waterline; we need the **length at this waterline from the ship's lines plan / offset table**. For this KTU problem, assume the value is provided as:

$$L_s = 142.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K$**

Since $L = 150 \text{ m}$ lies between 85 m and 200 m, use the standard SOLAS formula:

$$K = 0.6 - \frac{L - 85}{1000} \times 0.2$$

Substituting $L = 150$:

$$K = 0.6 - \frac{150 - 85}{1000} \times 0.2$$

$$K = 0.6 - \frac{65}{1000} \times 0.2$$

$$K = 0.6 - 0.013$$

$$K = 0.587$$

**Step 3 — Compute Factor of Subdivision $s_1$**

Using the SOLAS Criterion 1 formula:

$$s_1 = \frac{K \cdot N}{N + 1}$$

$$s_1 = \frac{0.587 \times 400}{400 + 1}$$

$$s_1 = \frac{234.8}{401}$$

$$s_1 = 0.5855$$

**Step 4 — Compute Permissible Length $L_p$**

Using the IMO curve value $F = 0.575$ (read at $s_1 = 0.5855$):

$$L_p = F \cdot L_s = 0.575 \times 142.0$$

$$L_p = 81.65 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Verification**

Compare with the actual compartment length:

$$L_{\text{actual}} = 38 \text{ m} \;<\; L_p = 81.65 \text{ m}$$

$$\therefore \text{The 38 m central compartment } \textbf{ SATISFIES } \text{ Criterion 1.} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Bonus Check (List Angle after flooding, optional)**

For a side compartment of width $b = 8 \text{ m}$ and permeability $\mu = 0.65$:

- Flooded volume: $V_f = \mu \cdot b \cdot l \cdot (\text{compartment height}) = 0.65 \times 8 \times 38 \times 8 \approx 1\,581 \text{ m}^3$
- Mass of floodwater (seawater $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$): $m_f = 1.025 \times 1\,581 = 1\,621 \text{ t}$
- Eccentricity $e = 4 \text{ m}$ (half-width)
- $GG' = \rho \cdot I_T / \nabla$; for a typical waterplane moment of inertia $I_T = 9 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^4$ and $\nabla = 24\,000 \text{ m}^3$: $GG' = (1.025 \times 9 \times 10^5) / 24\,000 \approx 38.4 \text{ m}$
- $\tan\theta = (\rho \cdot e) / GG' = (1.025 \times 4) / 38.4 = 0.1068$
- $\theta \approx 6.1^{\circ}$ — well within the safe limit of $15^{\circ}$–$20^{\circ}$. $\checkmark$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison of Methods

| Method | When Used | Output |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| Factor-of-Subdivision ($s_1 = KN/(N+1)$) | Passenger ships, Criterion 1 | $s_1$ value |
| Permissible-Length Curve ($L_p = F L_s$) | Initial design check | Max compartment length |
| Floodable-Length Curve (graphical) | Final design / damage cases | Max floodable length at station $x$ |
| Probabilistic ($R = \sum P_i C_i$) | Risk-based design (advanced) | Risk index $R$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip** — When a problem gives you $F$ directly, skip the curve-reading steps. When $F$ is **not** given, expect the answer to be in terms of $F$ and $L_s$ only.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Procedure for Determining Permissible Length

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ship Design Data] --> B[Compute Subdivision Length Ls at 0.85D]
    B --> C{Is it a Passenger Ship?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Identify Number of Passengers N]
    C -- No --> E[Use Cargo-Ship SOLAS Curves]
    D --> F[Compute K from L via Table]
    F --> G[Compute s1 = K*N / N+1]
    G --> H[Read F from IMO Criterion Curve]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Compute Lp = F * Ls]
    I --> J{Actual Compartment Length <= Lp?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Subdivision SATISFIES Criterion 1]
    J -- No --> L[Add Watertight Bulkhead and Re-evaluate]
    L --> F
    K --> M[End: Compliance Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Damage-Stability Assessment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Block]
        A1[Ship Geometry L, B, D, T]
        A2[Compartment Data length, mu]
        A3[Loading Condition]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Block]
        B1[Compute Ls and K]
        B2[Compute s1 and F]
        B3[Compute Lp]
    end

    subgraph CHECK[Validation Block]
        C1{Actual length vs Lp}
        C2[Residual GZ check]
        C3[Margin-line immersion check]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Block]
        D1[Compliance Report]
        D2[Recommended Bulkhead Locations]
    end

    INPUT --> COMPUTE
    COMPUTE --> CHECK
    CHECK --> OUTPUT
    C1 -- Pass --> C2
    C2 -- Pass --> C3
    C3 -- Pass --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Permissible Length Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Port Side] --- SH[Ship Hull]
    SH --- ST[Starboard Side]

    subgraph HULL_INTERNAL[Internal Subdivision]
        B1[Bulkhead 1] --- Z1[Compartment 1 l1]
        Z1 --- B2[Bulkhead 2]
        B2 --- Z2[Compartment 2 l2]
        Z2 --- B3[Bulkhead 3]
        B3 --- Z3[Compartment 3 l3]
    end

    SH -. encloses .-> HULL_INTERNAL

    LP[Permissible Length Lp] -. maximum allowed .-> Z1
    LP -. maximum allowed .-> Z2
    LP -. maximum allowed .-> Z3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation** — Every compartment length $l_i$ must satisfy $l_i \leq L_p$. If *any* compartment is longer than $L_p$, the bulkhead arrangement is non-compliant and additional subdivision is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
> Define the term **Permissible Length** of a compartment. State its significance in ship subdivision as per SOLAS regulations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Permissible Length is the maximum length of a watertight compartment that may be flooded without the resultant waterplane submerging the **margin line** of the ship. It is given by $L_p = F \cdot L_s$, where $F$ is the permissible-length factor (from the IMO criterion curve) and $L_s$ is the subdivision length. It is significant because SOLAS regulations require every actual compartment length to be less than or equal to $L_p$ so that the damaged ship retains adequate residual buoyancy and stability, ensuring the safety of passengers and crew. **(3 marks)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
> What is the **margin line**? Why is it located 76 mm below the bulkhead deck, and what role does it play in damage stability?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The margin line is an **imaginary reference line drawn on the ship's side plating, parallel to the bulkhead deck, 76 mm (3 inches) below the uppermost continuous deck at the side**. It is placed 76 mm below the deck to provide a **safety reserve** against wave action, rolling, and any minor unaccounted volumes of floodwater. In damage stability, the rule is that *the ship must remain afloat with the margin line above the water surface* after flooding of the prescribed compartments. Submersion of the margin line is treated as a **failure of damage-stability criteria** and indicates the subdivision is inadequate. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model]**
>
> **(a) Explain the concept of Factor of Subdivision as applied to passenger ships. Derive the SOLAS formula $s_1 = KN/(N+1)$ and discuss its significance. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) A passenger ship has length $L = 160$ m, subdivision length $L_s = 152$ m and carries $N = 600$ passengers. Using the permissible-length factor $F = 0.55$ obtained from the criterion curve, determine (i) the factor of subdivision, and (ii) the permissible length of a compartment at midship. Verify whether an actual compartment of 45 m length satisfies Criterion 1. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

- Definition of $s$ and its role in subdivision (1 mark).
- Probability–consequence rationale: long compartments are hit more often and admit more water (2 marks).
- Empirical SOLAS derivation: $s_1 = KN/(N+1)$ where $K$ is length-dependent (2 marks).
- Significance: lower $s$ means heavier subdivision and greater safety (1 mark).
- Use in design and classification (1 mark).

The Factor of Subdivision $s$ is a non-dimensional index used by SOLAS to quantify the level of subdivision of a passenger ship. It is defined as the ratio of the *criterion number* achieved by the existing bulkhead arrangement to the *criterion number required* by the regulations. A ship with $s = 1$ is barely subdivided (just one bulkhead for the whole ship), while $s = 0$ is infinitely subdivided (impractical).

The SOLAS empirical formula is:

$$s_1 = \frac{K \cdot N}{N+1}$$

where $K$ is a function of the ship's length $L$ (read from the regulation table) and $N$ is the number of passengers the ship is permitted to carry. The form $KN/(N+1)$ comes from the *asymptotic* condition: as $N \to \infty$ (a very large passenger ship), $s_1 \to K$; and as $N \to 0$ (a cargo ship), $s_1 \to 0$. The formula captures the **diminishing-returns trade-off** between subdivision and passenger count.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute $K$**

$L = 160$ m lies in the 85 m $\leq L \leq$ 200 m range, so:

$$K = 0.6 - \frac{L - 85}{1000} \times 0.2 = 0.6 - \frac{75}{1000} \times 0.2 = 0.6 - 0.015 = 0.585$$

**Step 2 — Compute Factor of Subdivision $s_1$**

$$s_1 = \frac{K \cdot N}{N+1} = \frac{0.585 \times 600}{601} = \frac{351.0}{601} = 0.5840$$

**Step 3 — Compute Permissible Length $L_p$**

Given $F = 0.55$:

$$L_p = F \cdot L_s = 0.55 \times 152 = 83.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Verification**

$$L_{\text{actual}} = 45 \text{ m} \;<\; L_p = 83.6 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the 45 m compartment **satisfies Criterion 1**. ✓

> **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
>
> **(a) Discuss the SOLAS regulations governing the subdivision of passenger ships. Explain the various *Criteria of Service Number* used in damage-stability assessment. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) With the aid of a labelled sketch, describe the construction and use of the **floodable-length curve**. How is the permissible length determined from this curve? (7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

- Historical background: 1914 SOLAS, 1960 revisions, modern probabilistic rules (1 mark).
- The 1960 SOLAS deterministic approach: factor of subdivision and criterion number (2 marks).
- Criterion 1: any *one* compartment may be flooded (2 marks).
- Higher criteria: any *n* consecutive compartments may be flooded; the maximum $n$ the ship can survive is the *criterion number* (1 mark).
- Margin-line rule and required residual GZ area (1 mark).

SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) regulations, originally adopted in 1914 and substantially revised in 1960, prescribe the **subdivision of passenger ships** into watertight compartments separated by watertight bulkheads. The 1960 regulations use a *deterministic* approach based on the **Criterion of Service Number**.

- **Criterion 1** — The ship must remain afloat with the margin line not submerged when *any one* compartment is flooded.
- **Criterion 2** — The ship must satisfy Criterion 1 *and* remain afloat when *any two adjacent* compartments are simultaneously flooded.
- **Criterion 3, 4 …** — Extension to *n* adjacent compartments.

The *maximum* criterion number the ship can satisfy is the **Criterion of Service Number** of the ship, and it must equal or exceed the *required* criterion number set by SOLAS for the ship's length and number of passengers. The Factor of Subdivision $s$ links the *required* and *achieved* criteria.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

- Definition of floodable length (1 mark).
- Method of construction: consider a small compartment $dx$ at station $x$, flood it, find new waterline, check margin-line condition, plot $dx$ vs $x$ (3 marks).
- Shape and use of the curve (2 marks).
- Link to permissible length: $L_p$ read off the curve as the maximum ordinate corresponding to the criterion number (1 mark).

The **floodable-length curve** is a graph of *maximum floodable length* $dx$ (ordinate) against *position along the ship's length* $x$ (abscissa). It is constructed as follows:

1. At each station $x$ along the ship's length, assume a hypothetical compartment of length $dx$ at that location is flooded.
2. Compute the new waterline using buoyancy balance:

$$\nabla_{\text{new}} = \nabla_{\text{old}} + \mu \cdot A_x \cdot dx$$

3. Check the condition that the new waterline does not submerge the margin line.
4. The largest $dx$ satisfying this condition is the **floodable length** at station $x$.
5. Plot $dx$ against $x$ to obtain the floodable-length curve.

**Use:** The permissible length $L_p$ is found by selecting a length segment of the curve whose area is the *required* value, and reading off the maximum compartment length that fits within the segment. Any actual compartment whose length exceeds $L_p$ at its location violates the criterion and must be shortened by adding a bulkhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Using $L$ instead of $L_s$** in the permissible-length formula ($L_p = F L_s$, not $F L$). Many students confuse the two and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 85 % waterline** — subdivision length is at $0.85D$, NOT at design draft. Wrong waterline = wrong $L_s$ = wrong answer.
> 3. **Mixing up $K$ formulas** for different length ranges (85–200 m vs 200–300 m vs 300 m+). Always check the range first.
> 4. **Not stating the units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit-less numerical answers.
> 5. **Confusing the criterion number with the factor of subdivision**. The criterion number is a *count* (1, 2, 3 …); $s$ is a *fraction* (0 to 1). Mixing them up = wrong answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Permissible Length**

- **Definition** — Permissible Length $L_p$ = maximum compartment length that can be flooded without margin-line submergence.
- **Governing equation** — $L_p = F \cdot L_s$.
- **Subdivision length** — at waterline $0.85 D$ above moulded baseline; $L_s = L_{0.85D}$.
- **Margin line** — 76 mm (3 in) below bulkhead deck at side; the *no-submergence* reference.
- **Permeability $\mu$** — passenger 0.95, machinery 0.85, cargo 0.60 (approximate defaults).
- **Factor of subdivision (Criterion 1)** — $s_1 = K N / (N+1)$.
- **$K$ formula (85 m $\leq L \leq$ 200 m)** — $K = 0.6 - (L-85)/1000 \times 0.2$.
- **$K$ formula (200 m $\leq L \leq$ 300 m)** — $K = 0.5 - (L-200)/1000 \times 0.1$.
- **$K$ formula ($L \geq 300$ m)** — $K = 0.45$ (approximately, per SOLAS).
- **Criterion of Service Number** — the largest $n$ such that any $n$ consecutive compartments may be flooded without submerging the margin line.
- **Floodable-length curve** — graphical method: plot $dx$ (max floodable length) vs $x$ (position); $L_p$ is the longest actual compartment that fits below the curve.
- **Compliance check** — for every compartment, $l_{\text{actual}} \leq L_p$ must hold.
- **List angle after flooding** — $\tan\theta = (\rho e) / GG'$; must be $\leq 15^{\circ}$–$20^{\circ}$ typically.
- **Residual GZ area** — must be $\geq 0.055$ rad·m up to $40^{\circ}$ heel (weather criterion).
- **High-yield ratio** — for KTU 2024, ~2 marks per part for definitions + 5–7 marks for the numerical in a 14-mark question.
- **Always include units** in final numerical answers.
- **Always check the length range** before picking the $K$ formula.
- **Use $L_s$ (not $L$)** in the permissible-length formula.
- **Distinguish** between $s$ (a fraction) and criterion number $n$ (an integer).
- **Permeability** of a flooded compartment modifies the buoyancy gain, NOT the formula form.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Permissible Length** | *Stay ship-shape, study smart.* ⚓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curves of Floodable Length and Permissible Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Curves of Floodable Length and Permissible Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Floodable Length (FL):** The maximum length of a watertight compartment (or a portion of a compartment) that, when assumed to be flooded completely, would not submerge the margin line of a ship. It is a function of the longitudinal position of the compartment along the ship's length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permissible Length (PL):** The actual length of a watertight compartment in the ship, as built. It represents the real subdivision length that the naval architect has provided in the design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Design Criterion:** A ship is considered to possess adequate **subdivision** if, for every watertight compartment and every possible damage case, the **Permissible Length is less than or equal to the Floodable Length** (PL ≤ FL).

The **Margin Line** is a reference line drawn on the ship's profile (usually **76 mm or 3 inches** below the uppermost continuous deck at the side). A compartment is *floodable* in length if, when filled with water, the resulting waterline does not rise above this margin line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a long **cork raft floating on a lake**. The raft has a maximum safe waterline (margin line). If you punch a hole in any portion of the raft, water will rush in and the raft will sink a little. If the hole is in the **middle**, only a moderate amount of water enters before the raft reaches the danger line. But if the hole is in a **narrow end** (like the bow tip), even a tiny puncture will cause that end to plunge below water rapidly because the buoyant lift there is already small.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Floodable Length is *how much* damage the ship can tolerate at a given location without sinking. Permissible Length is *how much* compartment length you actually have at that location. The naval architect's job is to make sure **the actual compartment is never longer than the allowed floodable length** — otherwise, that section is a "weak link" in survivability.

In simple English:
- **FL** = "How much length of damage the ship can survive at this position?"
- **PL** = "How much length of compartment did I actually build here?"
- The curves of FL tell the designer: "**Here, no compartment may be longer than this value.**"

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Margin Line Standard (SOLAS):** **76 mm (3 inches)** below the uppermost continuous deck at the side.
- **Permeability (μ):** Volume of water that can enter / total volume of the space (ranges from **0 to 1.0**).
- **Block Coefficient (Cb):** Used in computing the equilibrium waterline after flooding.
- **Standard Ship Density:** Sea water **1.025 t/m³**; Fresh water **1.000 t/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The Curves of Floodable Length are derived from a **statical stability after flooding** problem. The classical analysis proceeds in logical steps:

**Step 1 — Equate Volumes (Loss of Buoyancy = Added Weight)**
When a compartment of length `L_c` and average permeability `μ` floods, the **shaft (tunnel)** of the compartment is filled with water. The added weight must equal the loss of buoyancy due to the ship's sinking by an amount `h`.

**Step 2 — Locate New Waterline**
Using the **Lost Buoyancy (LB) curve** and **Added Weight (AW) curve** of the flooded compartment, the intersection gives the new equilibrium waterline. The condition of safety is that this new waterline stays **at or below the margin line**.

**Step 3 — Convert Length to Floodable Length**
Since the required `L_c` (length of compartment) depends on the position of flooding along the ship, we *reverse* the problem: **given that the final waterline is exactly at the margin line, what is the maximum permissible flooded length at that longitudinal position?** This maximum is the **Floodable Length**.

**Step 4 — Plot Curves**
The Floodable Length is plotted against the longitudinal position (usually from AP or FP) to obtain the **Curve of Floodable Length**. A typical FL curve has a **minimum near amidships** and **rises sharply toward the ends** (because the ends have low buoyancy, so the safe flooding length is small — actually, the FL is *long* amidships and *short* at the ends).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A common misconception is that the FL is *small* amidships. The opposite is true — amidships has the **greatest floodable length** because buoyancy is maximum there, and any single compartment flooding is least destabilizing. The **ends have the shortest floodable lengths**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam critical**. Memorize the variables, units, and the physical meaning of each.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---|-----------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W_{lost} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_{wl} \cdot h$ | Loss of buoyancy when ship sinks by height `h` | N (or t·m/s²) |
| 2 | $W_{added} = \mu \cdot \rho \cdot V_{comp}$ | Added weight of floodwater in compartment | N (or t) |
| 3 | $L_c = \dfrac{A_{wl} \cdot h}{\mu \cdot A_{cs}}$ | Length of compartment that can be flooded to sink by `h` | m |
| 4 | $h = d_f - d_0$ | Change in draft due to flooding | m |
| 5 | $FL = f(x)$ | Floodable length as a function of longitudinal position `x` | m |
| 6 | $PL \le FL$ | Subdivision criterion for survivability | — |
| 7 | $\mu = \dfrac{\text{Volume of water entered}}{\text{Total volume of space}}$ | Permeability (0 for empty/fully packed, 1.0 for empty space) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $F_s = \dfrac{\text{Permissible Length}}{\text{Floodable Length}}$ | Factor of subdivision (must be ≤ 1.0) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $d_f = \dfrac{W_{ship} + W_{flood}}{A_{wl} \cdot \rho}$ | Final mean draft after flooding | m |
| 10 | $V_{comp} = L_c \cdot A_{cs}$ | Volume of compartment (length × cross-sectional area) | m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> The **permeability `μ` is NOT a constant**. It depends on the **use of the space**:
> - **Engine room:** μ = 0.85
> - **Stores, chain lockers, mails:** μ = 0.60
> - **Accommodation spaces:** μ = 0.95
> - **Cargo holds (empty):** μ = 0.95
> - **Cargo holds (full of grain/ore):** μ = 0.60–0.70

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Construction Logic (Bulleted)

- **Identify the load condition** (Light Ship, Loaded, Partial).
- **Compute the waterplane area (A_wl)** and **block coefficient (C_b)** for the intact ship.
- **Define the margin line** and measure the allowable sinkage `h` from the load waterline to the margin line.
- **Choose a longitudinal station** `x` measured from amidships or AP.
- **Iterate the flooding length** `L_c` until the new waterline (LB = AW) just touches the margin line.
- **Record the maximum `L_c`** at that station — this is the **Floodable Length**.
- **Repeat for all stations** from AP to FP.
- **Plot the curve** with `L_c` on the Y-axis and station `x` on the X-axis.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Application in Naval Architecture & Marine Engineering:**
> 1. **Subdivision of passenger ships** is regulated by **SOLAS Chapter II-1** using exactly these curves.
> 2. **Warship survivability** is designed so that loss of any two adjacent compartments does not sink the vessel — directly governed by the PL/FL comparison.
> 3. **Damage stability booklets** issued to the Master use FL curves as a basis for flooding scenarios.
> 4. **Insurance and classification** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) require subdivision indices computed from these curves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equilibrium Condition for a Flooded Compartment

**Problem Setup:** A ship of intact displacement `Δ` floats at draft `d_0`. A compartment of length `L_c`, mean cross-sectional area `A_cs`, and permeability `μ` is suddenly flooded. Find the equilibrium draft `d_f` and the corresponding floodable length if the final waterline must lie at the margin line.

**Derivation:**

The **weight of floodwater added** to the ship is:

$$
W_{added} = \mu \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{comp}
$$

Substituting the compartment volume:

$$
W_{added} = \mu \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot L_c \cdot A_{cs}
$$

The **loss of buoyancy** when the ship sinks by an amount `h` is given by:

$$
W_{lost} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_{wl} \cdot h
$$

Equating for equilibrium (Added Weight = Lost Buoyancy):

$$
\mu \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot L_c \cdot A_{cs} = \rho_{sw} \cdot A_{wl} \cdot h
$$

Cancelling `ρ_sw` from both sides:

$$
\mu \cdot L_c \cdot A_{cs} = A_{wl} \cdot h
$$

Solving for the **floodable length** `L_c`:

$$
L_c = \frac{A_{wl} \cdot h}{\mu \cdot A_{cs}}
$$

This `L_c` is the **Floodable Length at that particular station** when the allowable sinkage is `h`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The floodable length is *directly proportional* to the waterplane area (more buoyancy reserve = longer safe flooding length) and *inversely proportional* to the cross-sectional area of the compartment and its permeability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Position on Floodable Length (Block Coefficient Method)

For a ship of length `L`, beam `B`, and block coefficient `C_b`, with a prismatic compartment near station `x`, the cross-sectional area of the hull at that section is:

$$
A_{cs}(x) = B(x) \cdot T(x) \cdot C_m(x)
$$

where `C_m` is the mid-ship area coefficient and `B(x)`, `T(x)` are local beam and draft.

For a **parallel middle body** (most common simplification), `A_cs` is constant in the middle region. The waterplane area `A_wl` is approximately:

$$
A_{wl} = L \cdot B \cdot C_{wp}
$$

where `C_wp` is the waterplane area coefficient (typically **0.75 to 0.85** for merchant ships).

Substituting into the FL equation:

$$
L_c = \frac{L \cdot B \cdot C_{wp} \cdot h}{\mu \cdot A_{cs}}
$$

To express the dependence on position `x` (measured from amidships), the cross-section of the hull tapers parabolically near the ends. A common parabolic form is:

$$
A_{cs}(x) = A_{cs,\text{mid}} \cdot \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{2x}{L} \right)^2 \right]
$$

Therefore, the **Floodable Length as a function of position** is:

$$
FL(x) = \frac{L \cdot B \cdot C_{wp} \cdot h}{\mu \cdot A_{cs,\text{mid}} \cdot \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{2x}{L} \right)^2 \right]}
$$

**Boundary Behaviour Analysis:**

$$
\text{At } x = 0 \text{ (amidships):} \quad FL_{\max} = \frac{L \cdot B \cdot C_{wp} \cdot h}{\mu \cdot A_{cs,\text{mid}}}
$$

$$
\text{At } x = \frac{L}{2} \text{ (perpendiculars):} \quad FL \to \infty \text{ (since } A_{cs} \to 0\text{)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The apparent infinite FL at the ends is **physically meaningless** because the FL must also satisfy the condition that the **permeability-weighted longitudinal moment of the flooding does not exceed the residual metacentric height** (stability after flooding check). The "ends" are limited by **GM** and **range of stability**, not by sinkage.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Criterion for Permissible Length Compliance

A ship has a series of watertight compartments with actual lengths `L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n` at positions `x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n`. The ship is *correctly subdivided* if and only if:

$$
\forall i: \quad L_i \le FL(x_i)
$$

In practice, naval architects use the **Factor of Subdivision**:

$$
F_s(i) = \frac{L_i}{FL(x_i)} \le 1.0
$$

For **SOLAS-compliant passenger ships**, the **Index of Subdivision R** must satisfy:

$$
R = \sum \left( 1 - F_s \right) \ge R_{required}
$$

where `R_required` depends on ship size and number of persons on board.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Given Data:**
- Ship length `L = 120 m`
- Beam `B = 18 m`
- Mean draft `d_0 = 6.0 m`
- Margin line above load waterline `h_{allow} = 0.20 m`
- Compartment permeability `μ = 0.85` (engine room)
- Waterplane area coefficient `C_{wp} = 0.78`
- Mid-section area `A_{cs,\text{mid}} = 95 m²`

**Step 1 — Compute Waterplane Area:**

$$
A_{wl} = L \cdot B \cdot C_{wp} = 120 \cdot 18 \cdot 0.78
$$

$$
A_{wl} = 1684.8 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FL at Amidships (x = 0):**

$$
FL(0) = \frac{A_{wl} \cdot h_{allow}}{\mu \cdot A_{cs,\text{mid}}}
$$

$$
FL(0) = \frac{1684.8 \cdot 0.20}{0.85 \cdot 95}
$$

$$
FL(0) = \frac{336.96}{80.75} = 4.17 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute FL at x = 30 m off amidships:**

$$
\left( \frac{2x}{L} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{60}{120} \right)^2 = 0.25
$$

$$
A_{cs}(30) = 95 \cdot (1 - 0.25) = 71.25 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
FL(30) = \frac{1684.8 \cdot 0.20}{0.85 \cdot 71.25} = \frac{336.96}{60.56} = 5.56 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compile the Analytical Matrix:**

| Station `x` (m) | `A_cs(x)` (m²) | `FL(x)` (m) | Remarks |
|----------------|---------------|------------|---------|
| 0 (amidships) | 95.00 | **4.17** | Minimum FL if end-effects ignored |
| 15 | 84.69 | 4.68 | |
| 30 | 71.25 | 5.56 | |
| 45 | 47.50 | 8.35 | |
| 55 | 14.06 | 28.18 | End region — limited by stability |
| 60 (end) | 0.00 | ∞ | Theoretically infinite; physically limited |

**Step 5 — Verification by Subdivision:**
If the engine room (μ = 0.85) has actual length `L_1 = 20 m` and is positioned at `x = 0`, then:

$$
F_s = \frac{L_1}{FL(0)} = \frac{20}{4.17} = 4.80 \quad \gg 1.0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The ship FAILS the subdivision criterion.** The engine room is 4.8 times longer than the floodable length. The vessel is not survivable if this compartment is breached. Solution: introduce a **watertight bulkhead** to subdivide the engine room.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Floodable Length Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Intact Ship at Load Draft] --> B[Define Margin Line 76mm below Deck]
    B --> C[Compute Allowable Sinkage h]
    C --> D[Choose Longitudinal Station x]
    D --> E[Compute Waterplane Area A_wl]
    E --> F[Compute Compartment Cross-Section A_cs at x]
    F --> G[Apply Permeability mu for Space Type]
    G --> H[Calculate FL = A_wl x h / mu x A_cs]
    H --> I{FL Meets Stability Criterion?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Record FL at Station x]
    I -- No --> K[Apply GM / Range Correction]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Move to Next Station]
    L --> M{All Stations Covered?}
    M -- No --> D
    M -- Yes --> N[Plot Curve FL vs x]
    N --> O[Overlay Permissible Lengths]
    O --> P{PL <= FL Everywhere?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Ship Subdivision ACCEPTED]
    P -- No --> R[Add Watertight Bulkheads]
    R --> S[Re-evaluate Subdivision]
    S --> O
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Relationship Between FL and PL

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intact Ship Geometry] --> B[Block Coefficient Cb]
    A --> C[Waterplane Coefficient Cwp]
    B --> D[Cross Section A_cs]
    C --> E[Waterplane Area A_wl]
    D --> F[FL Calculation Engine]
    E --> F
    G[Permeability mu Database] --> F
    H[Margin Line Position] --> F
    F --> I[Floodable Length Curve]
    J[Designer Compartment Layout] --> K[Permissible Length Curve]
    I --> L[Subdivision Comparator]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Compliant?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Approved Design]
    M -- No --> O[Redesign Compartments]
    O --> J
```

## 4.3 Typical Shape of a Floodable Length Curve (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[AP - 0m] --> P2[Station 15m: FL rises from 0]
    P2 --> P3[Station 30m: FL increases rapidly]
    P3 --> P4[Station 45m: FL approaches maximum]
    P4 --> P5[Amidships 60m: FL peak - widest safe compartment]
    P5 --> P6[Station 75m: FL decreases symmetrically]
    P6 --> P7[Station 90m: FL low - narrow safe zone]
    P7 --> P8[FP 120m: FL minimum near zero]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reading:** A **long horizontal section** of the FL curve (near amidships) indicates the ship can tolerate *long* compartments there. A **steep drop** near the ends means compartments there must be **very short** for survivability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define **Floodable Length (FL)** and **Permissible Length (PL)**. State the fundamental criterion that connects them for ship subdivision.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Floodable Length (FL):** The maximum length of a watertight compartment, situated at a given longitudinal position, which when completely flooded (considering the space's permeability) will not submerge the margin line of the ship.
>
> **Permissible Length (PL):** The actual length of a watertight compartment as designed in the ship.
>
> **Fundamental Criterion:** For the ship to be considered properly subdivided, the **Permissible Length of any watertight compartment must not exceed the Floodable Length at that station**, i.e., **PL ≤ FL** at every position along the ship's length.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the **Margin Line**? State its standard distance from the uppermost continuous deck as per SOLAS regulations.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Margin Line** is an imaginary reference line drawn on the ship's profile, parallel to the uppermost continuous deck, used to assess survivability after flooding. As per **SOLAS Chapter II-1 Regulation 2**, the margin line is drawn **76 mm (3 inches)** below the uppermost continuous deck at the side. A compartment is *floodable in length* if, when completely flooded, the resulting equilibrium waterline lies **at or below** this margin line.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A ship of length **150 m**, beam **20 m**, mean draft **6.5 m**, has a watertight compartment positioned at amidships with **permeability μ = 0.95**. The waterplane area coefficient is **0.80** and the mid-ship cross-sectional area is **110 m²**. The margin line is **0.18 m** above the load waterline. Calculate the **Floodable Length** at this station. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **shape and significance** of the Curve of Floodable Length for a typical merchant ship. Why is the Floodable Length *minimum* near amidships for end-flooded compartments? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a)**

**Given:**
- L = 150 m, B = 20 m, d₀ = 6.5 m
- μ = 0.95, C_wp = 0.80, A_cs,mid = 110 m²
- h_allow = 0.18 m

**Step 1 — Waterplane Area:**

$$
A_{wl} = L \cdot B \cdot C_{wp} = 150 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.80 = 2400 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Floodable Length at Amidships (x = 0):**

$$
FL(0) = \frac{A_{wl} \cdot h_{allow}}{\mu \cdot A_{cs,\text{mid}}}
$$

$$
FL(0) = \frac{2400 \cdot 0.18}{0.95 \cdot 110} = \frac{432.0}{104.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{FL(0) = 4.13 \; \text{m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
A compartment of **4.13 m** or less located at amidships can be completely flooded without submerging the margin line. Any longer compartment at this position is unsafe unless subdivided.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b)**

**Shape of the FL Curve:**

The Curve of Floodable Length is plotted with the **ship's length** (or station number) on the abscissa and the **Floodable Length** on the ordinate. For a typical merchant ship, the curve has the following characteristics:

- **At amidships (x = 0):** The FL attains a **minimum value** because a compartment here, when flooded, removes the most buoyancy from the ship (the cross-section is largest), so the safe flooding length is shortest.
- **Moving toward the ends:** The cross-sectional area of the hull decreases, so a given length of compartment contains less volume — the **FL increases** and may even reach very high values near the ends.
- **Near the perpendiculars:** The curve is **steeply rising** and is **practically limited by residual GM and range of stability**, not by sinkage to the margin line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Common Mistake):** Students often think FL is *maximum* at amidships. The opposite is true for an amidships-flooded compartment — the **FL is minimum** there because the buoyant volume lost per metre of flooding is maximum. However, for an *end-flooded* compartment, the FL is large near amidships and small at the ends. The curve shape depends on **which station is being considered as the position of flooding**.

**Significance:**
1. Determines the **maximum allowable compartment length** at every station.
2. Forms the basis of **SOLAS subdivision rules** for passenger ships.
3. Guides the placement of **watertight bulkheads**.
4. Used in computing the **Index of Subdivision R**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Floodable Length** in terms of waterplane area, allowable sinkage, permeability, and cross-sectional area of the compartment. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier of length **180 m**, beam **24 m**, draft **8.0 m**, has an **engine room** (μ = 0.85) at the stern of **length 25 m**, and a **cargo hold** (μ = 0.70) amidships of **length 45 m**. Waterplane area is **3200 m²** and mid-section area is **140 m²**. Margin line is **0.25 m** above load waterline. The hull tapers parabolically. Determine the **factor of subdivision** for both compartments and comment on survivability. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a)**

**Derivation of FL Expression:**

Consider a ship floating in still water at mean draft `d_0`. A watertight compartment of length `L_c`, uniform cross-sectional area `A_cs`, and permeability `μ` is suddenly flooded.

**Assumptions:**
1. Ship remains upright (no trim or heel changes considered in basic formula).
2. Permeability `μ` is uniform throughout the compartment.
3. Waterplane area `A_wl` remains constant during the small sinkage `h` (valid for `h << d_0`).
4. Flooding is instantaneous and the compartment is completely filled.
5. The compartment is open to the sea (no internal air pockets).

**Derivation Steps:**

*Step 1 — Weight of Floodwater Added:*

$$
W_{added} = \mu \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L_c \cdot A_{cs}
$$

*Step 2 — Loss of Buoyancy Due to Sinkage h:*

$$
W_{lost} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_{wl} \cdot h
$$

*Step 3 — Equating for Equilibrium:*

$$
\mu \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L_c \cdot A_{cs} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_{wl} \cdot h
$$

*Step 4 — Cancelling Common Factors:*

$$
\mu \cdot L_c \cdot A_{cs} = A_{wl} \cdot h
$$

*Step 5 — Solving for L_c:*

$$
\boxed{FL = L_c = \frac{A_{wl} \cdot h}{\mu \cdot A_{cs}}}
$$

This is the **Floodable Length** of the compartment, valid at any longitudinal station where the cross-sectional area is `A_cs` and the allowable sinkage is `h`.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b)**

**Given:**
- L = 180 m, B = 24 m, d₀ = 8.0 m
- Engine room: μ₁ = 0.85, L_PL,1 = 25 m (at stern)
- Cargo hold: μ₂ = 0.70, L_PL,2 = 45 m (amidships)
- A_wl = 3200 m², A_cs,mid = 140 m²
- h_allow = 0.25 m

**Step 1 — FL at Amidships (x = 0):**

$$
FL(0) = \frac{A_{wl} \cdot h}{\mu_2 \cdot A_{cs,\text{mid}}} = \frac{3200 \cdot 0.25}{0.70 \cdot 140}
$$

$$
FL(0) = \frac{800.0}{98.0} = 8.16 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — FL at Stern (Assuming Parabolic Taper):**
At the stern (x ≈ 80 m off amidships, so 2x/L ≈ 0.89):

$$
\left( \frac{2x}{L} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{160}{180} \right)^2 = 0.79
$$

$$
A_{cs}(\text{stern}) = 140 \cdot (1 - 0.79) = 29.4 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
FL(\text{stern}) = \frac{3200 \cdot 0.25}{0.85 \cdot 29.4} = \frac{800.0}{24.99} = 32.01 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Subdivision:**

> For **Engine Room** at stern:

$$
F_{s,1} = \frac{L_{PL,1}}{FL(\text{stern})} = \frac{25}{32.01} = 0.78
$$

Since `F_s,1 = 0.78 < 1.0`, the engine room **PASSES** the subdivision criterion. ✓

> For **Cargo Hold** at amidships:

$$
F_{s,2} = \frac{L_{PL,2}}{FL(0)} = \frac{45}{8.16} = 5.51
$$

Since `F_s,2 = 5.51 >> 1.0`, the cargo hold **FAILS** the criterion. ✗

**Step 4 — Survivability Comment:**

> [!WARNING]
> The cargo hold at amidships is **5.51 times longer** than the floodable length. This is a **critical survivability failure** — flooding this single compartment would submerge the margin line. The bulk carrier requires:
> 1. **Additional watertight bulkheads** to subdivide the 45 m cargo hold into smaller sections (each ≤ 8.16 m).
> 2. At least **6 sub-compartments** (8.16 × 6 = 48.96 m > 45 m) would be required to meet the criterion.
> 3. The engine room is safe and requires no further subdivision.

**Conclusion:** The ship is **NOT survivable in its current form** and must be redesigned with additional bulkheads amidships.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in A_wl and A_cs** — partial credit only if units are wrong.
> 2. **Using displacement instead of waterplane area** — a frequent conceptual error.
> 3. **Not cancelling ρ_sw (sea water density)** in derivation — examiner may deduct 0.5 marks for incomplete simplification.
> 4. **Confusing PL and FL** — always write out which is which; "compartment can be flooded if its length is less than FL" is a recurring mark-loser area.
> 5. **Ignoring permeability values** — must use the correct μ for the space type (engine room 0.85, cargo 0.70, accommodation 0.95).
> 6. **Forgetting to comment on survivability** after the calculation — KTU 2024 scheme rewards *interpretive* answers over raw computation.
> 7. **Plotting FL curve in wrong orientation** — station (x) on X-axis, FL on Y-axis; reversal loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Curves of FL and PL**

- **Margin Line** is **76 mm (3 in)** below the uppermost continuous deck (SOLAS).
- **Floodable Length (FL)** = maximum length of compartment that can be flooded at station `x` without submerging the margin line.
- **Permissible Length (PL)** = actual length of compartment as designed.
- **Survivability Criterion:** `PL ≤ FL` at every station.
- **Master Formula:**

$$
\boxed{FL = \frac{A_{wl} \cdot h}{\mu \cdot A_{cs}}}
$$

- **Position Effect:** FL is **minimum at amidships** (for a flooding at that position) and **maximum at the ends** (cross-section is small, so less volume is flooded per metre of length).
- **End Region:** Theoretically infinite FL is curtailed by the **residual GM and range of stability** criterion.
- **Permeability values** (must memorize): Engine Room = **0.85**, Cargo (empty) = **0.95**, Cargo (bulk grain) = **0.70**, Stores = **0.60**, Accommodation = **0.95**.
- **Factor of Subdivision** `F_s = PL / FL ≤ 1.0` — violation means redesign with additional bulkheads.
- **SOLAS Index R:** For passenger ships, `R = Σ(1 − F_s) ≥ R_required`; failure triggers redesign.
- **Waterplane Area A_wl = L · B · C_wp** is a key input; use C_wp ≈ 0.75 to 0.85.
- **Margin line check** is the *sinkage* limit; **GM check** is the *stability* limit — both must be satisfied.
- **The ship is correctly subdivided** when ALL compartments satisfy `PL ≤ FL` AND residual GM is positive after flooding.
- **Taper Effect:** Parabolic hull sections have `A_cs(x) = A_{cs,mid} [1 − (2x/L)²]`.
- **Engine room and passenger ships** use lower FL (μ high) → shorter permissible compartments → more bulkheads.
- **Practical Tip for Board Exam:** Always show **3–4 stations** in the FL curve table, then plot a sketch showing the characteristic U-shape (high at ends, low amidships for flooding-at-position curves).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compartment Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compartment Standard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Compartment Standard** (also referred to as the **Standard of Subdivision**) is a damage-stability regulation prescribed by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** for **passenger ships**. It defines the *required degree of watertight subdivision* of a vessel so that, after a defined flooding casualty, the ship will not capsize and will retain a survivable residual stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The compartment standard is fundamentally a **probabilistic damage-stability criterion** that links the *number of persons carried* and the *ship's length* to the *maximum permissible length of a floodable compartment*.

The underlying regulatory philosophy is given by the inequality:

$$A \geq R$$

where:
- **A** = **Attained Subdivision Index** (calculated from the actual subdivision arrangement of the ship).
- **R** = **Required Subdivision Index** (a function of the ship's length and the number of persons carried).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Watertight Honeycomb:** Imagine a large rectangular sponge (the hull) which is full of water. If you punch a single hole, the entire sponge fills with water and sinks. Now, imagine the same sponge is internally divided into many smaller, sealed honeycomb cells (the watertight compartments). Puncturing one cell only floods that small region; the remaining cells continue to keep the sponge afloat. The **Compartment Standard** is essentially the engineering *rulebook* that tells the naval architect **how many internal walls (bulkheads)**, **how high they must reach**, and **how far apart they must be placed** so that even the worst-case hull puncture leaves the ship floating and stable.

The whole regulation is a safety *trade-off*: more compartments ⇒ greater subdivision ⇒ heavier ship and reduced cargo space; too few compartments ⇒ catastrophic flooding risk. The standard mathematically balances these concerns.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The following fixed design constants are universally used:

| Parameter | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Margin Line** | **76 mm (3 inches)** | Distance below the bulkhead deck within which no progressive flooding may occur. |
| **Permeability (μ)** | **0.95** | Passenger spaces, accommodation, etc. |
| | **0.85** | Machinery spaces |
| | **0.60** | Cargo holds |
| | **0.30** | Double bottom, fuel tanks, etc. |
| **Damage Length (ℓₐ)** | Function of L | Hypothetical flooded zone length |
| **Damage Height** | **Upward unlimited** | Extends to the bulkhead deck |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Compartment Standard

The regulation works in **five logical steps** (each must be fully understood for KTU):

1. **Determine the ship length L** (rule length) and the total **number of persons N** the ship is permitted to carry.
2. **Compute the Criterion of Service Numeral R** from N and L.
3. **Compute the Permissible Length (Lₛ)** of any single floodable compartment as a function of the residual stability.
4. **Compute the Attained Subdivision Index (A)** by integrating the actual subdivision along the ship's length, weighted by a probability function (s-factor).
5. **Verify that A ≥ R**; if not, additional bulkheads must be added.

## 2.2 The Criterion of Service Numeral (R)

This is a pure regulatory value that scales the *risk* with the *consequence*:

$$R = \frac{N + L}{100}$$

where:
- **N** = Total number of persons the ship is certified to carry (passengers + crew).
- **L** = Rule length of the ship in **metres** (measured per SOLAS Reg. II-1/2.2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by 100?** The factor 100 was selected historically so that, for a ship of L = 100 m carrying N = 100 persons, R = 2.0 — a tractable index that historically was used to set the number of required compartments (1, 2, 3, or 4 watertight bulkheads depending on R).

## 2.3 The Factor of Subdivision (F)

The factor of subdivision is what *transforms* the standard permissible length (Lₛ) into the *permissible length* for a particular ship:

$$F = \frac{N}{100}$$

For the more precise SOLAS formulation (post-1990 amendments), it becomes:

$$F = 0.5 + \frac{N}{1000}$$

applied to R, and the **permissible length** of any two adjacent compartments is:

$$L_{\text{perm}} = L_s \times F$$

## 2.4 The Permissible Length Curve (Floodable Length)

The **floodable length** at any point along the ship is computed by *reverse-damaging* the ship from that point: open all spaces progressively until the residual stability factors (residual GM, range of positive righting lever, residual freeboard) fall to their minimum allowable values. The maximum compartment length that can be flooded at that position is the **floodable length**.

The SOLAS standard residual stability (for a passenger ship) requires:

| Quantity | Minimum Value |
|---|---|
| **Residual GM** | $\geq 0.05 \text{ m}$ |
| **Range of positive righting lever ($\theta$ range)** | $\geq 7^\circ$ |
| **Residual freeboard to margin line** | $\geq 0$ (must not submerge) |
| **Maximum GZ in range** | $\geq 0.10 \text{ m}$ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Compartment Standard**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{N+L}{100}$ | Criterion of Service Numeral | dimensionless |
| 2 | $F = \dfrac{N}{100}$ | Factor of Subdivision (basic) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $L_{\text{perm}} = L_s \times F$ | Permissible length of two adjacent compartments | m |
| 4 | $A = \displaystyle \sum_{i} P_i \cdot s_i$ | Attained Subdivision Index | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A \geq R$ | Compliance check | — |
| 6 | $A = \displaystyle \int_{0}^{L} P(x)\cdot s(x)\, dx$ | Continuous-form subdivision integral | — |
| 7 | $s_i = f(N,\ell_i)$ | Probability of survival after flooding zone *i* | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\ell_a = \left(\dfrac{L}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot F_{\text{max}}$ | Damage length assessment (for small ships) | m |
| 9 | $GM_{\text{residual}} = GM - \dfrac{\text{loss of VCB due to flood}}{\text{displacement factor}}$ | Residual metacentric height | m |
| 10 | Margin line = **76 mm** below bulkhead deck | Reference line for residual freeboard | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> In exam answers, **always** state the units of L (metres) and the meaning of N (the *total number of persons* the ship is certified to carry, **not** only passengers). Mixing up these is a common KTU valuation error.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The compartment standard is not merely an academic exercise — it is a **legal safety obligation** enforced by every Flag State and Port State Control under SOLAS. Naval architects use this standard to:

- Decide the **number and position of transverse watertight bulkheads** during the early design stage.
- Optimize the **trade-off between ship safety and deadweight/cargo capacity**.
- Justify any deviation (e.g., alternative design arrangements under SOLAS Reg. II-1/13.10) using direct numerical simulation.
- Verify **passenger ship survivability** after any casualty.

It also serves as a benchmark in *RoPax ferries*, *cruise liners*, and *warship damage-control doctrine* where the same mathematical philosophy underpins the survivability grid.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Criterion of Service Numeral

The criterion of service numeral **R** expresses the *severity* of casualty expected on a passenger ship. It is built on the empirical observation that the *risk of foundering* scales with the **number of people on board** (a higher N ⇒ more risk to human life) and the **length of the ship** (a longer L ⇒ more chance of a large casualty zone).

**Step 1 — Risk component due to persons carried (N):**
Empirically, when a ship carries N persons, the casualty's fatality potential grows roughly linearly with N.

$$\text{Risk}_1 \propto N$$

**Step 2 — Risk component due to ship length (L):**
A longer ship has a proportionally larger probability of being hit at a critical point along its length, giving:

$$\text{Risk}_2 \propto L$$

**Step 3 — Combine and normalize:**
Summing the two linearly independent risk components and dividing by a normalization constant (100) gives the dimensionless criterion of service numeral:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{N+L}{100}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *purely regulatory* number — it has no physical SI units. The factor **100** is a historical calibration constant chosen so that, for a "standard" 100 m ship with 100 persons, R = 2.0.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Permissible Length Formula

The **permissible length** $L_{\text{perm}}$ of any two adjacent compartments is the longest length such that, if both compartments are simultaneously flooded (the worst permitted casualty), the ship still satisfies the residual-stability requirements.

**Step 1 — Compute the standard permissible length (Lₛ):**
For a flood of length $\ell$ at position *x*, compute residual $GZ$ curve, residual freeboard, residual GM, and range. The maximum $\ell$ for which *all* criteria are met gives Lₛ.

**Step 2 — Apply the factor of subdivision (F):**
A ship carrying more persons (larger N) has a higher R and must therefore be *more* finely subdivided. Mathematically, the allowable compartment length is *inversely* proportional to F; this is implemented as:

$$L_{\text{perm}} = \frac{L_s}{F}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the basic definition of F = N/100:**

$$L_{\text{perm}} = \frac{100\, L_s}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Converting to the SOLAS exact form** (R = (N+L)/100 and F = R/L is sometimes used) the full expression is:

$$L_{\text{perm}} = L_s \times \frac{100}{N + L}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Complete KTU Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A passenger ship of rule length **L = 180 m** is certified to carry **N = 2400 persons**. Compute R, the basic F, the permissible length, and the number of transverse bulkheads required. Standard permissible length Lₛ = 30 m (assume).

**Step 1 — Criterion of Service Numeral R:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{N+L}{100} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2400 + 180}{100} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2580}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 25.8
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Factor of Subdivision F (basic form):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{N}{100} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2400}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 24.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Permissible length of two adjacent compartments:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{perm}} &= L_s \times F \\[4pt]
&= 30 \times 24.0 \\[4pt]
&= 720 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The result Lₚₑᵣₘ = 720 m is *larger* than the ship's length, which means *any* pair of adjacent compartments along the 180 m length is *permitted* under this (assumed) Lₛ. In real SOLAS calculations, the attained subdivision must still be computed and the inequality A ≥ R verified. For exam purposes, the point being tested is the algebraic sequence above.

**Step 4 — Estimated number of bulkheads:**
For R = 25.8, the historical guidance suggests an average compartment length of about L/R = 180/25.8 ≈ 7 m, which translates to approximately:

$$\text{Number of bulkheads} \approx \frac{L}{L_{\text{avg}}} - 1 = \frac{180}{7} - 1 \approx 25 \text{ bulkheads}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Attained Subdivision Index A

The attained subdivision index is computed in a *continuous* form along the ship length:

**Step 1 — Define a probability of survival function.**
For each damage zone centred at *x* with length $\ell(x)$:

$$p(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if ship survives the damage at } x \\ 0 & \text{if not} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Define the s-factor (probability weighting):**
The s-factor gives the *probability of the damage occurring* at any point:

$$s(x) = f\!\left(\frac{\ell(x)}{L}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Continuous integration form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \int_{0}^{L} p(x)\cdot s(x)\, dx \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i \cdot \Delta x_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Discrete summation form used in practice:**

$$A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot s_i$$

where $P_i$ is the *length-probability* of the $i^{\text{th}}$ damage zone and $s_i$ is its survival factor.

**Step 5 — Compliance:**

$$\boxed{A \geq R}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Compartment Standard Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the **step-by-step functional architecture** of the compartment standard calculation, mapping the data flow between each regulatory block as required by KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start: Input Ship Data]) --> input1[/Rule Length L in metres/]
    input1 --> input2[/Total Number of Persons N/]
    input2 --> input3[/Standard Permissible Length Ls from stability curves/]
    
    input1 --> calc1{Calculate R}
    input2 --> calc1
    calc1 -->|R = N + L over 100| valR[/Computed R value/]
    
    input2 --> calc2{Calculate F}
    calc2 -->|F = N over 100| valF[/Computed F value/]
    
    valR --> comp1{R vs A Check}
    valF --> calc3{Calculate Lperm}
    input3 --> calc3
    calc3 -->|Lperm = Ls times F| valLperm[/Permissible Length/]
    
    valLperm --> subdiv1{Attained Subdivision Index A}
    subdiv1 -->|Integral of P and s| valA[/Computed A value/]
    
    valA --> comp1
    comp1 -->|A greater than R| pass1([Ship Meets Compartment Standard])
    comp1 -->|A less than R| fail1([Add Bulkheads or Re-Design])
    fail1 --> calc3
    
    pass1 --> finish1([End: Documentation and Certification])
    fail1 --> finish1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Subsystems of the Standard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input1A[Input Subsystem]
        A1[L: Rule Length]
        A2[N: Persons]
        A3[Ls: Standard Permissible]
    end
    
    subgraph calc1A[Calculation Subsystem]
        B1[R Computation]
        B2[F Computation]
        B3[Lperm Computation]
        B4[A Integral Solver]
    end
    
    subgraph output1A[Output Subsystem]
        C1[R Value]
        C2[F Value]
        C3[Lperm Value]
        C4[A Value]
    end
    
    subgraph check1A[Compliance Subsystem]
        D1{Equality Check A greater than R}
        D2[Pass Certificate]
        D3[Re-Design Loop]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    A1 --> B4
    A2 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    C1 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 -->|Yes| D2
    D1 -->|No| D3
    D3 --> B3
```

## 4.3 Schematic Logic of Damage Stability Evaluation per Compartment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start2([Begin: Choose Compartment i]) --> comp1[Open Watertight Bulkheads to Define Damage Zone]
    comp1 --> comp2[Apply Permeability mu to Each Space]
    comp2 --> comp3[Compute Lost Buoyancy delta V]
    comp3 --> comp4[Compute New Waterline and Draft]
    comp4 --> comp5[Compute Residual GM]
    comp5 --> comp6[Compute Residual GZ Curve]
    comp6 --> comp7{Residual Stability Satisfies SOLAS Limits}
    comp7 -->|Yes| mark1[Compartment Permitted]
    comp7 -->|No| mark2[Compartment Exceeds Floodable Length]
    mark2 --> reduce1[Reduce Compartment Length or Add Bulkhead]
    reduce1 --> comp1
    mark1 --> next1[Move to Next Compartment]
    next1 --> finish2([End: Compartment Standard Verified])
```

---
