# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Inclining Experiment and state the conditions under which it is conducted.**

> **Model Answer**:
> The **inclining experiment** is a standard shipboard procedure carried out to determine the **as-built metacentric height (GM)** and the **vertical position of the centre of gravity (KG)** of a completed vessel. A known test weight (`w`) is moved transversely through a known distance (`y`) to apply a known heeling moment, and the resulting small angle of heel (`θ`) is measured using pendulums or U-tube manometers. From the equilibrium of heeling and righting moments, GM is calculated.
>
> It is conducted:
> 1. On **completion of construction** of every new ship.
> 2. After **major structural modifications** that may alter weight distribution.
> 3. Under the **inclining condition** — the lightest seagoing condition with minimum fuel, water, and no cargo.
> 4. In **calm weather** with all moorings slack so the vessel floats freely.

---

### **Question 2** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q: Why is the free-surface correction applied to the GM obtained from the inclining experiment?**

> **Model Answer**:
> The **free-surface correction** is applied because partially-filled tanks contain liquids (water, fuel oil) with a free surface that is not in contact with the tank top. When the ship heels, the liquid **sloshes** to the low side, effectively **shifting the centre of gravity of the ship upward** and to the low side. This reduces the *true* metacentric height of the vessel. Since the GM obtained from the raw experiment assumes a rigid ship, we must add the **free-surface moment correction** `GG_FS = (Σ ρ_i × i_i) / Δ` to the observed GM to obtain the corrected (true) value. Regulatory bodies (IMO, classification societies) **mandate** this correction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the fundamental inclining formula, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ship of displacement **12 000 tonnes** is subjected to an inclining test. A weight of **25 tonnes** is moved **12 m** across the deck. A pendulum of length **4 m** shows a deflection of **60 mm**. Calculate: (i) the angle of heel in degrees, (ii) the metacentric height `GM`, and (iii) the position of the centre of gravity `KG` if **KB = 1.50 m** and **BM = 4.20 m**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Assumptions**:
1. The ship is initially upright and floating in still water.
2. The angle of heel `θ` is small (typically `θ < 4°`).
3. The displacement `Δ` is constant (weight moved is *internal* to the ship).
4. The metacentre `M` remains fixed for small angles.
5. The ship is free to trim (not constrained laterally).

**Derivation**:

When the test weight `w` is shifted transversely by a distance `y`, the **heeling moment** produced is:
$$M_{\text{heel}} = w \cdot y$$

This moment rotates the ship until equilibrium with the **righting moment**:
$$M_{\text{right}} = \Delta \cdot GZ$$

For a small angle, the righting lever is:
$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\theta) \approx GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

At equilibrium:
$$w \cdot y = \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

Solving for `GM`:
$$\boxed{GM = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{\Delta \cdot \tan(\theta)}}$$

This is the **fundamental inclining formula**.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given**: `Δ = 12 000 t`, `w = 25 t`, `y = 12 m`, `l = 4 m`, `d = 60 mm`, `KB = 1.50 m`, `BM = 4.20 m`.

**(i) Angle of heel**:

$$\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{d}{l} = \dfrac{0.060 \;\; \text{m}}{4 \;\; \text{m}} = 0.015$$

$$\theta = \arctan(0.015) = 0.8594°$$

**(ii) Metacentric height**:

$$GM = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{\Delta \cdot \tan(\theta)} = \dfrac{25 \times 12}{12\,000 \times 0.015}$$

$$GM = \dfrac{300}{180} = 1.667 \;\; \text{m}$$

**(iii) Centre of gravity**:

$$KM = KB + BM = 1.50 + 4.20 = 5.70 \;\; \text{m}$$

$$KG = KM - GM = 5.70 - 1.667 = 4.033 \;\; \text{m}$$

> **Final Answers**: (i) `θ = 0.86°`, (ii) `GM = 1.67 m`, (iii) `KG = 4.03 m`.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** List the precautions to be observed during an inclining experiment and explain the importance of free-surface correction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vessel of displacement **6500 tonnes** has a test weight of **15 tonnes** shifted through **10 m**, producing a heel of **1.2°**. A free-surface correction of **0.30 m** is to be applied. Calculate: (i) the uncorrected `GM`, (ii) the corrected `GM`, and (iii) comment on the stability of the vessel. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Qualitative (7 Marks)**

**Precautions during Inclining Experiment**:
1. The vessel must be in **calm weather** — wind, current, and waves corrupt the readings.
2. All **moorings must be completely slack** so the vessel floats freely.
3. **Loose items, crew, and tools** must be removed or stationed at fixed, known positions.
4. The **test weight** must be moved **carefully and slowly** to avoid dynamic oscillations.
5. The vessel should be in the **inclining condition** — lightest seagoing condition.
6. **Pendulums** must be installed in **oil baths** to damp oscillations and read at the **extreme** of swing.
7. Readings are taken in **multiple cycles** (typically 4–8) to compute a **reliable mean**.
8. The **draughts** at all marks must be read to obtain the exact displacement.

**Importance of Free-Surface Correction**:
Partially-filled tanks contain liquids that shift during heel. This shifts the effective centre of gravity of the ship upward, **reducing the true GM**. Without correction, the ship *appears* more stable than it actually is — a **dangerous** overestimation. The free-surface correction `GG_FS` is added to the observed GM to obtain the corrected GM, ensuring the stability booklet reflects **real** vessel behaviour under service conditions.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given**: `Δ = 6500 t`, `w = 15 t`, `y = 10 m`, `θ = 1.2°`, `GG_FS = 0.30 m`.

**(i) Uncorrected GM**:

Convert angle to radians:
$$\theta = 1.2° = \dfrac{1.2 \times \pi}{180} = 0.02094 \;\; \text{rad}$$

$$\tan(1.2°) = 0.02094$$

Apply the master formula:
$$GM_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{w \cdot y}{\Delta \cdot \tan(\theta)} = \dfrac{15 \times 10}{6500 \times 0.02094}$$

$$GM_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{150}{136.1} = 1.102 \;\; \text{m}$$

**(ii) Corrected GM**:

$$GM_{\text{corrected}} = GM_{\text{obs}} + GG_{FS} = 1.102 + 0.30 = 1.402 \;\; \text{m}$$

**(iii) Stability comment**:
Since `GM_corrected = 1.40 m > 0`, the vessel has **positive initial transverse stability**. The vessel is **stable** in the inclining condition. The IMO minimum recommended GM for a service vessel is typically **0.15 m for passengers** and **0.05 m for cargo**; this vessel comfortably exceeds both.

> **Final Answers**: (i) `GM_obs = 1.10 m`, (ii) `GM_corr = 1.40 m`, (iii) **Vessel is positively stable** with comfortable margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Degree vs. Radian Mix-up** — The biggest mark-killer. `tan(θ)` in the master formula requires `θ` in **radians** (or use a calculator in degree mode consistently). State the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency** — Convert `mm` to `m` for the pendulum deflection `d` *before* dividing by `l`. Mixing units leads to `GM` in wrong order of magnitude.
> 3. **Forgetting the Free-Surface Correction** — KTU explicitly tests this. A raw `GM` without `GG_FS` is **incomplete** and loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing `KB + BM` with `KG + GM`** — Remember: `KM = KB + BM` is on the **buoyancy** side, while `KG + GM` is on the **gravity** side, and they share the metacentre `M`.
> 5. **Weight in kg vs. tonnes** — Ships use **tonnes** (1000 kg). Converting incorrectly leads to a 1000× error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Last-Minute KTU Prep**

- [ ] **Definition**: Inclining Experiment = procedure to find as-built `GM` & `KG` of a ship.
- [ ] **Master Formula**: `GM = (w × y) / (Δ × tan θ)` — the **only** equation you must memorise.
- [ ] **Pendulum Equation**: `tan θ = d / l` where `d` is in the same units as `l`.
- [ ] **Small-Angle Approximation**: `tan θ ≈ θ` (in radians) is valid for `θ < 4°`.
- [ ] **KG Calculation**: `KG = KM − GM = (KB + BM) − GM`.
- [ ] **Free-Surface Correction**: Always **add** `GG_FS` to observed `GM`.
- [ ] **Required Conditions**: Calm weather, slack moorings, lightest seagoing condition.
- [ ] **Test Weight**: Heavy (10–25 t) lead weights, moved 8–15 m athwartships.
- [ ] **Reading Cycles**: 4–8 cycles of port-starboard shifts; average for accuracy.
- [ ] **Stability Verdict**:
  - `GM > 0` → **Positive stability** (safe).
  - `GM = 0` → **Neutral stability** (marginal, avoid).
  - `GM < 0` → **Negative stability** (CAPSIZE — reject).
- [ ] **Conversion Constants**: 1° = 0.01745 rad, 1 tonne = 1000 kg.
- [ ] **Why Real Test?**: Accounts for "swung-on" outfit weight that design calculations miss.
- [ ] **Who Mandates It?**: IMO, classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS).
- [ ] **Typical Procedure Time**: 2–4 hours, with a team of 6–10 personnel.
- [ ] **Common KG Sources**: Crew quarters (high), machinery (low), superstructure (high), double bottom (low).
- [ ] **Units Everywhere**: Never mix tonnes/kg, mm/m, degrees/radians without conversion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heeling Moments due to Wind, Shift of Cargo, Passengers, Turning and Nonsymmetrical Accumulation of Ice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heeling Moments due to Wind, Shift of Cargo, Passengers, Turning and Nonsymmetrical Accumulation of Ice ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Heeling Moment** is defined as the **product of a disturbing transverse force (or weight) and its lever arm measured vertically from the ship's centre of gravity (G)**. It is the moment that tends to **rotate the ship about its longitudinal fore-aft axis**, causing a transverse inclination (heel) from the upright position. In ship stability analysis, a heeling moment is **statically equivalent** to the righting moment it must balance when the ship attains a small equilibrium heel angle (θ) in still water. The classical equilibrium condition is: $$\text{Heeling Moment} = \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$$ where **Δ** is the ship's displacement, **GM** is the metacentric height, and **θ** is the resulting heel angle (assumed small, i.e., < 15°). The five canonical sources of heeling moments prescribed by the **IMO/SOLAS** framework and tested under the **KTU 2024 scheme (Module 4)** are: 1. **Wind Heeling Moment (H_V)** — Aerodynamic pressure on the above-water lateral windage area. 2. **Shift of Cargo Heeling Moment (H_SC)** — Transverse sliding/loosening of a partial cargo mass. 3. **Passenger Heeling Moment (H_P)** — Mass of passengers collecting at one rail. 4. **Turning Heeling Moment (H_T)** — Centrifugal force on a ship executing a turn. 5. **Non-symmetrical Ice Accumulation Heeling Moment (H_I)** — Asymmetric ice accretion on exposed decks and superstructures. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Tilting Cargo Truck:** Imagine a tall truck carrying a heavy wooden crate. If the crate slides sideways by even half a metre, the truck visibly leans to that side. That tilt is the same as a **shift-of-cargo heeling moment** in a ship. Replace the crate with **wind pushing against the ship's side (sail effect)**, **passengers crowding one rail**, **ice forming thicker on the port deck**, or the **ship itself curving through a turn** — and you have the other four heeling moments. All of them, irrespective of their physical origin, produce the same end-result: a **transverse list (heel)** that the righting arm of the ship must oppose. ### 1.3 Fundamental Engineering Constants (Bold for Emphasis) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Standard passenger mass (SOLAS):** **75 kg per passenger** > - **Standard wind pressure (SOLAS simplified):** **P = 50 Pa = 50 N/m²** (corresponding to **V = 10 m/s ≈ 20 knots**, derived from **P = ½·ρ_air·V²** with **ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³**) > - **Standard ice density:** **ρ_ice = 0.9 t/m³ (900 kg/m³)** > - **Standard ice thickness (Severe/Region A):** **30 mm**; (Moderate/Region B): **15 mm**; (Light/Region C): negligible > - **Ice accumulation per SOLAS Reg. II-1/25-A:** **30 mm** on all exposed weather decks and horizontal surfaces > - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** > - **Maximum allowable weather heel angle (SOLAS Weather Criterion):** **tan(θ) ≤ 0.5·(B/GM)** with **θ ≤ ~16°** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Universal Heeling Equation All five heeling moments discussed in this module share a **common equilibrium equation** for a freely floating ship in still water: $$H = \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \quad \text{or} \quad \tan\theta = \frac{H}{\Delta \cdot GM}$$ where: - **H** = any one of the heeling moments (H_V, H_SC, H_P, H_T, H_I) in **tonne-metres (t·m)** or **kN·m** - **Δ** = displacement in **tonnes (t)** - **GM** = metacentric height in **metres (m)** - **θ** = resulting heel angle in **degrees or radians** > [!NOTE] > For very small angles (θ < 10°), **sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ θ (radians)** — this is the small-angle assumption universally used in SOLAS weather criteria and KTU numerical problems. ### 2.2 Breakdown of Each Heeling Moment #### (A) Wind Heeling Moment — *H_V* Wind exerts a transverse aerodynamic force on the ship's above-water lateral windage area. The heeling moment is the product of wind force and its vertical lever. $$H_V = P \cdot A_{LV} \cdot L \quad \text{[t·m]}$$ - **P** = wind pressure (typically **P = 50 Pa** for a **10 m/s (≈ 20 knot)** wind, per SOLAS) - **A_{LV}** = lateral projected (windage) area in **m²** - **L** = vertical lever from **waterline (WL)** to the **centroid of the lateral windage area** in **m** Equivalently, using the full aerodynamic formulation: $$H_V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{air}\,V^2\,A_{LV}\,L \quad [\text{N·m}]$$ > The resulting heeling angle is **tan θ = H_V / (Δ · GM)**. #### (B) Shift of Cargo Heeling Moment — *H_SC* A loose or improperly stowed partial cargo of mass **w** shifts transversely by a distance **d**, generating a moment about G. $$H_{SC} = w \cdot d \quad [\text{t·m}]$$ - **w** = mass of shifted cargo in **tonnes** - **d** = transverse shift distance in **m** Resulting heel: $$\tan\theta = \frac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot GM}$$ > The shift also lowers G (a **virtual loss of GM**), but in elementary KTU analysis only the direct heeling moment is considered. #### (C) Passenger Heeling Moment — *H_P* Per **SOLAS**, passengers are assumed to collect at one rail, each weighing **75 kg (0.075 t)**, positioned at a horizontal distance of **B/2** (half the beam) from the centreline. $$H_P = n \cdot 0.075 \cdot \frac{B}{2} \quad [\text{t·m}]$$ - **n** = number of passengers - **B** = ship's beam in **m** Resulting heel: $$\tan\theta = \frac{n \cdot 0.075 \cdot (B/2)}{\Delta \cdot GM}$$ > The lever arm is taken at the **uppermost deck where passengers are permitted**, not at the waterline. #### (D) Turning Heeling Moment — *H_T* When a ship turns with **angular velocity ω** at **forward speed V**, every mass element experiences a centrifugal force directed outward. The component acting at the **ship's centre of gravity** is balanced by the rudder force, but a **transverse moment** remains because the ship's mass is distributed fore-and-aft. A simplified model treats the ship as **two halves rotating about midship**, each of mass **Δ/2** at a distance **L/2** from the pivot: $$H_T = \frac{\Delta}{2g} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{\Delta \cdot V^2 \cdot L}{4 \cdot g \cdot R} \quad [\text{t·m}]$$ - **V** = speed of advance in **m/s** - **R** = radius of turning circle in **m** - **L** = length between perpendiculars in **m** - **Δ** = displacement in **tonnes** - **g = 9.81 m/s²** Resulting heel: $$\tan\theta = \frac{V^2 \cdot L}{4 \cdot g \cdot R \cdot GM} = \frac{V^2 \cdot L}{4 \cdot R \cdot GM \cdot g}$$ > Heel is **outward** (away from turn centre) for a ship with **fin keel / moderate GM**, and **inward** if **GM is small** (free-surface dominated tankers often turn to the outside). #### (E) Nonsymmetrical Ice Accumulation Heeling Moment — *H_I* SOLAS Regulation **II-1/25-A** mandates an assumed ice load on **all exposed weather decks, exposed decks of superstructures and deckhouses**, considering **nonsymmetrical loading** to obtain the worst-case heeling moment. $$H_I = w_{ice} \cdot y_{ice} \quad [\text{t·m}]$$ where: - **w_ice = ρ_ice · t · A_ice = 0.9 · t · A_ice** in tonnes - **t** = ice thickness in **m** (standard **0.030 m** for severe zones) - **A_ice** = ice-covered area in **m²** - **y_ice** = transverse distance from **CL to centroid of ice-covered area** in **m** > For a **rectangular deck** of width **B** covered half-way, **y_ice = B/4**; covered fully on one side, **y_ice = B/2**. The corresponding heel angle: $$\tan\theta = \frac{H_I}{\Delta \cdot GM}$$ The SOLAS icing computation uses **three standard regions** with their prescribed thicknesses: | Region | Ice Thickness (t) | Notes | |--------|-------------------|-------| | **A — Severe** | **30 mm (0.030 m)** | North Atlantic winter, etc. | | **B — Moderate** | **15 mm (0.015 m)** | North Sea, mid-latitudes winter | | **C — Light** | Negligible | Tropical / summer service | > The ice also adds **weight** to the ship (reducing **KG** effectively) but for KTU Module 4, the heeling component is the focus. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every formula you must memorise for Module 4 numerical questions. | # | Source | Heeling Moment (H) [t·m] | Heel Angle Equation | |---|--------|--------------------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Wind** | $H_V = P \cdot A_{LV} \cdot L$ | $\tan\theta = H_V / (\Delta \cdot GM)$ | | 2 | **Shift of Cargo** | $H_{SC} = w \cdot d$ | $\tan\theta = (w \cdot d)/(\Delta \cdot GM)$ | | 3 | **Passengers** | $H_P = n \cdot 0.075 \cdot (B/2)$ | $\tan\theta = n \cdot 0.075 \cdot (B/2) / (\Delta \cdot GM)$ | | 4 | **Turning** | $H_T = \Delta \cdot V^2 \cdot L / (4 g R)$ | $\tan\theta = V^2 \cdot L / (4 g R \cdot GM)$ | | 5 | **Ice (Nonsym.)** | $H_I = 0.9 \cdot t \cdot A_{ice} \cdot y_{ice}$ | $\tan\theta = H_I / (\Delta \cdot GM)$ | **Universal constants & assumed values (use these defaults in KTU numericals unless stated otherwise):** | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |----------|--------|----------------|------| | Wind pressure (SOLAS) | P | **50** | Pa = N/m² | | Passenger mass (SOLAS) | m_p | **75** = **0.075** | kg / t | | Air density | ρ_air | **1.225** | kg/m³ | | Ice density | ρ_ice | **0.9** | t/m³ | | Severe ice thickness | t | **0.030** | m | | Gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² | | Wind arm lever (typ.) | L | half-height of windage area | m | | Number of passengers | n | given | — | | Radius of turn | R | given | m | | Beam | B | given | m | | LBP | L | given | m | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Naval Architecture (Design Phase):** Designers compute each heeling moment to set the **minimum required GM** so that no single source can capsize or excessively list the ship. - **Regulatory Compliance (Classification):** IACS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV all enforce **SOLAS Ch. II-1** weather criterion and ice loading rules during plan approval. - **Operational Decision (Master/Mate):** Real-time decisions on **cargo lashing, passenger muster, speed in turns, and route planning** rely on understanding these moments. - **Accident Investigation:** The 1994 *MS Estonia* disaster, the *Titanic* (iceberg), and many bulk carrier casualties trace their root cause to mismanaged heeling moments (cargo shift, ice, wind). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Universal Heeling Equilibrium **Starting principle:** When a ship is in static equilibrium under a heeling moment **H** in still water, the **righting moment** of the ship must equal **H** exactly. **Step 1:** The righting moment at a small heel angle θ is given by the classical Atwood's formula: $$RM = \Delta \cdot \overline{GZ}(\theta)$$ **Step 2:** For small angles (θ < 10°), the righting lever simplifies to: $$\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Step 3:** Substituting: $$RM = \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Step 4:** Equilibrium condition **RM = H**: $$\Delta \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = H$$ **Step 5:** For small angles, **sin θ ≈ tan θ**: $$\boxed{\tan\theta = \frac{H}{\Delta \cdot GM}}$$ This is the **master equation** for all Module 4 problems. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Wind Heeling Moment (Aerodynamic Form) **Step 1:** Bernoulli's dynamic pressure for a wind of velocity **V** moving against a flat plate (lateral windage area): $$P = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{air}\,V^2$$ **Step 2:** Substitute ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³ and V = 10 m/s: $$P = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(10)^2 = 61.25 \text{ Pa}$$ SOLAS adopts a **rounded down P = 50 Pa** for safety and simplicity, often quoted as "**corresponding to a 10 m/s wind**" (sometimes called a "**10 m/s criterion**" — though strictly the algebra yields 50 Pa at ~9.03 m/s). **Step 3:** Total wind force on lateral area: $$F_W = P \cdot A_{LV} \quad [\text{N}]$$ **Step 4:** Heeling moment about the centre of gravity G (lever L from waterline to centroid of windage area): $$H_V = F_W \cdot L = P \cdot A_{LV} \cdot L \quad [\text{N·m}]$$ **Step 5:** Convert to t·m (1 t·m = 9810 N·m) for KTU calculations, or keep SI consistent. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Turning Heeling Moment (Centrifugal Half-Ship Model) **Step 1:** Consider the ship as two rigid half-lengths, each of mass **Δ/2**, rotating about the midship point at angular velocity **ω**. **Step 2:** The forward speed **V** and turning radius **R** are related by: $$V = \omega R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{V}{R}$$ **Step 3:** The centripetal acceleration of each half's centre of mass (located at **L/2** from the pivot) is: $$a_c = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{V^2}{R^2} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$ **Step 4:** The centrifugal force on each half (mass **Δ/2**) is: $$F_c = \frac{\Delta}{2} \cdot \frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{\Delta}{2g} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R^2} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Both halves act in the **same transverse direction** (outward), and the lever from G is **B/2** (half-beam) for the resultant moment — but a more rigorous treatment integrates mass distribution; the simplified formula retained by KTU is: $$H_T = \frac{\Delta}{2g} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{\Delta \, V^2 L}{4 g R}$$ **Step 6:** Substituting into the master equation: $$\tan\theta = \frac{V^2 L}{4 g R \cdot GM}$$ > This is **the formula KTU expects** for a turning heeling problem. --- ### 3.4 Derivation — Ice Heeling Moment (Nonsymmetrical Case) **Step 1:** Volume of ice on a deck area **A_ice** with thickness **t**: $$V_{ice} = A_{ice} \cdot t \quad [\text{m}^3]$$ **Step 2:** Mass of ice at standard density ρ_ice = 0.9 t/m³: $$w_{ice} = 0.9 \cdot A_{ice} \cdot t \quad [\text{t}]$$ **Step 3:** The ice is assumed to lie **entirely to one side of the centreline**, with its centroid at distance **y_ice** from CL. For a **rectangular weather deck of beam B** completely covered: $$y_{ice} = \frac{B}{4}$$ (centroid of a rectangle measured from one edge to its centre) For a **half-covered** deck of beam B: $$y_{ice} = \frac{B}{4}$$ For a **fully covered deck of width B (centred on CL)** — nonsymmetrical per SOLAS, we take **ice on one side only**, so: $$y_{ice} = \frac{B}{4}$$ **Step 4:** Ice heeling moment: $$H_I = w_{ice} \cdot y_{ice} = 0.9 \cdot A_{ice} \cdot t \cdot y_{ice} \quad [\text{t·m}]$$ **Step 5:** Resulting heel: $$\tan\theta = \frac{0.9 \cdot A_{ice} \cdot t \cdot y_{ice}}{\Delta \cdot GM}$$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample KTU-style problem (illustrative, not part of question bank):** > > A ship of **Δ = 12,000 t**, **GM = 0.60 m**, **B = 20 m**, **LBP = 140 m** is in a **turn of radius R = 500 m** at **V = 7.7 m/s (≈ 15 knots)** with a **partial deck cargo of mass 80 t shifted 3 m to one side**. Calculate (a) the turning heeling moment and heel angle, (b) the cargo-shift heeling moment and heel angle, and (c) the **combined** heel. **Solution (a) — Turning:** $$H_T = \frac{12{,}000 \times (7.7)^2 \times 140}{4 \times 9.81 \times 500}$$ $$= \frac{12{,}000 \times 59.29 \times 140}{19{,}620} = \frac{99{,}607{,}200}{19{,}620} \approx 5{,}076 \text{ t·m}$$ $$\tan\theta_T = \frac{5{,}076}{12{,}000 \times 0.60} = \frac{5{,}076}{7{,}200} = 0.7050$$ $$\theta_T \approx 35.2° \text{ (this is above the small-angle limit; in practice GM is higher or R is larger)}$$ **Solution (b) — Shift of cargo:** $$H_{SC} = 80 \times 3 = 240 \text{ t·m}$$ $$\tan\theta_{SC} = \frac{240}{7{,}200} = 0.0333 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_{SC} \approx 1.91°$$ **Solution (c) — Combined (worst case, all acting on the same side):** $$H_{total} = 5{,}076 + 240 = 5{,}316 \text{ t·m}$$ $$\tan\theta_{total} = \frac{5{,}316}{7{,}200} = 0.7383 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_{total} \approx 36.4°$$ > This illustrative example shows the **dominance of the turning moment** — KTU commonly tests the **superposition principle** of moments. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sources of Heeling Moments and Their Physical Origins ```mermaid flowchart TD A["HEELING MOMENT H
= Delta x GM x sin theta"] B1["WIND
P x A_LV x L"] B2["CARGO SHIFT
w x d"] B3["PASSENGERS
n x 0.075 x B/2"] B4["TURNING
Delta x V squared x L / 4 g R"] B5["ICE LOADING
0.9 x t x A_ice x y_ice"] C1["Lateral windage area
above waterline"] C2["Loose or shifting
deck/cargo mass"] C3["Crowding of passengers
to one rail"] C4["Centrifugal force during
turning maneuver"] C5["Asymmetric ice accretion
on exposed decks"] D["EQUILIBRIUM HEEL ANGLE
tan theta = H / Delta x GM"] E1["SOLAS Reg V/17
P = 50 Pa"] E2["SOLAS Reg II-1/25-A"] E3["SOLAS Weather Criterion
tan theta <= 0.5 B/GM"] B1 --> A B2 --> A B3 --> A B4 --> A B5 --> A A --> D C1 --> B1 C2 --> B2 C3 --> B3 C4 --> B4 C5 --> B5 E1 --> B1 E2 --> B5 E3 --> D ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Free-Body / Vector Logic of a Heeling Ship ```mermaid flowchart LR A["WIND FORCE F_W
= P x A_LV"] B["HEELING ARM L
WL to windage centroid"] C["HEELING MOMENT H_V
= F_W x L"] D["WEIGHT W = Delta x g
acting at G"] E["BUOYANCY F_B
acting at B"] F["RIGHTING MOMENT
= Delta x GZ"] G["EQUILIBRIUM
H_V = Delta x GZ"] H["HEEL ANGLE theta
tan theta = H_V / Delta x GM"] A --> C B --> C C --> G D --> F E --> F F --> G G --> H ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — SOLAS Weather Criterion Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Compute H_V = P x A_LV x L"] --> B["Compute tan theta = H_V / Delta x GM"] B --> C{"Check 1:
tan theta <= 0.5 B/GM"} C -- "YES" --> D{"Check 2:
H_V <= 0.5 Delta GM"} C -- "NO" --> F["FAIL - Increase GM
or reduce A_LV"] D -- "YES" --> E{"Check 3:
theta between 0 and 16 deg"} D -- "NO" --> F E -- "YES" --> G["PASS - Weather Criterion Satisfied"] E -- "NO" --> F ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define the term "heeling moment" in ship stability. List the five main sources of heeling moments that are assessed under SOLAS regulations.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Heeling Moment (1 mark):** A heeling moment is the moment caused by an external or internal transverse force acting on a ship, which tends to incline (heel) the ship about its longitudinal axis. Mathematically, **H = Δ · GM · sin θ**, where Δ is the displacement, GM is the metacentric height, and θ is the resulting heel angle. > > **Five main sources (2 marks):** > 1. **Wind** — aerodynamic force on lateral windage area > 2. **Shift of cargo** — transverse movement of a cargo mass > 3. **Passengers** — mass of passengers collecting at one rail > 4. **Turning** — centrifugal force during a turning manoeuvre > 5. **Non-symmetrical ice accumulation** — asymmetric ice accretion on exposed decks --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the simplified SOLAS expression for wind heeling moment. What is the standard wind pressure value adopted in the simplified weather criterion, and what speed does it represent?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Wind heeling moment expression (1.5 marks):** > $$H_V = P \cdot A_{LV} \cdot L$$ > where **P** = wind pressure, **A_{LV}** = lateral projected (windage) area, **L** = vertical lever from waterline to centroid of windage area. > > **Standard wind pressure (1 mark):** **P = 50 Pa = 50 N/m²** > > **Equivalent wind speed (0.5 mark):** From **P = ½ ρ_air V²**, with **ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³**, we get **V = √(2·50/1.225) ≈ 9.03 m/s ≈ 17.5 knots (≈ 20 knots rounded).** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024]* **(a)** A bulk carrier of **displacement Δ = 25,000 t** has a **metacentric height GM = 1.20 m**. The lateral windage area is **A_{LV} = 1,500 m²** with its centroid at **L = 7.5 m above the waterline**. Using the SOLAS simplified wind pressure of **P = 50 Pa**, calculate: (i) The wind heeling moment **H_V**. (ii) The resulting heel angle **θ** in degrees. **(b)** A **partial cargo of mass 150 t** shifts transversely by **2.5 m** due to a rough sea. Determine: (i) The cargo-shift heeling moment **H_SC**. (ii) The additional heel angle produced (assume cargo is on the same side as the wind, and total heel is the **superposition** of the wind and cargo moments). --- **Model Solution (Question A):** **Part (a) — Wind Heeling Moment (7 marks)** **(i) Wind heeling moment (3 marks):** $$H_V = P \cdot A_{LV} \cdot L = 50 \times 1{,}500 \times 7.5$$ $$H_V = 562{,}500 \text{ N·m}$$ Convert to t·m (1 t·m = 9,810 N·m): $$H_V = \frac{562{,}500}{9{,}810} \approx 57.34 \text{ t·m}$$ > *Note:* KTU accepts the value in N·m if the heel-angle equation is then solved consistently. For consistency, we use **H_V in t·m**. **(ii) Heel angle due to wind (4 marks):** $$\tan\theta_V = \frac{H_V}{\Delta \cdot GM} = \frac{57.34}{25{,}000 \times 1.20}$$ $$\tan\theta_V = \frac{57.34}{30{,}000} = 1.911 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\theta_V = \tan^{-1}(1.911 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 0.1095° \text{ ≈ 0.11°}$$ --- **Part (b) — Shift of Cargo & Superposition (7 marks)** **(i) Cargo-shift heeling moment (3 marks):** $$H_{SC} = w \cdot d = 150 \times 2.5 = 375 \text{ t·m}$$ **(ii) Combined heel angle (4 marks):** Total heeling moment on the same side: $$H_{total} = H_V + H_{SC} = 57.34 + 375 = 432.34 \text{ t·m}$$ (Note: When the heeling moment is large enough to invalidate the small-angle assumption — as in this case where 375 t·m is dominated by the cargo shift — we should still use tan θ = H/(Δ·GM) as the KTU Module-4 first-order answer.) $$\tan\theta_{total} = \frac{432.34}{30{,}000} = 0.01441$$ $$\theta_{total} = \tan^{-1}(0.01441) \approx 0.826°$$ > **Additional heel due to cargo shift alone** = θ_total − θ_V ≈ 0.826° − 0.11° ≈ 0.72°. > **SOLAS Weather Check:** Required **tan θ ≤ 0.5 · (B/GM)**. If B = 32 m (typical Panamax bulk carrier), **0.5 · B/GM = 0.5 · 32/1.20 ≈ 13.33**, so the 0.01441 is **well within** the criterion — but in heavy weather a much larger A_LV and P are used. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 — Alternative Choice]* **(a)** A passenger liner has the following particulars: **Δ = 18,000 t**, **GM = 0.85 m**, **B = 24 m**, **LBP = 160 m**, and is carrying **600 passengers**. If all passengers crowd to one rail (each weighing **75 kg** per SOLAS): (i) Compute the **passenger heeling moment H_P**. (ii) Calculate the **heel angle** caused by this crowding. **(b)** The same ship is now executing a turn of **radius R = 600 m** at a speed of **V = 10.3 m/s (≈ 20 knots)**. Determine: (i) The **turning heeling moment H_T**. (ii) The **heel angle** due to the turn. (iii) Comment on whether the **combined heel** (passengers + turning) exceeds the SOLAS weather-criterion limit of **tan θ ≤ 0.5 · B/GM**. --- **Model Solution (Question B):** **Part (a) — Passenger Heeling (7 marks)** **(i) Passenger heeling moment (3 marks):** $$H_P = n \cdot 0.075 \cdot \frac{B}{2}$$ $$H_P = 600 \times 0.075 \times \frac{24}{2}$$ $$H_P = 600 \times 0.075 \times 12 = 540 \text{ t·m}$$ **(ii) Heel angle due to passengers (4 marks):** $$\tan\theta_P = \frac{H_P}{\Delta \cdot GM} = \frac{540}{18{,}000 \times 0.85}$$ $$\tan\theta_P = \frac{540}{15{,}300} = 0.03529$$ $$\theta_P = \tan^{-1}(0.03529) \approx 2.022°$$ --- **Part (b) — Turning Heeling & Weather Criterion (7 marks)** **(i) Turning heeling moment (3 marks):** $$H_T = \frac{\Delta \cdot V^2 \cdot L}{4 \cdot g \cdot R}$$ $$H_T = \frac{18{,}000 \times (10.3)^2 \times 160}{4 \times 9.81 \times 600}$$ Compute numerator: 18,000 × 106.09 × 160 = 305,539,200 Denominator: 4 × 9.81 × 600 = 23,544 $$H_T = \frac{305{,}539{,}200}{23{,}544} \approx 12{,}977.6 \text{ t·m}$$ **(ii) Heel angle due to turning (2 marks):** $$\tan\theta_T = \frac{H_T}{\Delta \cdot GM} = \frac{12{,}977.6}{15{,}300} = 0.8482$$ $$\theta_T = \tan^{-1}(0.8482) \approx 40.3°$$ > *This is clearly outside the small-angle regime; the linear formula over-predicts. In reality, large heel produces a much larger GZ from the full GZ curve, restoring the ship. KTU Module-4 problems typically keep V moderate and R large so θ < 15°; nevertheless, the algebraic answer stands.* **(iii) Weather criterion check (2 marks):** Allowed: $\tan\theta_{max} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{B}{GM} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{24}{0.85} = 14.12$ So θ_max ≈ 86° (theoretical) — clearly a passenger ship is rarely weather-criterion-limited. Combined heeling moment: $H_{total} = H_P + H_T = 540 + 12{,}977.6 = 13{,}517.6$ t·m $$\tan\theta_{combined} = \frac{13{,}517.6}{15{,}300} = 0.8835$$ $$\theta_{combined} \approx 41.5°$$ > **Conclusion:** The combined heel far exceeds the safe design heel (≈ 16°), so such a fast tight turn with passengers on the rail is **operationally unsafe** — the speed must be reduced or the radius increased to keep θ < 15°. **The weather criterion itself is satisfied** (tan θ << 14.12), but the **practical operational limit** is violated. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4 Heeling-Moment Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch in H_V** — Students often write **H_V = 50 × A_{LV} × L** directly in **N·m** and then plug it into **tan θ = H / (Δ·GM)** where Δ is in tonnes. The two systems are inconsistent. Either: > - Convert H to **t·m** (divide by 9,810), OR > - Use **Δ in kg** (= Δ_t × 1,000) and keep H in N·m. > *Kerala university markers deduct 1 mark* for this confusion. > > 2. **Wrong Lever for Wind** — Students sometimes take **L = LBP** instead of the **vertical lever from WL to windage centroid**. Wind lever is **vertical (Z-direction)**, not the ship's length. > > 3. **Passenger Mass** — A common slip is using **75 t per passenger** instead of **75 kg = 0.075 t**. > > 4. **Ice Centroid Position** — When ice is on **one side of CL only** (nonsymmetrical case), the centroid is at **B/4** from CL (for a rectangular deck of beam B), **not B/2**. > > 5. **Turning Heel Direction** — Forgetting that the heel is **outward** in a normal ship, and **inward** for ships with very low GM (tankers with slack tanks). Marks are lost if direction is not specified. > > 6. **Forgetting to Add Moments** — When two heeling sources act on the same side, the **total moment is the algebraic sum**, not the larger one alone. > > 7. **SOLAS Weather Criterion** — Don't confuse **0.5·(B/GM)** (a dimensionless ratio) with an actual angle; always report the angle in degrees and verify **θ ≤ 16°** as a separate check. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Module 4 — Heeling Moments: At-a-Glance Summary** > > ✔ **Heeling Moment definition:** H = Δ · GM · sin θ ≈ Δ · GM · tan θ (small angles) > > ✔ **Wind:** H_V = P · A_LV · L, with **P = 50 Pa** (SOLAS). L is **vertical lever from WL**. > > ✔ **Cargo Shift:** H_SC = w · d. Use **w in t, d in m → t·m**. > > ✔ **Passengers:** H_P = n × 0.075 × (B/2). Standard mass = **75 kg** per SOLAS. > > ✔ **Turning:** H_T = (Δ · V² · L) / (4 · g · R). Heel is **outward** for normal ships. > > ✔ **Ice:** H_I = 0.9 · t · A_ice · y_ice. **t = 0.030 m** (severe), **y_ice** depends on geometry. > > ✔ **All five sources follow the universal heel equation:** tan θ = H / (Δ · GM). > > ✔ **Constants to memorise:** ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³; ρ_ice = 0.9 t/m³; g = 9.81 m/s²; m_p = 75 kg. > > ✔ **Weather criterion (SOLAS):** tan θ ≤ 0.5 · (B / GM), and θ must lie within the range **0 to ~16°**. > > ✔ **Always check units consistently:** t·m for H, t for Δ, m for GM, deg or rad for θ. > > ✔ **Worst-case design assumption:** all heeling moments act on the **same side** unless otherwise specified. > > ✔ **Ice density must be remembered** as **0.9 t/m³ (not 1.0)** — using fresh-water density is a common mistake costing 1 mark. > > ✔ **Turning moment is dominant** in practice when V is high and R is small — always check whether θ remains in the small-angle regime before quoting degrees. > > ✔ **For ice, the worst-case is "nonsymmetrical",** meaning the ice load is placed entirely on **one side** of the centreline. > > ✔ **Passenger lever is at the uppermost deck** where passengers are permitted, not the waterline — this differs from wind lever. > > ✔ **SOLAS Regulation II-1/25-A** is the **canonical regulatory reference** for ice loading; **Reg V/17** for wind. ---

topics.title: Stability in Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Stability in Waves # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability in Waves** is defined as the ability of a floating vessel to develop a positive righting moment (or righting lever) when subjected to the combined dynamic actions of a transverse (beam) wave system and external heeling disturbances, evaluated not from the static GZ curve in calm water, but from the **effective (or "weather") righting lever curve** that accounts for the instantaneous change in buoyancy distribution caused by the wave passing under the hull. In naval architecture (KTU 2024 Module-4 framework), *stability in waves* is a **time-dependent, dynamic** problem — fundamentally different from the static (calm water) stability analysis of Modules 1, 2, and 3. The syllabus emphasizes three catastrophic loss-of-stability mechanisms: | Phenomenon | KTU Designation | Triggering Condition | |---|---|---| | Pure Loss of Stability (PLOS) | **Dead-Ship Wave Condition** | Wave crest at midship, beam seas | | Synchronous Rolling | **Resonance Roll** | Wave encounter period ≈ Natural roll period | | Parametric Rolling | **Mathieu-Type Resonance** | Wave period ≈ ½ × Natural roll period | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine you are balancing a book (the ship) on a moving treadmill (the wave).** On a flat floor (calm water), the book is stable — you can compute easily how far you can tilt it before it falls. Now place that same book on a moving treadmill whose surface rises and falls in long swells. Even a gentle tilt can suddenly become catastrophic when a swell lifts the middle of the book and "sucks" support from one end. The book hasn't changed, the floor (sea) has. **That is exactly what happens to a ship in waves — the "floor" is no longer flat, so the static stability numbers you memorized earlier may not save the vessel.** The key idea: *A ship can pass every calm-water stability criterion (GM, GZ, area ratios of Module 3) and STILL capsize in a moderate seaway.* This is the central lesson of Module 4. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²** - **Standard wave steepness limit (breaking):** **H/λ ≤ 1/7** (deep water) - **IMO Weather Criterion reference angles:** **30°** and **40°** - **Minimum weather-lever areas (Resolution A.749(18)):** **0.055 m·rad**, **0.09 m·rad**, **0.03 m·rad** - **Critical synchronism ratio for parametric roll:** **T_wave / T_natural ≈ 0.5** ## 1.4 Why Calm-Water Theory Fails in Waves — The Physical Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Calm-water assumptions (Modules 1–3):** > 1. Waterline is a horizontal plane. > 2. Buoyancy force is purely vertical. > 3. Center of buoyancy (B) shifts only due to heel angle. > 4. Pressure on the hull bottom is hydrostatic. > > **In waves, all four assumptions break down simultaneously.** The waterline is wavy, the buoyancy force has a horizontal component due to wave pressure asymmetry, B shifts due to *both* heel AND wave-induced pressure redistribution, and the dynamic pressure includes inertial (Froude–Krylov) terms. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Loss-of-Stability Mechanisms ### 2.1.1 Pure Loss of Stability (PLOS) — Dead Ship in Beam Seas **Physical Picture:** A ship in beam seas with a wave crest located at midship. The wave-induced pressure field causes additional buoyancy amidships and reduced buoyancy at the ends, producing a net **heeling moment** that reduces effective stability. **Why it matters:** Under this configuration, the effective metacentric height can drop to zero or become negative even though the calm-water GM is positive — a phenomenon called **"GM vanishing."** ### 2.1.2 Synchronous Rolling (Resonance) **Physical Picture:** When the wave encounter period **T_E** approaches the ship's natural roll period **T_φ**, energy is continuously fed into the roll motion, leading to roll amplitudes of **30°–40°** or more in otherwise moderate seas. The natural roll period (KTU Module 2 revision) is: $$T_\phi = \frac{2 \pi \cdot k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}}$$ where **k = roll radius of gyration** (≈ 0.35 B for typical ships). The wave encounter period for a ship moving with speed **Vs** at heading angle **μ** to waves of period **T_w** is: $$T_E = \frac{T_w}{1 - \dfrac{V_s}{g} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \cos\mu}$$ **Synchronism condition:** $T_E \approx T_\phi$ ### 2.1.3 Parametric Rolling (Mathieu Instability) **Physical Picture:** When the wave period is approximately **half** the natural roll period ($T_w \approx T_\phi / 2$), the stability itself oscillates with wave passage. Even small disturbances grow exponentially — a parametric resonance governed by the **Mathieu equation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parametric roll is the most dangerous phenomenon in modern container ships.** The MV *Cougar Ace* (2006) and MV *Chicago Express* (2008) are textbook KTU-case-study losses attributed to parametric rolling, where vessels satisfied ALL IMO calm-water criteria yet capsized. ## 2.2 The IMO Weather Criterion — Analytical Framework The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** introduced Resolution **A.749(18)** to address the gap between calm-water criteria and real seakeeping. The criterion is computed on a **weather righting lever curve (GZ_w)** rather than the static GZ curve. ### Step-by-Step Construction (KTU Board Pattern) 1. **Compute steady wind heeling angle** $\phi_0$ by intersecting the wind heeling lever curve $l_{w1}(\phi) = (P \cdot A \cdot Z) / (1000 \cdot \Delta g \cdot \cos\phi)$ with the calm-water GZ curve. 2. **Compute wave slope angle** $\phi_1$ from the prevailing sea state (function of significant wave height and length). 3. **Effective heeling angle:** $\phi_2 = \phi_0 + \phi_1$ 4. **Construct weather righting lever:** - For synchronous rolling: $GZ_w(\phi) = GZ(\phi) - 0.5 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot \sin\phi$ - For beam wind and waves at $40°$ heel: $GZ_w(\phi) = GZ(\phi) - \sin\phi \cdot (\phi_2 - 40°)$ for $\phi \le 40°$ 5. **Apply area criteria** (see Formula Sheet). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The table below contains the complete formula set tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for this topic. Memorize all rows.** | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicability | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_\phi = 2\pi k / \sqrt{g \cdot GM}$ | Natural roll period | s | All ship types | | 2 | $T_E = T_w / (1 - (V_s/g)(2\pi/\lambda)\cos\mu)$ | Wave encounter period | s | Forward speed case | | 3 | Synchronism: $T_E = T_\phi$ | Resonance condition | — | Synchronous roll | | 4 | Parametric trigger: $T_w = T_\phi / 2$ | Mathieu resonance | — | Parametric roll | | 5 | $\phi_2 = \phi_0 + \phi_1$ | Effective heeling angle | rad or ° | Weather criterion | | 6 | $l_{w1} = (P \cdot A \cdot Z) / (1000 \Delta g \cos\phi)$ | Wind heeling lever | m | Steady wind heel | | 7 | $GZ_w(\phi) = GZ(\phi) - 0.5\phi_2 \sin\phi$ | Weather lever (sync. roll) | m | IMO criterion | | 8 | $GZ_w(\phi) = GZ(\phi) - \sin\phi(\phi_2 - 40°)$ | Weather lever (40° form) | m | IMO alt. form | | 9 | Area $\int_0^{30} GZ_w \, d\phi \ge 0.055$ | Weather area criterion | m·rad | IMO A.749(18) | | 10 | Area $\int_0^{40} GZ_w \, d\phi \ge 0.090$ | Weather area criterion | m·rad | IMO A.749(18) | | 11 | Area $\int_{30}^{40} GZ_w \, d\phi \ge 0.030$ | Weather area criterion | m·rad | IMO A.749(18) | | 12 | Effective wave lever reduction $\Delta GZ = (B^2/2T)(1-\cos\phi)$ | Pure loss reduction | m | PLOS estimate | | 13 | $\lambda_w = gT_w^2 / (2\pi)$ | Deep-water wave length | m | Wave kinematics | | 14 | $H_{\max} = 0.0214 V_{ref}^2 / g$ | Max wave height (Beaufort) | m | Sea state estimate | | 15 | $k \approx 0.35 B$ to $0.40 B$ | Roll radius of gyration | m | Empirical | > **Absolute value notation convention used in formulas:** $GZ_w \ge 0$ means the weather righting lever is non-negative at angle $\phi$. The notation $\vert \sin\phi \vert$ is written in the formula sheet as $\sin\phi$ to keep the table compatible with markdown. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The study of stability in waves governs: - **Route planning** for container vessels (avoiding head-on parametric roll conditions) - **Loading computer interlocks** that warn the master when GM × T_φ falls in the dangerous zone - **Class notation** (e.g., DNV GL's *Parametric Roll* notation, ABS's *Safe Return to Port* standards) - **Damage stability** in waves (SOLAS Chapter II-1 amendments for passenger ships) - **Offshore platform and FPSO design** where wave-induced roll and pitch are continuous > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Hint:** Any 14-mark question on Module 4 will almost certainly involve (a) constructing a weather righting lever curve, (b) computing the three IMO areas, and (c) commenting on compliance. Practice this workflow until automatic. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Effective Reduction in GZ for a Ship in Beam Waves (Pure Loss of Stability) ### 3.1.1 Physical Setup and Coordinate System Consider a wall-sided ship of **beam B**, **draft T**, in a regular transverse wave of length $\lambda_w \approx L_{PP}$ (ship length between perpendiculars) with the wave crest located at midship. The instantaneous waterline deviates from horizontal by the wave slope angle $\alpha_w$ where: $$\alpha_w = \frac{\pi H_w}{\lambda_w}$$ with $H_w$ the wave height. ### 3.1.2 Buoyancy Distribution Assumption (Froude–Krylov) We adopt the **Froude–Krylov hypothesis** that the pressure on the hull is the same as on the instantaneous (wavy) waterplane. For a wall-sided ship, the buoyant force per unit length along the ship (in the wave direction) can be expressed as an addition $\delta b(x)$ to the still-water buoyancy. The integration of the **horizontal shift of the center of buoyancy** due to this non-uniform buoyancy gives an effective horizontal force component that, when decomposed about the ship's center of gravity, creates a **heeling moment**. ### 3.1.3 Reduction in Righting Lever For a wall-sided ship at small to moderate heel angle $\phi$, the wave-induced reduction in righting lever is: $$\Delta GZ = \frac{B^2}{2T}\,(1 - \cos\phi)$$ **Derivation walkthrough:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Vertical center of buoyancy shift from wave:} \quad & \delta B_z = \frac{B^2}{2T}\,(1 - \cos\phi) \quad \text{(geometric integration of wedge volumes)} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Corresponding horizontal lever arm:} \quad & l_{wave} = \delta B_z \cdot \tan\phi \quad \text{at small angles, } \tan\phi \approx \sin\phi \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Net effect on the righting lever:} \quad & \Delta GZ = GZ_{calm} - GZ_{wave} = \frac{B^2}{2T}\,(1 - \cos\phi) \\[4pt] \text{Step 4: Limiting case check at } \phi = 0: \quad & \Delta GZ(0) = \frac{B^2}{2T}\,(1 - 1) = 0 \quad \text{(no reduction upright)} \\[4pt] \text{Step 5: At } \phi = 30°: \quad & \Delta GZ(30°) = \frac{B^2}{2T}\,(1 - 0.866) = \frac{0.134 B^2}{2T} = \frac{0.067 B^2}{T} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Illustration **Given:** $B = 32$ m, $T = 12$ m, ship in beam seas with $\lambda_w = L_{PP} = 200$ m, $H_w = 6$ m. **Find:** Reduction in GZ at $\phi = 20°$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Compute } \Delta GZ \text{ at } \phi = 20°: \quad & \Delta GZ = \frac{(32)^2}{2 \times 12}\,(1 - \cos 20°) \\[4pt] & = \frac{1024}{24}\,(1 - 0.9397) \\[4pt] & = 42.67 \times 0.0603 \\[4pt] & = 2.57 \text{ m} \quad \text{(reduction in lever)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A wave-induced reduction of **2.57 m** in the righting lever is enormous — for a typical GZ_max of 0.5–1.0 m, this single wave condition would make the ship unstable. **This is the essence of PLOS.** ## 3.2 Derivation II — Weather Righting Lever Curve (IMO Formulation) ### 3.2.1 The Wind Heeling Lever $l_{w1}$ The wind heeling moment per unit displacement at angle $\phi$ is: $$l_{w1}(\phi) = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot Z}{1000 \cdot \Delta \cdot g \cdot \cos\phi}$$ **Where:** - $P$ = wind pressure in **Pa** ($P = 0.613 V_{ref}^2$ with $V_{ref}$ in m/s) - $A$ = lateral projected area above waterline (m²) - $Z$ = vertical distance from centroid of $A$ to waterline (m) - $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes) - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $\cos\phi$ accounts for reduction in lever arm at heel ### 3.2.2 The Wave Slope Heeling Lever $l_{w2}$ For the IMO criterion, the wave heeling moment is represented as a constant lever: $$l_{w2}(\phi) = \phi_1 \cdot \sin\phi$$ where $\phi_1$ is the wave slope in radians, taken as the angle of the wave surface at the ship's midship. ### 3.2.3 Total Heeling Lever and Equilibrium Angle The total heeling lever is: $$l_w(\phi) = l_{w1}(\phi) + l_{w2}(\phi) = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot Z}{1000 \Delta g \cos\phi} + \phi_1 \sin\phi$$ The equilibrium heeling angle $\phi_0$ is found by setting $l_w(\phi_0) = GZ(\phi_0)$. ### 3.2.4 Construction of Weather Righting Lever $GZ_w$ For the **synchronous rolling case** (IMO Form 1), the weather lever at any angle $\phi$ is: $$GZ_w(\phi) = GZ(\phi) - 0.5 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot \sin\phi$$ **Derivation of the factor 0.5:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Effective heeling:} \quad & \phi_2 = \phi_0 + \phi_1 \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Averaging over the rolling cycle (sinusoidal):} \quad & \langle \phi_2 \cdot \sin\phi \rangle = 0.5 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot \sin\phi \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: The reduction is the projected average loss:} \quad & GZ_w(\phi) = GZ(\phi) - \langle \phi_2 \sin\phi \rangle \\[4pt] & = GZ(\phi) - 0.5 \phi_2 \sin\phi \end{aligned} $$ For the **40° form** (IMO Form 2), applicable to vessels that may roll to the second zero of the GZ curve: $$GZ_w(\phi) = GZ(\phi) - \sin\phi \cdot (\phi_2 - 40°), \quad \phi \le 40°$$ ### 3.2.5 Worked Example — IMO Weather Area Calculation **Given:** Calm-water GZ values (in m) at angles 0°, 10°, 20°, 30°, 40°: $\{0, 0.18, 0.34, 0.42, 0.40\}$. Effective heeling angle $\phi_2 = 25° = 0.4363$ rad. **Find:** Verify IMO criteria. **Step 1: Compute weather lever $GZ_w = GZ - 0.5 \phi_2 \sin\phi$** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi = 0°: \quad & GZ_w = 0 - 0.5 \times 0.4363 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m} \\[4pt] \phi = 10°: \quad & GZ_w = 0.18 - 0.5 \times 0.4363 \times 0.1736 = 0.18 - 0.0379 = 0.1421 \text{ m} \\[4pt] \phi = 20°: \quad & GZ_w = 0.34 - 0.5 \times 0.4363 \times 0.3420 = 0.34 - 0.0746 = 0.2654 \text{ m} \\[4pt] \phi = 30°: \quad & GZ_w = 0.42 - 0.5 \times 0.4363 \times 0.5000 = 0.42 - 0.1091 = 0.3109 \text{ m} \\[4pt] \phi = 40°: \quad & GZ_w = 0.40 - 0.5 \times 0.4363 \times 0.6428 = 0.40 - 0.1402 = 0.2598 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Areas using Simpson's Rule (h = 10° = 0.1745 rad)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Area}_{0-30} & = \frac{h}{3}\,(GZ_w(0) + 4 GZ_w(10°) + GZ_w(20°)) + \frac{h}{3}\,(GZ_w(20°) + 4 GZ_w(30°) + GZ_w(40°)) \\[4pt] & = \frac{0.1745}{3}\,(0 + 4(0.1421) + 0.2654) + \frac{0.1745}{3}\,(0.2654 + 4(0.3109) + 0.2598) \\[4pt] & = 0.05817 \times 0.8338 + 0.05817 \times 1.7690 \\[4pt] & = 0.0485 + 0.1029 = 0.1514 \text{ m·rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Check IMO criteria** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Area}_{0-30} = 0.1514 \text{ m·rad} \quad & \ge 0.055 \text{ m·rad} \quad \checkmark \\[4pt] \text{Area}_{0-40} \text{ (computed similarly)} \quad & \ge 0.090 \text{ m·rad} \quad \checkmark \\[4pt] \text{Area}_{30-40} \text{ (computed similarly)} \quad & \ge 0.030 \text{ m·rad} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > **The vessel satisfies the IMO weather criterion under the given loading condition.** ## 3.3 Derivation III — Parametric Roll Threshold (Mathieu Equation) The linearized equation of roll motion in regular beam waves, assuming a slowly varying roll restoring stiffness $GM(t) = GM_0 + \delta GM \cdot \cos(\omega_e t)$, is the **Mathieu equation**: $$\ddot{\phi} + \omega_\phi^2 \left(1 + \epsilon \cos(\omega_e t)\right)\phi = 0$$ where: - $\omega_\phi = \sqrt{g \cdot GM_0 / k^2}$ is the natural roll frequency - $\epsilon = \delta GM / GM_0$ is the relative stiffness variation - $\omega_e$ is the wave encounter frequency **Instability condition (principal parametric resonance):** $$\omega_e \approx 2 \omega_\phi \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad T_w \approx T_\phi / 2$$ The growth rate at exact resonance is: $$\phi(t) = \phi_0 \exp\left(\frac{\epsilon \cdot \omega_\phi}{4} \cdot t\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Parametric roll grows **exponentially**, not linearly. In **5–10 wave encounters**, a small initial heel of 2° can escalate to 30°+, far too fast for human intervention. This is why the IMO, classification societies, and route-planning software all treat parametric roll as a *predict-and-avoid* problem rather than a *react-and-correct* one. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Each Phenomenon Dominates | Sea State | Wave Length vs Ship | Encounter Condition | Dominant Phenomenon | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Calm (Hs < 1 m) | $\lambda_w \ll L$ | Sub-resonant | None | — | | Moderate, head seas | $\lambda_w \approx L$ | $T_E \approx T_\phi$ | Synchronous roll | Alter course, reduce speed | | Moderate, beam seas | $\lambda_w \approx L$ | $T_w \approx T_\phi / 2$ | **Parametric roll** | Change heading > 30° | | Heavy, beam seas | $\lambda_w \approx L$ | Multiple matches | All three combined | Heave to, ballast adjustment | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stability-in-Waves Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Ship Particulars
