Reduce GM or Alter Stowage] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between synchronous rolling and parametric rolling in a ship.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Synchronous Rolling | Parametric Rolling | |---|---|---| | Trigger | $T_E \approx T_\phi$ (encounter ≈ natural) | $T_w \approx T_\phi / 2$ (wave ≈ half natural) | | Mechanism | Direct forced resonance | Parametric (Mathieu-type) resonance | | Growth | Linear, energy build-up | Exponential, very rapid (5–10 cycles) | | Sea state | Moderate to heavy | Moderate, but precisely tuned period | | Governing eq. | Linear D'Erlanger | Mathieu equation | | Typical vessel | All ship types | Large container ships, car carriers | *(1 mark for correct trigger condition, 1 mark for mechanism distinction, 1 mark for growth/sea-state difference.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the IMO weather criterion areas and the angles up to which they must be computed.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The criterion is applied to the **weather righting lever curve (GZ_w)** computed from IMO Resolution A.749(18). - **Area 0 to 30°** must be **≥ 0.055 m·rad** (1 mark) - **Area 0 to 40°** must be **≥ 0.090 m·rad** (1 mark) - **Area 30 to 40°** must be **≥ 0.030 m·rad** (1 mark) - The applicable angle is the lesser of: angle of flooding, angle of deck-edge immersion, or **40°**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For weather-criterion problems, students routinely lose **4–6 marks** in three places: (1) forgetting to convert heel angles from degrees to radians in Simpson's rule integration, (2) applying the 0.5 factor of synchronous-roll form without justification, and (3) failing to check the *applicability* of the criterion (i.e., angle of flooding, deck-edge immersion, 40° minimum). Always document your applicability angle explicitly. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, derive the expression for the wave-induced reduction in righting lever $\Delta GZ = (B^2 / 2T)(1 - \cos\phi)$ for a wall-sided ship in beam seas with the wave crest at midship. **State the key assumptions.** *(7 marks)* **(b)** The weather righting lever at angles 0°, 10°, 20°, 30°, 40° is given as 0, 0.142, 0.265, 0.311, 0.260 m respectively. Using Simpson's rule, verify compliance with the IMO weather criterion (A.749(18)). **Comment on the result.** *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for A(a) **Sketch (textual):** ``` Wave Crest at Midship ╱╲ ╱ ╲ ════════╱ ╲════════ ← instantaneous waterline (wavy) Ship hull (wall-sided, beam B, draft T) ``` **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Geometric setup} \quad & \text{For a wall-sided ship at heel } \phi \text{, the emerged/immersed wedge of buoyancy has area:} \\[4pt] & A_w = \frac{B^2 \tan\phi}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Vertical shift of B} \quad & \delta B_z = \frac{A_w \cdot (B/3)}{V} \quad \text{where } V = B \cdot T \cdot L \text{ is displacement volume form} \\[4pt] & \text{For unit length along ship (working in 2D):} \quad \delta B_z = \frac{B^2 \tan\phi / 2 \cdot (B/3)}{B \cdot T} = \frac{B^2 \tan\phi}{6T} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Wave-induced correction (full derivation)} \quad & \text{Integrating pressure over the wavy bottom, the wave's effective contribution to } \delta B_z \text{ is:} \\[4pt] & \Delta(\delta B_z) = \frac{B^2}{2T}(1 - \cos\phi) \\[4pt] \text{Step 4: Connection to GZ reduction} \quad & \Delta GZ = \Delta(\delta B_z) = \frac{B^2}{2T}(1 - \cos\phi) \quad \text{(since lever arm scales with } \sin\phi \text{)} \\[4pt] \text{Step 5: Sanity check at } \phi = 0: \quad & \Delta GZ(0) = 0 \text{ (no reduction upright)} \\[4pt] \text{Step 6: Sanity check as } \phi \to 90°: \quad & \Delta GZ(90°) = B^2/2T \text{ (max possible)} \end{aligned} $$ **Key Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Wall-sided hull form (constant B, T) 2. Froude–Krylov pressure assumption (hull feels instantaneous hydrostatic pressure of wave) 3. Wave crest located at midship (worst case for PLOS) 4. Wave length $\lambda_w \approx L_{PP}$ (ship fully supported by one wave) 5. Small-to-moderate angles (geometric wedge approximation valid) #### Model Solution for A(b) **Step 1: Tabulate values** | $\phi$ (°) | $\phi$ (rad) | GZ_w (m) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0.0000 | 0.000 | | 10 | 0.1745 | 0.142 | | 20 | 0.3491 | 0.265 | | 30 | 0.5236 | 0.311 | | 40 | 0.6981 | 0.260 | **Step 2: Apply Simpson's 1/3 Rule with $h = 10° = 0.1745$ rad** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Area}_{0-30} & = \frac{h}{3}\,[GZ_w(0) + 4 \cdot GZ_w(10°) + GZ_w(20°)] + \frac{h}{3}\,[GZ_w(20°) + 4 \cdot GZ_w(30°) + GZ_w(40°)] \\[4pt] & = \frac{0.1745}{3}\,[0 + 4(0.142) + 0.265] + \frac{0.1745}{3}\,[0.265 + 4(0.311) + 0.260] \\[4pt] & = 0.05817 \times 0.833 + 0.05817 \times 1.769 \\[4pt] & = 0.0485 + 0.1029 = 0.1514 \text{ m·rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Area 0–40 directly** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Area}_{0-40} & = \frac{h}{3}\,[GZ_w(0) + 4 GZ_w(10°) + 2 GZ_w(20°) + 4 GZ_w(30°) + GZ_w(40°)] \\[4pt] & = \frac{0.1745}{3}\,[0 + 0.568 + 0.530 + 1.244 + 0.260] \\[4pt] & = 0.05817 \times 2.602 = 0.1514 \text{ m·rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute Area 30–40** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Area}_{30-40} & = \frac{h}{3}\,[GZ_w(30°) + 4 GZ_w(35°) + GZ_w(40°)] \end{aligned} $$ Need GZ_w(35°). Interpolate: $GZ_w(35°) = 0.311 + 0.5(0.260 - 0.311) = 0.2855$ m. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Area}_{30-40} & = \frac{0.1745}{3}\,[0.311 + 4(0.2855) + 0.260] \\[4pt] & = 0.05817 \times 1.713 = 0.0997 \text{ m·rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: IMO Compliance Check** | Criterion | Computed | Required | Status | |---|---|---|---| | Area 0–30 | 0.1514 m·rad | ≥ 0.055 m·rad | ✓ Pass | | Area 0–40 | 0.1514 m·rad | ≥ 0.090 m·rad | ✓ Pass | | Area 30–40 | 0.0997 m·rad | ≥ 0.030 m·rad | ✓ Pass | **Comment (1 mark):** The vessel **satisfies all three IMO weather criteria** with substantial margin. The Area 0–30 is **2.75× the minimum** and Area 0–40 is **1.68× the minimum**, indicating excellent residual stability in waves. No remedial action (ballast adjustment, course change) is required under the assumed sea state. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **parametric rolling** phenomenon in ships. Derive the **Mathieu equation** governing it and state the instability condition. **Why is it particularly dangerous for modern container ships?** *(7 marks)* **(b)** A ship has natural roll period $T_\phi = 18$ s, displacement $\Delta = 50{,}000$ t, beam $B = 30$ m, draft $T = 10$ m. It encounters beam seas with wave period $T_w = 8$ s and wave height $H_w = 4$ m. Compute the **synchronism ratio** and the **reduction in GZ at 25° heel** under the PLOS assumption. **Comment on the safety implication.** *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for B(a) **Step 1: Physical Description (2 marks)** Parametric rolling is a violent roll motion that occurs when the **wave encounter period is approximately half the natural roll period** ($T_w \approx T_\phi/2$). The stability (metacentric height) itself oscillates as the wave passes along the hull, with the wave crest amidships *increasing* effective GM and the wave trough amidships *decreasing* it. This time-varying stiffness pumps energy into the roll mode, leading to **exponential growth** of roll amplitude. **Step 2: Equation of Motion (3 marks)** For roll motion $\phi$ with a time-varying righting moment coefficient $N_\phi(t) = N_0 + \Delta N_\phi \cos(\omega_e t)$: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} + N_0 \phi + \Delta N_\phi \cos(\omega_e t) \cdot \phi &= 0 \\[4pt] \text{Divide by } I_{xx}: \quad \ddot{\phi} + \omega_\phi^2 \left(1 + \epsilon \cos(\omega_e t)\right)\phi &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ where $\omega_\phi^2 = N_0/I_{xx} = gGM/k^2$ and $\epsilon = \Delta N_\phi/N_0$ is the relative stiffness modulation. **Step 3: Instability Condition (1 mark)** By Floquet theory / Mathieu-Hill analysis, the principal instability region is: $$\omega_e \approx 2\omega_\phi \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad T_w \approx \frac{T_\phi}{2}$$ **Step 4: Danger for Container Ships (1 mark)** Container ships have (i) **high GM** (low natural roll period $T_\phi$ 15–25 s), (ii) **long parallel middle body** (large B, large stiffness variation in waves), (iii) **low bilge keels** (low roll damping), and (iv) operation in **trade routes with consistent wave periods** (e.g., North Pacific winter). The combination makes them uniquely susceptible — a 2006 incident (MV *Cougar Ace*) saw 60° rolls in otherwise moderate Hs = 4 m seas. #### Model Solution for B(b) **Step 1: Synchronism Ratio** $$ \begin{aligned} T_w / T_\phi = 8 / 18 = 0.444 \end{aligned} $$ This is **close to 0.5** → **parametric roll regime is approached** but not exactly triggered. (If $T_w = 9$ s, ratio = 0.5 = exact parametric resonance.) **Step 2: GZ Reduction at 25° Heel under PLOS** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta GZ(25°) & = \frac{B^2}{2T}\,(1 - \cos 25°) \\[4pt] & = \frac{(30)^2}{2 \times 10}\,(1 - 0.9063) \\[4pt] & = \frac{900}{20} \times 0.0937 \\[4pt] & = 45 \times 0.0937 = 4.22 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Safety Comment** > [!WARNING] > **Critical Safety Implication:** A reduction of **4.22 m** in the righting lever at 25° heel is **catastrophically large** for a vessel whose calm-water GZ_max is typically 0.6–1.0 m. Under the assumed beam-sea condition with a wave crest at midship, the ship would be **dynamically unstable** at 25° heel and would capsize. The synchronism ratio of 0.444 places the vessel in the **dangerous parametric-roll neighborhood**; a 1-second increase in wave period would bring it to exact resonance. **Mitigation:** Immediate course alteration by > 30° to put the sea on the quarter, OR speed reduction to alter $T_E$, OR ballast transfer to lower GZ into a safer range. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section and keep it beside you during revision.** - ☐ **Stability in waves ≠ Stability in calm water.** Always re-evaluate on GZ_w curve. - ☐ Three dangerous phenomena: **PLOS, Synchronous Roll, Parametric Roll** — know the trigger of each. - ☐ Natural roll period: $T_\phi = 2\pi k / \sqrt{gGM}$ — memorized. - ☐ Encounter period: $T_E = T_w / (1 - (V_s/g)(2\pi/\lambda)\cos\mu)$ — memorized. - ☐ Synchronous roll when $T_E = T_\phi$; parametric roll when $T_w = T_\phi/2$. - ☐ IMO weather criterion areas: **0.055, 0.090, 0.030 m·rad** — memorized. - ☐ Weather lever form 1 (sync roll): $GZ_w = GZ - 0.5\phi_2\sin\phi$. - ☐ Weather lever form 2 (40° form): $GZ_w = GZ - \sin\phi(\phi_2 - 40°)$. - ☐ Always convert heel angle to **radians** before Simpson's integration. - ☐ Applicability angle = min(angle of flooding, deck-edge immersion, 40°). - ☐ PLOS lever reduction: $\Delta GZ = (B^2/2T)(1 - \cos\phi)$. - ☐ Parametric roll grows **exponentially** — 5 to 10 wave encounters can be enough. - ☐ Cougar Ace (2006), Chicago Express (2008) — standard KTU case-study references. - ☐ For large container ships: high GM + low damping + consistent sea state = parametric roll hotspot. - ☐ Wind heeling lever: $l_{w1} = (P A Z) / (1000 \Delta g \cos\phi)$. - ☐ Wave slope angle: $\phi_1 = (\pi H_w)/\lambda_w$ (small-angle approximation). - ☐ Course alteration > 30° is the most effective operational mitigation. --- ### End of Module 4 Notes — Stability in Waves **Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme regulations and B.Tech Naval Architecture syllabus (PBNST304).**

topics.title: Recommendations of IMO, Classification Societies and Government Authorities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recommendations of IMO, Classification Societies and Government Authorities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 International Maritime Organization (IMO)

The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** is a specialized agency of the United Nations responsible for regulating shipping. It is the global standard-setting authority for the safety, security, and environmental performance of international shipping. Its primary role is to create a regulatory framework for the shipping industry that is fair and effective, universally adopted and implemented.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IMO Headquarters:** 4 Albert Embankment, London, United Kingdom.
> **Established:** 17 March 1948 (convention adopted); began functioning in 1958.
> **Official Name Before 1982:** Inter-Governmental Maritime Consultative Organization (IMCO).
> **Member States:** Over 175 Member States + 3 Associate Members.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the IMO as the **"United Nations of the Sea."** Just as traffic rules govern roads to prevent accidents, the IMO creates maritime "traffic rules" — but for ships. Imagine every car manufacturer could make cars with any brake system they like, with no agreed standard. That would be chaos. IMO solves this for ships: it sets the **minimum safety, stability, and pollution rules** every ship on international voyages must follow, no matter which country owns it.

### 1.2 Classification Societies

A **Classification Society** (also called a "Class Society" or simply "Class") is a non-governmental organization that establishes and applies technical standards to the design, construction, and operation of ships and offshore structures. They certify that a vessel is "in class," meaning it has been built and maintained according to the society's rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Function:** Classification = "A passport of seaworthiness" issued to a ship.
> **Key Principle:** Classification societies do **NOT** replace flag state or port state authority; they act as **delegated technical advisors** to governments.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A classification society is like a **"certified mechanic for ships."** Just as a car needs regular fitness certification, a ship needs to prove it is structurally and mechanically sound. The classification society's "Class Surveyor" inspects the ship annually, much like an authorized service center inspects your car. The famous "wheel mark" or class symbol on a ship's hull is the **"fitness certificate sticker"** of the maritime world.

### 1.3 Government Authorities

**Government Authorities** in the maritime context refer to the national regulatory bodies that enforce international and domestic maritime law. The three principal categories are:

| Authority Type | Jurisdiction | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Flag State** | Ships flying its national flag | Owns the ship on the regulatory register; issues certificates |
| **Port State** | Ships entering its ports | Inspects foreign ships for compliance |
| **Coastal State** | Ships in its territorial waters | Enforces laws in waters under its jurisdiction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Flag State Jurisdiction:** A ship is generally subject to the laws of the country whose flag it flies. This is the **foundational principle** of international maritime law (UNCLOS, Article 92).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a driver's license. Your license is issued by **Flag State** (your home country). When you drive in a foreign country, that country's **Port State** police can pull you over and check your license and vehicle. The rules of the road are set by the **IMO equivalent** (an international convention), and the country you're driving in enforces them. If you break a rule, the port state can fine you, even though you're not a citizen.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IMO: Structural Breakdown

The IMO operates through five main Committees and a Sub-Committee structure, supported by a Secretariat headed by the **Secretary-General**.

#### 2.1.1 IMO Committees (Governing Structure)

- **Maritime Safety Committee (MSC)** – Highest technical body for safety-related conventions.
- **Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC)** – Promulgates MARPOL and environmental rules.
- **Legal Committee** – Handles liability, compensation, and jurisdictional issues.
- **Technical Cooperation Committee** – Aids developing states to implement IMO rules.
- **Facilitation Committee** – Simplifies port formalities and documentation.

#### 2.1.2 Key IMO Conventions Related to Ship Stability

The IMO conventions are binding international treaties, ratified by member states, which then become part of their national law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Maritime Law:** UNCLOS > IMO Conventions > National Law of Flag State > Port State Enforcement.

**A. International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974**
- **Most important convention** for ship stability.
- Chapter II-1 (Construction – Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations):
  - Part A: General (Regulations 1–15)
  - Part B: Subdivision and stability (Regulations 4–28)
  - Part B-1: Stability and stability-related regulations (Regulations 35–39)
  - **Regulation 38** – **Stability information to be supplied to the master** (mandatory stability booklet).
  - **Regulation 39** – Subdivision and stability requirements for cargo ships.
- **Stability-related SOLAS regulations cover:** subdivision of damaged ships, residual stability after damage, weather criterion, and the operational booklet.

**B. International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL / LL66), 1966**
- Sets the **minimum freeboard** a ship may load to.
- Freeboard is the distance from the waterline to the uppermost continuous deck (freeboard deck).
- Determines the **Load Line (Plimsoll Line)** markings.
- Linked to stability because insufficient freeboard implies insufficient reserve buoyancy and stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plimsoll Mark:** The disk-and-line symbol on the ship's side. Letters denote load lines:
> - **TF** – Tropical Fresh Water
> - **F** – Fresh Water
> - **T** – Tropical Seawater
> - **S** – Summer Seawater (the main reference line)
> - **W** – Winter Seawater
> - **WNA** – Winter North Atlantic

**C. International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL), 73/78**
- Less directly related to stability but relevant for **tankers** (oil outflow calculations are stability-dependent).

**D. International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping (STCW)**
- Ensures crew competence — indirectly linked to operational stability (e.g., understanding the stability booklet).

**E. International Convention on Tonnage Measurement (ITC 69)**
- Establishes the gross and net tonnage — basis for fee, manning, and regulation thresholds.

**F. International Safety Management (ISM) Code**
- Imposes safety management systems on shipping companies — **operational stability compliance.**

#### 2.1.3 IMO Stability Code (Resolution A.749(18))

Although **SOLAS** sets the framework, IMO also issues **non-mandatory Codes** and **Recommendations** which often become de facto mandatory through class adoption. The most important for stability:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code on Intact Stability for All Types of Ships Covered by IMO Instruments (Resolution A.749(18), 1995)**
> - The **"mother code"** of intact stability criteria.
> - Includes criteria for passenger ships, cargo ships, fishing vessels, and special-purpose ships.
> - **Superseded** for many vessel types by the **2008 IS Code** (Intact Stability Code), Resolution MSC.267(85), which consolidates A.749(18) and A.167(ES.IV).

**Key Stability Criteria from IMO A.749(18) / IS Code 2008:**

| Criterion | Symbol | Formula | Min. Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial GM | $\overline{GM}$ | $KM - KG$ | $\geq 0.15 \text{ m}$ (general) |
| Maximum righting lever | $GZ_{max}$ | From GZ curve | $\geq 0.20 \text{ m}$ at angle $\geq 30°$ |
| Angle of maximum GZ | $\theta_{max}$ | From GZ curve | $\geq 30°$, preferably $\geq 35°$ |
| Area under GZ curve up to 30° | $A_{30}$ | $\int_0^{30} GZ\,d\theta$ | $\geq 0.055 \text{ m·rad}$ |
| Area under GZ curve 30°–40° | $A_{40}$ | $\int_{30}^{40} GZ\,d\theta$ | $\geq 0.030 \text{ m·rad}$ |
| Area 30°–40° + leakage area | $A_{30-40} + A_{f}$ | — | $\geq 0.090 \text{ m·rad}$ |
| Weather Criterion (Heeling Moment) | — | $b \cdot k \cdot B$ | $GZ \geq$ weather heeling lever |
| Severe wind and rolling criterion (for vessels $< 100 \text{ m}$) | — | MSC.1/Circ.1200 | Compliance with graphical diagram |

> [!NOTE]
> **Knee Points and Downflooding Angles:**
> - **Downflooding angle ($\theta_f$):** the angle at which seawater enters any opening (hatches, ventilators, etc.) below the freeboard deck.
> - GZ curve must show **positive values up to $\theta_f$**.
> - **Range of positive stability** (between angle of vanishing stability and angle of downflooding) must be **$\geq 60°$** if downflooding is the limiting factor, or **$\geq 90°$** if vanishing stability is the limiting factor.

### 2.2 Classification Societies: Detailed Working

#### 2.2.1 Role and Function

Classification societies perform four main functions:

1. **Establish technical rules** for ship design, construction, and maintenance.
2. **Survey ships during construction** to verify compliance with rules.
3. **Conduct periodical surveys** of ships in service to maintain class.
4. **Issue certificates** that governments may accept as evidence of compliance with international conventions (delegation of statutory work).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The IACS — International Association of Classification Societies:**
> Founded 1969. As of 2024, comprises **12 member societies** covering approximately **94% of world tonnage**. IACS members consult each other to develop unified interpretation (UI) of SOLAS and other IMO rules.

**IACS Member Societies (alphabetical order):**

- **ABS** – American Bureau of Shipping (USA)
- **BV** – Bureau Veritas (France)
- **CCS** – China Classification Society
- **CRS** – Croatian Register of Shipping
- **DNV** – Det Norske Veritas (Norway) — *merged 2013 with Germanischer Lloyd to form DNV GL, rebranded DNV in 2021*
- **IRS** – Indian Register of Shipping
- **KR** – Korean Register of Shipping
- **LR** – Lloyd's Register (UK)
- **NK** – ClassNK – Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (Japan)
- **PRS** – Polish Register of Shipping
- **RINA** – Registro Italiano Navale (Italy)
- **RS** – Russian Maritime Register of Shipping

#### 2.2.2 Class Rules Related to Stability

Classification societies publish their own **"Rules and Regulations"** for ship stability, which generally reference IMO Resolutions but add **stricter** or more **detailed** requirements.

**Common Class Stability Requirements:**

- **Part C / Part 2 of Class Rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register): Strength and stability requirements.
- **Stability Booklet Approval:** Class reviews and approves the ship's trim and stability booklet, including loading conditions and KG limits.
- **Loading Conditions Tested:**
  - Homogeneous loading (full and part)
  - Alternate loading (heavy lifts)
  - Ballast conditions
  - Departure, mid-voyage, and arrival conditions
  - For tankers: Sagging and hogging conditions.
- **Damage Stability:** Class rules often require compliance with SOLAS Chapter II-1 subdivision regulations; class performs deterministic damage stability analysis (probabilistic method for tankers per MARPOL Annex I Reg. 23).
- **Special Service Notations:** Vessels carrying timber deck cargo, grain, container lashing, etc., are checked against class-specific service stability rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Class Surveys Cycle (Typical):**
> 1. **Annual Survey** – Every year.
> 2. **Intermediate Survey** – Between 2nd and 3rd annual surveys.
> 3. **Special Survey (Class Renewal)** – Every **5 years** — major survey, similar to a complete recommissioning.
> 4. **Continuous Survey** – Some components surveyed continuously on a 5-year cycle instead of as a single dry-dock event.

### 2.3 Government Authorities: Extended Framework

#### 2.3.1 Flag State Administration

The **flag state** is the country where the ship is registered. It has the primary duty under UNCLOS Article 94 to administer safety, pollution prevention, and seafarer standards.

**Flag State Functions:**
- Maintains the **ship register** (Open, Closed, or International Register).
- Issues **statutory certificates** (Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Safety Radio, Load Line, MARPOL, etc.).
- Conducts or delegates surveys for the issuance of these certificates.
- Investigates casualties and detentions.
- May delegate survey work to a classification society (called a **"Recognized Organization" or RO**).

**Example Flag State Authorities:**
- **MCA (UK)** – Maritime and Coastguard Agency.
- **USCG (USA)** – United States Coast Guard.
- **DG Shipping (India)** – Directorate General of Shipping, Ministry of Shipping.
- **MSA China** – Maritime Safety Administration of the People's Republic of China.
- **MLIT Japan** – Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism.

#### 2.3.2 Port State Control (PSC)

Under UNCLOS and various regional agreements, port states can inspect foreign ships in their ports for compliance.

**Major PSC Regimes (MoU – Memorandum of Understanding):**
- **Paris MoU** – Europe and North Atlantic (1982).
- **Tokyo MoU** – Asia-Pacific.
- **Indian Ocean MoU** – 2013.
- **Vina del Mar MoU** – Latin America.
- **Caribbean MoU** – Caribbean region.
- **Abuja MoU** – West and Central Africa.
- **Mediterranean MoU (Med MoU)** – Mediterranean.
- **Black Sea MoU** – Black Sea.
- **Riyadh MoU** – Gulf States.
- **Helsinki Convention** – Baltic Sea (specialized).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequences of PSC Deficiencies:**
> - Minor deficiencies: Rectify before departure.
> - Major deficiencies: Detain ship; cannot sail until corrected.
> - Three detentions in 12 months → Ship may be **banned** from the MoU region.

**PSC Inspection Focus on Stability:**
- Stability booklet on board and understood by master.
- Stability information is up to date.
- Loading computer (where required) operational and accurate.
- Ballast water management records.
- Damage control plans and stability after flooding.

#### 2.3.3 Coastal State Rights

Under UNCLOS, a coastal state has:
- **Internal waters** – full sovereignty.
- **Territorial sea (12 nm)** – sovereignty, subject to innocent passage.
- **Contiguous zone (24 nm)** – limited control.
- **Exclusive Economic Zone (200 nm)** – rights over resources.

Coastal states also enforce **SOLAS Reg. V/10** – **Ships' routeing** — and may impose traffic separation schemes in their waters.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Key Formula / Definition | Unit / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Freeboard | $F$ | $F = D - d$ where $D$ is moulded depth, $d$ is draft | m |
| Reserve Buoyancy | $RB$ | $\frac{\nabla_{max} - \nabla_{load}}{\nabla_{max}} \times 100$ | % |
| Initial GM | $GM$ | $KB + BM - KG$ | m; min **0.15 m** |
| BM (transverse) | $BM_T$ | $\frac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | m |
| BM (longitudinal) | $BM_L$ | $\frac{I_L}{\nabla}$ | m |
| Metacentric Height | $GM$ | $KM - KG$ | m |
| GZ at angle $\phi$ | $GZ(\phi)$ | $KN \sin\phi - KG \sin\phi$ | m |
| Righting Lever | $GZ$ | $GZ = \sin\phi \cdot (KM - KG)$ | m |
| Range of Positive Stability | — | $\theta_v - \theta_u$ | $\geq 60°$ to 90° |
| Weather Heeling Lever | $\ell_w$ | $\frac{P \cdot A \cdot H \cdot \cos\phi}{g \cdot \Delta}$ | m |
| Allowable Heeling Lever | $\ell_a$ | From IS Code tables | m |
| Downflooding Angle | $\theta_f$ | Angle of first seawater entry | degrees |
| Angle of Vanishing Stability | $\theta_v$ | GZ = 0 again | degrees |
| Trim | $t$ | Trim = Draft_Aft $-$ Draft_Fwd | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Pipe Rule:** All absolute value bars ($\vert x \vert$) are rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because this topic is **regulatory and conceptual** in nature, the "derivations" here are **regulatory derivation chains** — how a stability requirement flows from the UN → IMO Convention → Class Rule → National Law → Operational Practice. We also provide a numerical worked example of weather criterion verification, as it is a frequent KTU exam question.

### 3.1 The Regulatory Derivation Chain (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Universal Framework (UNCLOS, 1982)**
UNCLOS establishes the principle that ships have a nationality (Article 91) and that every state shall fix conditions for granting its nationality (Article 91(1)). The flag state has the duty to ensure safety at sea with regard to, *inter alia*, the **construction, equipment, and seaworthiness of ships** (Article 94(3)(a)).

**Step 2 — International Treaty Layer (IMO Conventions)**
UNCLOS directs states to "conform to generally accepted international regulations" — these are the IMO conventions. The principal instrument for ship stability is **SOLAS 1974, Chapter II-1, Part B-1**, which provides the binding framework for intact and damage stability.

**Step 3 — Operational Codes and Recommendations (Codes)**
Within the SOLAS mandate, IMO issues codes with technical details. For intact stability, the principal document is the **IS Code 2008 (Resolution MSC.267(85))**. It specifies numerical criteria (GM, GZ areas, weather criterion, severe wind and rolling).

**Step 4 — National Law (Flag State)**
A flag state enacts national law to "translate" SOLAS into enforceable domestic regulations.
- *Example: India — Merchant Shipping Act, 1958, plus the Rules and Regulations under it.*
- The flag state also nominates a **Recognized Organization (RO)** — usually a classification society — to perform surveys and certification on its behalf.

**Step 5 — Classification Rules**
The class society's own rules (e.g., Lloyd's Register "Right Ship" stability rules) often go beyond SOLAS minima, providing additional, voluntary requirements. Class reviews the design, approves the stability booklet, and conducts surveys.

**Step 6 — Operational Practice**
The ship is built to class rules, certified by the flag state (often via class), and the master operates it using the approved stability booklet. Port state control enforces on foreign ships.

> [!NOTE]
> This **six-layer pyramid** is the key to understanding how a stability rule in a textbook ultimately reaches the ship.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Weather Criterion (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement (simulated KTU pattern):**
A container ship has the following particulars:
- Displacement: $\Delta = 25{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$
- Beam: $B = 32.0 \text{ m}$
- Wind pressure: $P = 1000 \text{ N/m}^2$
- Windage area: $A = 1500 \text{ m}^2$
- Height of the centroid of the windage area above the waterline: $H = 8.5 \text{ m}$
- Heeling angle: $\phi = 30°$

Compute the **weather heeling lever** and verify whether the ship meets the IMO weather criterion (assuming the allowable heeling lever at $30°$ from the IS Code is $\ell_a = 0.45 \text{ m}$).

#### Step 3.2.1 — Identify Known Quantities

$$
\Delta = 25{,}000 \text{ t}, \quad B = 32.0 \text{ m}, \quad P = 1000 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

$$
A = 1500 \text{ m}^2, \quad H = 8.5 \text{ m}, \quad \phi = 30°, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

#### Step 3.2.2 — Recall the Weather Heeling Lever Formula

Per IMO IS Code (Annex 1, Section 3.2), the heeling lever due to a steady wind is:

$$
\ell_w = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot H \cdot \cos\phi}{g \cdot \Delta}
$$

#### Step 3.2.3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

First, compute the numerator:

$$
P \cdot A \cdot H \cdot \cos\phi = 1000 \times 1500 \times 8.5 \times \cos 30°
$$

Since $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$:

$$
1000 \times 1500 = 1{,}500{,}000
$$

$$
1{,}500{,}000 \times 8.5 = 12{,}750{,}000
$$

$$
12{,}750{,}000 \times 0.8660 = 11{,}041{,}500 \text{ N·m}
$$

Now compute the denominator:

$$
g \cdot \Delta = 9.81 \times 25{,}000 = 245{,}250 \text{ kN (since tonnes-force × g gives N, but here we treat Δ in tonnes as mass for direct lever computation as is conventional in IMO formula)}
$$

Actually, the correct way per IS Code: $\Delta$ in tonnes is mass; multiplied by $g$ gives weight in kN:

$$
g \cdot \Delta = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 25{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 245{,}250 \text{ kN}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\ell_w = \frac{11{,}041{,}500 \text{ N·m}}{245{,}250 \text{ kN}} = \frac{11{,}041.5 \text{ kN·m}}{245{,}250 \text{ kN}} = 0.0450 \text{ m}
$$

#### Step 3.2.4 — Compare with Allowable Value

$$
\ell_w = 0.045 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \ell_a = 0.45 \text{ m}
$$

Since $\ell_w \ll \ell_a$, the ship easily satisfies the weather criterion. (The allowable heeling lever is the **minimum** GZ the ship must produce at angle $\phi$ under wind action.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** In the IMO weather criterion, the rule is **$GZ \geq \ell_w$**. So the criterion is *not* that the weather lever must be less than the allowable; rather, the allowable heeling lever is a **prescribed minimum GZ** at a specific angle (e.g., 0.20 m at 30° for some ships). The verification is $GZ_{\text{at 30°}} \geq \ell_w$.

#### Step 3.2.5 — Revised Final Verification

In the standard IS Code procedure (Annex 1, Reg. 3.2.2):

$$
GZ_{\text{at angle }\phi} \geq \ell_w
$$

The "allowable heeling lever" $\ell_a$ in some class interpretations is a **minimum** GZ that must always exceed the calculated $\ell_w$ for compliance. Here:

$$
GZ_{\text{at }30°} = 0.45 \text{ m (assumed)} \geq \ell_w = 0.045 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The ship **passes** the weather criterion comfortably.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stability Criteria Cross-Reference

| Criterion | SOLAS Reference | IS Code Reference | Class Typical | KTU Common Question? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Min. GM | Reg. 27 (Cargo Ships) | Ch. 2, 2.1.1 | 0.15 m | **Yes (3-mark)** |
| Max GZ ≥ 0.20 m at ≥ 30° | — | Ch. 2, 2.3.2 | Same | **Yes (14-mark)** |
| Area 0–30° ≥ 0.055 m·rad | — | Ch. 2, 2.3.1 | Same | **Yes (14-mark)** |
| Area 0–40° ≥ 0.090 m·rad | — | Ch. 2, 2.3.1 | Same | **Yes (14-mark)** |
| Range of Positive Stability | — | Ch. 2, 2.3.3 | Same | Yes (3-mark) |
| Weather Criterion | — | Annex 1, 3.2 | Same | **Yes (14-mark)** |
| Severe Wind & Rolling | MSC.1/Circ.1200 | — | Adopted | Yes (advanced) |
| Grain Stability | SOLAS Ch. VI/Reg. 5, 7, 8 | — | Class Grain Rules | Yes (specialized) |
| Damage Stability (Passenger) | SOLAS Ch. II-1, Part B | — | — | Yes (advanced) |
| Damage Stability (Cargo) | SOLAS Ch. II-1, Part B-1 | — | — | Yes (advanced) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Maritime Regulatory Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[UNCLOS 1982 - United Nations Convention on Law of the Sea]
    L1[IMO Conventions - SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, STCW]
    L2[IMO Codes and Recommendations - IS Code 2008, MSC Circulars]
    L3[Flag State National Law - e.g. Merchant Shipping Act 1958 India]
    L4[Recognized Organization - Classification Society delegated by Flag State]
    L5[Operational Application - Master uses stability booklet in real time]
    L6[Port State Control - Inspects foreign ships in port]
    L7[Coastal State - Enforces routeing in territorial waters]
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L3 --> L6
    L3 --> L7
    L6 -.feedback.-> L4
    L7 -.feedback.-> L4
```

### 4.2 Functional Block: IACS and Member Society Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMO[IMO]
    IACS[IACS Headquarters London]
    IMO --> IACS
    IACS --> ABS[ABS USA]
    IACS --> BV[BV France]
    IACS --> CCS[CCS China]
    IACS --> DNV[DNV Norway]
    IACS --> IRS[IRS India]
    IACS --> KR[KR Korea]
    IACS --> LR[LR UK]
    IACS --> NK[ClassNK Japan]
    IACS --> RINA[RINA Italy]
    IACS --> RS[RS Russia]
    IACS --> PRS[PRS Poland]
    IACS --> CRS[CRS Croatia]
    IACS --> FlagState[Flag State Authorities]
    FlagState --> RO[Recognized Organization RO - typically one Class Society]
    RO --> Ship[Ship - Surveyed and Certified]
    Ship --> PSC[Port State Control MoUs]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Stability Booklet Approval Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ship Design Stage - Naval Architect calculates KG, GM, GZ]
    B[Prepare Trim and Stability Booklet incl. loading conditions]
    C[Submit to Class Society for Approval]
    D{Class Review}
    E[Approved Booklet returned to Shipyard]
    F[Flag State Issues Stability Letter]
    G[Booklet placed on board Ship]
    H[Master receives Stability Information]
    I[Master computes loading every voyage]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -->|Meets IMO + Class rules| E
    D -->|Deficiencies| Rev[Revise Calculations]
    Rev --> C
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

```mermaid
    PSCH[Port State Control MoU Network]
    PSCH --> PMoU[Paris MoU - Europe and N Atlantic]
    PSCH --> TMoU[Tokyo MoU - Asia Pacific]

```

### 4.5 Plimsoll Mark Diagram (ASCII Schematic)

```
   TF (Tropical Fresh)
    \__________________
    |    F (Fresh)    |   Reference
    |_________________|  ____________ Ship's hull side
    |  T (Tropical S) |
    |    S (SUMMER)   |   <- Reference line for Salt Water Summer
    |    W (Winter)   |
    |_________________|
        WNA (Winter N. Atlantic)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Plimsoll Mark is a **physical realization** of the **Load Lines Convention** on every ship — connecting the abstract regulatory document to the actual painted line on the hull.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the International Maritime Organization? List any two of its main committees.**

**Model Answer:**

The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** is a specialized United Nations agency responsible for improving the safety, security, and environmental performance of international shipping. It develops and maintains a comprehensive regulatory framework for shipping, with the primary aim of ensuring safety of life at sea and protection of the marine environment.

Two main committees of the IMO are:

1. **Maritime Safety Committee (MSC):** Deals with all matters relating to ship safety, including SOLAS convention amendments and the IS Code.
2. **Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC):** Promulgates regulations for the prevention of pollution from ships, including the MARPOL Convention.

(Any two of MSC, MEPC, Legal, Technical Cooperation, Facilitation may be given full credit.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse IMO with IACS. IMO is a **UN inter-governmental body** (it makes *laws*); IACS is a **non-governmental association** of classification societies (it makes *technical rules*). Do not interchange them.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define 'Classification Society' and mention any two major classification societies with their countries.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Classification Society** is a non-governmental organization that establishes and applies technical standards for the design, construction, and operation of ships and offshore structures. It certifies a ship as "in class" by performing surveys, ensuring compliance with its rules, and acting as a technical advisor to flag states (a "Recognized Organization").

Two major classification societies:

1. **Lloyd's Register (LR)** – United Kingdom.
2. **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** – United States of America.

(Any other valid members of IACS such as BV, DNV, NK, RINA, KR, etc. are also acceptable.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common mistake is to write that a classification society **legislates maritime law.** It does NOT. It sets *technical rules* and *surveys* against them, and is delegated by the flag state to act on its behalf for surveys. The **flag state** legislates; the **class society** surveys.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE):** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B, not both. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Module 4, Past Pattern]`

**Q(A)(a)** Explain in detail the International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974, with emphasis on Chapter II-1 requirements related to subdivision and stability. (7 Marks)

**Q(A)(b)** A bulk carrier has a displacement $\Delta = 18{,}000 \text{ t}$, beam $B = 28 \text{ m}$, and a windage area of $A = 1200 \text{ m}^2$ with centroid at $H = 7.5 \text{ m}$ above the waterline. If the wind pressure is $P = 800 \text{ N/m}^2$ and the heeling angle is $\phi = 25°$, compute the weather heeling lever. Given the allowable heeling lever at $25°$ is $0.18 \text{ m}$, verify whether the ship complies. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Q(A)(a):**

1. **Historical Background of SOLAS:**
   - First SOLAS adopted 1914 in response to Titanic disaster (1912).
   - 1960 version was the first major comprehensive treaty.
   - **1974 SOLAS** is the current, living instrument, continuously updated with amendments.

2. **Structure of SOLAS 1974:**
   - **Chapter I — General Provisions**
   - **Chapter II-1 — Construction (Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations)**
   - **Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction**
   - **Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements**
   - **Chapter IV — Radiocommunications**
   - **Chapter V — Safety of Navigation**
   - **Chapter VI — Carriage of Cargoes and Oil Fuels**
   - **Chapter VII — Carriage of Dangerous Goods**
   - **Chapter VIII — Nuclear Ships**
   - **Chapter IX — Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)**
   - **Chapter X — Safety Measures for High-Speed Craft**
   - **Chapter XI-1 — Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety**
   - **Chapter XI-2 — Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Security (ISPS Code)**
   - **Chapter XII — Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers**

3. **SOLAS Chapter II-1 — Stability & Subdivision:**
   - **Part A — General** (Regulations 1–15): Definitions, application, etc.
   - **Part B — Subdivision and Stability** (Regulations 4–28): Damage stability requirements for passenger ships; subdivision length, permeability, etc.
   - **Part B-1 — Stability** (Regulations 35–39): Intact stability for all ships; the **mandatory stability booklet** (Reg. 38), and subdivision for cargo ships (Reg. 39).
   - **Regulation 38 — Stability Information:** Every passenger and cargo ship must be provided with approved stability information. Master must be supplied with the booklet containing sufficient information to enable him to load and operate the ship safely.
   - **Regulation 39 — Subdivision and Damage Stability of Cargo Ships:** Defines the `s`-factor subdivision index for cargo ships ≥ 100 m and 80 m.

4. **Significance for Ship Stability:**
   - Sets the **mandatory legal baseline** for intact and damage stability.
   - All other codes (IS Code, Class rules) are subordinate or supplementary.
   - Compliance is required for **all international voyages**.

**Model Solution for Q(A)(b):**

**Given:**
$\Delta = 18{,}000 \text{ t}, \; A = 1200 \text{ m}^2, \; H = 7.5 \text{ m}, \; P = 800 \text{ N/m}^2, \; \phi = 25°$

**Step 1:** Recall the weather heeling lever formula:

$$
\ell_w = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot H \cdot \cos\phi}{g \cdot \Delta}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\cos 25°$:

$$
\cos 25° = 0.9063
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator:

$$
P \cdot A \cdot H \cdot \cos\phi = 800 \times 1200 \times 7.5 \times 0.9063
$$

$$
800 \times 1200 = 960{,}000
$$

$$
960{,}000 \times 7.5 = 7{,}200{,}000
$$

$$
7{,}200{,}000 \times 0.9063 = 6{,}525{,}360 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator:

$$
g \cdot \Delta = 9.81 \times 18{,}000 = 176{,}580 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5:** Weather heeling lever:

$$
\ell_w = \frac{6{,}525.36 \text{ kN·m}}{176{,}580 \text{ kN}} = 0.0369 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare:

$$
GZ_{\text{at 25°}} \geq \ell_w \quad \text{(requirement)}
$$

Assuming ship's $GZ_{25°} = 0.20 \text{ m} \geq \ell_w = 0.037 \text{ m}$. **Complies** ✓

**Note:** The "allowable heeling lever" in the IS Code (Annex 1, 3.2.2) is actually a **minimum GZ** required at each angle of heeling. Provided $GZ_{25°} = 0.18 \text{ m}$ (or more), the ship satisfies the criterion comfortably.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **allowable heeling lever** $\ell_a$ with the **weather heeling lever** $\ell_w$. The criterion is: **$GZ(\phi) \geq \ell_w(\phi)$**. You do NOT need to compare $\ell_w$ directly to $\ell_a$ unless the class society has reformulated the criterion that way. Always present the comparison in terms of GZ vs $\ell_w$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Module 4, Past Pattern]`

**Q(B)(a)** What are Classification Societies? Explain their functions, with special reference to the stability rules followed by them. (7 Marks)

**Q(B)(b)** Discuss the role of the Flag State, Port State, and Coastal State authorities in enforcing ship stability regulations. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Q(B)(a):**

1. **Definition:** Classification societies are non-governmental organizations that develop and apply technical rules for ship design, construction, and operation, certifying that a ship is "in class" when it complies with these rules.

2. **Major Classification Societies:** Lloyd's Register (UK), American Bureau of Shipping (USA), Bureau Veritas (France), Det Norske Veritas (Norway), ClassNK (Japan), RINA (Italy), Korean Register, etc. IACS coordinates them.

3. **Functions of Classification Societies:**
   - **Rule-making:** Develop detailed technical rules for hull, machinery, and stability.
   - **Surveying:** Conduct initial and periodical surveys of ships (annual, intermediate, special/renewal).
   - **Certification:** Issue class certificates (e.g., "Lloyd's Register 100A1" notation).
   - **Delegated Statutory Work:** Act as Recognized Organization (RO) for flag states to perform SOLAS, MARPOL, and Load Line surveys.
   - **Technical Advisory:** Provide expert opinion to shipowners, designers, and flag states.
   - **R&D and Innovation:** Develop rules for novel ship types (LNG, autonomous, polar, etc.).

4. **Stability Rules Followed by Classification Societies:**
   - **Part C of the Class Rules** typically covers stability, subdivision, and load lines.
   - **Class requirements often more stringent than IMO minima** — they add their own criteria for special vessel types (tankers, bulk carriers, container ships).
   - **Trim and Stability Booklet:** Class reviews and approves. It contains:
     - General particulars and hydrostatic data.
     - Cross curves of stability (KN curves).
     - Loading conditions (homogeneous, alternate, ballast, departure/arrival).
     - Tank sounding tables, hydrostatic tables, KG limits.
     - Example loading conditions.
   - **Damage stability analysis:** Class performs deterministic and probabilistic (for tankers) damage stability calculations.
   - **Class notations:** Special service notations like "ESP" (Enhanced Survey Programme) for tankers, "BC-A" for bulk carriers, "CSR" for Common Structural Rules, "LNG" for gas carriers.
   - **Operational compliance:** The master is required to plan loading using the booklet; class periodically verifies continued compliance.

**Model Solution for Q(B)(b):**

1. **Flag State (Primary Responsibility):**
   - The state whose flag the ship is flying is the principal regulatory authority (UNCLOS Article 94).
   - Issues statutory certificates: Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Load Line, MARPOL, Minimum Safe Manning, etc.
   - Maintains the official ship register.
   - Enforces safety of life, property, and prevention of pollution on its flagged ships.
   - Investigates casualties and detentions of its flagged ships.
   - May delegate survey and certification functions to a Recognized Organization (RO), typically a classification society, but the flag state remains legally responsible.

2. **Port State Control (PSC) (Inspection on Entry):**
   - A state may inspect foreign ships visiting its ports to verify compliance with international conventions.
   - Conducted under regional MoUs: Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU, etc.
   - **Initial inspection** covers certificates, general condition, equipment, and stability-related documents (e.g., approved stability booklet, loading computer).
   - If deficiencies are found:
     - Minor: rectifiable before next voyage.
     - Major: detention — ship cannot sail.
   - PSC officers are authorized to check that the actual loading condition matches the calculated condition in the stability booklet.
   - Multiple detentions can lead to banning from the MoU region.

3. **Coastal State (Jurisdiction in Territorial Waters):**
   - Has full sovereignty in its internal waters and territorial sea (12 nm).
   - Enforces laws regarding **routeing, traffic separation schemes, and pollution prevention** in its waters.
   - In its **Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ, 200 nm)**, the coastal state has jurisdiction over resource-related and pollution matters.
   - Coastal state can take action against ships violating international rules in its waters, including detention or even arrest of the ship for serious breaches.

4. **Relationship and Mutual Reinforcement:**
   - Flag state has **continuous** jurisdiction; port state has **periodic**; coastal state has **spatial**.
   - PSC inspections, in effect, are a safety net catching flag states that fail to enforce their own rules.
   - This three-tiered system ensures that even on the high seas, ships are held accountable to international standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common student error is to say that **"the classification society is the regulator."** It is NOT. The classification society is a **technical service provider** that acts as a **Recognized Organization (RO)** on behalf of the flag state. The **flag state is the legal regulator.** Class is the auditor; the flag state is the lawmaker.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Conflating IMO and IACS:** IMO is the UN body; IACS is the non-governmental association of class societies. They have different roles.
> 2. **Treating Classification Society as a regulator:** Class is a service provider/RO, not a lawmaker.
> 3. **Forgetting IACS membership:** Many students don't mention that major class societies are IACS members covering ~94% of world tonnage.
> 4. **Mixing up MoU regions:** Paris MoU = Europe/N.Atlantic; Tokyo MoU = Asia-Pacific; Indian Ocean MoU = recent (2013). Don't confuse them.
> 5. **Weather criterion formula misapplication:** Always include $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ in the denominator; otherwise, units break.
> 6. **Skipping "Flag State" definition:** Always cite UNCLOS Article 94.
> 7. **Plimsoll Mark mistake:** S is **Summer** (not Standard); TF is **Tropical Fresh** (not Tropical Foul).
> 8. **IS Code misnumbering:** It is **MSC.267(85)** for 2008 IS Code; **A.749(18)** is the older code (still referenced for many ships).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **IMO** = United Nations specialized agency for maritime safety, security, and pollution. HQ: London. Established: 1948. Operative: 1958. Old name: IMCO.
- ✅ IMO's main instruments for stability = **SOLAS 1974 (Chapter II-1)** and the **IS Code 2008 (MSC.267(85))**.
- ✅ **Load Lines Convention (LL66, 1966)** defines freeboard, Plimsoll Mark, and is directly linked to stability.
- ✅ **MARPOL** is for pollution control, with overlap on tanker damage stability.
- ✅ **STCW** is for crew competence; **ISM Code** is for safety management.
- ✅ **Classification Society** = non-governmental, sets rules, surveys ships, certifies "in class," acts as **RO** for flag state.
- ✅ **IACS** = International Association of Classification Societies; ~12 members covering ~94% of world tonnage.
- ✅ **Flag State** = country of registry; primary responsibility for ship safety (UNCLOS Art. 94).
- ✅ **Port State** = can inspect foreign ships under MoUs; major MoUs: Paris, Tokyo, Indian Ocean, Vina del Mar, etc.
- ✅ **Coastal State** = enforces rules in its territorial waters (12 nm) and EEZ (200 nm).
- ✅ Key IMO intact stability criteria: $GM \geq 0.15 \text{ m}$, $GZ_{max} \geq 0.20 \text{ m}$ at $\theta \geq 30°$, $A_{30} \geq 0.055 \text{ m·rad}$, $A_{40} \geq 0.090 \text{ m·rad}$, range of positive stability $\geq 60°$ (with downflooding) or $\geq 90°$ (without).
- ✅ **Weather criterion** formula: $\ell_w = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot H \cdot \cos\phi}{g \cdot \Delta}$. Must be $\leq GZ(\phi)$.
- ✅ **Plimsoll Mark letters:** TF, F, T, S, W, WNA (from highest to lowest draft).
- ✅ The **trim and stability booklet** is **mandatory** under SOLAS Reg. 38 and is reviewed by class and approved by flag state.
- ✅ The **regulatory pyramid:** UNCLOS → IMO Convention → IMO Code/Recommendation → Flag State Law → Class Rules → Operational Practice.
- ✅ Always distinguish **statutory** certificates (issued by flag state) from **class** certificates (issued by classification society).
- ✅ **Most exam-favoured KTU topics from this module:** (1) IMO structure and SOLAS chapters, (2) IS Code criteria (numerical), (3) Weather criterion numerical, (4) Class society role, (5) PSC MoUs, (6) Flag/Port/Coastal state distinction.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST304 - SHIP STABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intact and Damage Stability Rules, Stability Criteria in Weather Condition, Stability Booklet, Suggestion on Project Topic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — SHIP STABILITY REGULATIONS & DOCUMENTATION

Welcome to the regulatory backbone of ship stability. Up to Module 3, we studied *how* a ship behaves — transverse GM, free surface effect, dynamical stability, etc. Module 4 answers the crucial follow-up question: *Who decides that the ship is "safe enough" to sail, and by what numerical rule?* This module connects classical naval architecture to the **legal framework of the IMO (International Maritime Organization)** through SOLAS, the **Weather Criterion**, the **Stability Booklet**, and finally, **student-level project topics** that can be taken up for the semester mini-project / final-year thesis.

---

## 1.1 Intact Stability Rules — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Intact Stability Rules):**
> Intact stability rules are a codified set of mandatory and recommendatory criteria, issued by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **SOLAS Chapter II-1** and codified in detail by **Resolution A.749(18) — Code on Intact Stability for All Types of Ships Covered by IMO Instruments (1993, as amended)**. They define the *minimum acceptable levels of transverse stability* a passenger or cargo ship must satisfy under *undamaged (intact)* conditions throughout its operational life.

The rules are *not a single equation*; they are a *checklist* of seven criteria that the area under the GZ-curve and the metacentric height must satisfy simultaneously. Failure of *any one* criterion is grounds for the vessel to be declared unfit to proceed to sea.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Driving Test"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of intact stability rules as a **driving test checklist** for a car. To get a license you don't just need to drive well — you must satisfy: (1) parallel parking, (2) reverse driving, (3) signal use, (4) speed control, etc. Similarly, a ship must satisfy *all seven IMO criteria* — having a large GM alone is not sufficient. The rules test the ship under several "manoeuvres" (steering, turning, wind, crowding, towing, head-sea, etc.) and the ship must pass *every* test, not just one.

### Who Enforces These Rules?

- **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** — The mother convention. Chapter II-1, Regulation 5–7.
- **Resolution A.749(18) (1993)** — Detailed intact stability criteria.
- **Resolution A.601(15) — Weather Criterion** (later superseded by A.749(18) Ch.3).
- **MARPOL / Load Line / Flag-State Administrations** — local enforcement.
- **Classification Societies** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS) — verify compliance during design.

---

## 1.2 Damage Stability Rules — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Damage Stability Rules):**
> Damage stability rules are the *post-flooding* survival criteria, predominantly codified in **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Parts B-1 to B-4**. They specify the *worst-case collision/grounding flooding scenario* a ship must withstand and still remain afloat with sufficient residual righting lever and range of positive stability to enable safe evacuation.

There are **two parallel regulatory regimes**:
1. **Deterministic (Subdivision & Damage Stability)** — SOLAS Reg. 7–8, defines a fixed *flooding length* as a function of L (ship length). Used for **passenger ships**.
2. **Probabilistic (Index 'A' / Index 'R' / Index 'S')** — SOLAS Reg. 27 for **Bulk Carriers**, Reg. 28 for **General Cargo**, and Regs. 29–30 for **Tankers**. Computes an *Attained Subdivision Index R*, which must exceed the *Required Subdivision Index R*.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Crash-Test Rating"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A car's **NCAP crash test rating** is the land equivalent of damage stability. The car is "crashed" in standardized ways (frontal, side, rollover) and graded on whether passengers can still escape. A ship is "flooded" in standardized compartments (length × breadth × depth as a function of L) and graded on whether the residual GZ-curve and range of positive stability are sufficient. Both are probabilistic/scenario-based survivability indices.

### The Core Idea of SOLAS Damage Criterion

For **passenger ships** (post-2009 amendments), the ship must satisfy, in the final stage of flooding:

| Parameter | Minimum Requirement |
|---|---|
| Angle of maximum residual GZ (θ_max) | ≥ **15°** (preferably ≥ 20°) |
| Range of positive residual stability | ≥ **16°** (for ships ≤ 80 m); **20°** for ships > 80 m in some cases |
| Residual righting lever at 20° | ≥ **0.1 m** |
| Residual righting lever between 30°–40° | ≥ **0.05 m** |
| Margin line (3.75 % of moulded breadth or 75 % of freeboard, whichever is smaller) | Not to be immersed |

For **cargo ships** (SOLAS 2009 Reg. 31), the simpler "Area-A/Range-B" criterion applies:
- **Area A** under the residual GZ-curve from 0° to 30° (with min 0.055 m·rad)
- **Range B** of positive residual stability ≥ 20°

---

## 1.3 Stability Criteria in Weather Condition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Weather Criterion):**
> The Weather Criterion (also called the **"Significant Wave Height Criterion"** or the **"Storm Condition"** criterion) is the most critical of the seven IMO intact-stability criteria. It tests the ship's ability to withstand a *gale* of force 8–10 (Beaufort scale) by comparing the **heeling arm curves** (wind + passenger crowding + rolling) against the **righting arm curve**. The criterion is codified in **Resolution A.749(18), Chapter 3, Regulation 3.2**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Tight-Rope Walker in a Storm"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a tight-rope walker in a calm breeze vs. a storm. In calm weather, even a small balancing ability (small GM) keeps them upright. But in a *gale* — the tight-rope walker faces cross-winds, gusts, and crowd shifts on the rope. The *Weather Criterion* essentially asks: *"Can the ship balance itself while being pushed sideways by a hurricane, with passengers shifting to one side, and with the ship rolling? If yes, sail; if no, the design is too tender."*

### The Two Heeling Arms the Ship Must Resist

1. **Heeling arm due to wind (l_w)** — from a steady beam wind of pressure **p = 504 N/m²** (≈ 10 m/s for 5 s — Beaufort 8–10).
2. **Heeling arm due to passenger crowding (l_p)** — all passengers suddenly moving to one side.
3. **Rolling arm (l_R)** — added to account for the *dynamic* rolling motion of the ship.

The criterion is expressed via the ratio **b / a**, where:
- **a** = area under the *wind heeling arm curve* (l_w1 curve) from the angle of steady heel (θ₀) to the angle of flooding (θ_f) or 60°, whichever is less.
- **b** = area under the *righting arm curve* (GZ) from θ₀ to the second intercept with the l_w1 curve, minus the area of the l_w1 curve in the same range.

The ship passes if **b ≥ a + 0.006 m·rad** (a small safety margin).

> **Note:** The factor **0.006 m·rad** is the IMO safety margin — it accounts for uncertainty in wind pressure, loading, and crew-free surface effect.

---

## 1.4 Stability Booklet

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Stability Booklet):**
> A Stability Booklet (officially called the *"Trim and Stability Information"* under SOLAS Reg. 22 / 23) is a **mandatory, ship-specific document** that the shipyard must provide to the master of every new ship. It contains *all hydrostatic, stability, and loading information* necessary for the master to operate the ship safely under any loading condition. It is approved by the **Flag Administration** and forms part of the ship's statutory documentation.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Aircraft Operating Manual"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Just as a pilot receives an *Aircraft Flight Manual* with every airframe — listing weight limits, centre-of-gravity envelopes, takeoff/landing speeds — the ship master receives a *Stability Booklet* with every hull. The booklet defines the *loading envelope* (min/max drafts, GM limits, shear & bending moment limits) and contains the *pre-computed curves* the master needs to plan the voyage.

### Typical Contents of a Stability Booklet (per IMO A.267/VIII)

1. General particulars (LBP, B, D, T, displacement, KG₀, LCG, VCG).
2. Hydrostatic data (form coefficients, KB, BM, MCT).
3. Cross-curves of stability (GZ vs. heel for various displacements).
4. Free-surface correction tables for each tank.
5. Tank capacity and KG tables.
6. Loading condition manuals (one-page summaries of *homologated* conditions).
7. Lightweight distribution.
8. Limiting KG / GM curves for each draft.
9. Flooding information (permeability assumed, downflooding angles, margin line).
10. Specific instructions for masters (e.g., "Do not exceed 1000 tonnes in No. 3 DB tank").
11. Example loading conditions.
12. The *Operational Information Notice* (OIN) explaining how to use the booklet.

## 2.1 Intact Stability — The Seven IMO Criteria (A.749(18))

The IMO requires a ship to satisfy **all seven** of the following criteria simultaneously:

| # | Criterion | Math Statement | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Area under GZ-curve from 0° to 30° | $A_{0\text{–}30°} \geq 0.055 \text{ m·rad}$ | $A_1$ |
| 2 | Area under GZ-curve from 0° to 40° (or flooding angle) | $A_{0\text{–}40°} \geq 0.09 \text{ m·rad}$ | $A_2$ |
| 3 | Area from 30° to 40° (or flooding) | $A_{30\text{–}40°} \geq 0.03 \text{ m·rad}$ | $A_3$ |
| 4 | Righting lever at 30° heel | $GZ_{30°} \geq 0.20 \text{ m}$ | $GZ_4$ |
| 5 | Maximum righting lever | $GZ_{\max} \geq 0.20 \text{ m}$ at $\theta \leq 25°$ (preferably $\leq 20°$) | $GZ_5$ |
| 6 | Metacentric height | $GM_0 \geq 0.15 \text{ m}$ (passenger) / 0.10 m (cargo) | $GM$ |
| 7 | **Weather Criterion** | $b \geq a + 0.006 \text{ m·rad}$ | $WC$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memorisation Tip:** The first three are *area criteria*; the next three are *lever criteria*; the last is a *dynamic criterion*. A.749(18) calls them the *Criteria 2.1 to 2.7* — examiners love to ask "List the seven intact stability criteria" for 7 marks.

### Why Each Criterion Exists — The "Why"

- **Criteria 1–3 (Areas)**: Ensure *total dynamical stability* is sufficient over the *full roll range*. A ship with high GM may have small total area (due to low-angle-only stability).
- **Criteria 4–5 (Levers)**: Ensure the ship is *not too stiff* (which causes rapid, uncomfortable rolling) and *not too tender*.
- **Criterion 6 (GM₀)**: The *first-order* stability requirement — the slope of the GZ curve at the origin.
- **Criterion 7 (Weather)**: The *real-world* test — can the ship survive a storm while heeled and rolling?

---

## 2.2 Damage Stability — Two Regulatory Regimes

### 2.2.1 Passenger Ships (SOLAS Chapter II-1, Part B-1)

The damage length is given by a *table* (or interpolation) as a function of L (ship length, in metres):

| L (m) | Damage Length (m) | Damage Transverse Extent |
|---|---|---|
| 60 | 8.4 | 0.46 B |
| 100 | 16.0 | 0.46 B |
| 200 | 25.4 | 0.46 B |
| 300 | 30.0 | 0.46 B |

**Damage Permeability Assumed (μ)**:
- Machinery spaces: **0.85**
- Accommodation: **0.95**
- Dry cargo holds: **0.90**
- Tanks: **0 or 1** (depending on whether perforated)

### 2.2.2 The "Final-Stage" Survival Requirements (Passenger Ships)

> [!NOTE]
> After the ship has flooded, the following **residual GZ-curve** criteria must be met:
> 1. **θ_max (angle of max GZ)** ≥ **20°** (ideally). Hard minimum: GZ ≥ 0.05 m between 20° and 30°.
> 2. **Range of positive residual stability** ≥ **20°** (post-2009 SOLAS).
> 3. **Residual GZ at 20°** ≥ **0.1 m**.
> 4. **Margin line** (3.75% B or 75% of freeboard, whichever is smaller) **must not be immersed**.

### 2.2.3 Cargo Ships (SOLAS 2009 Reg. 28 / 31)

The probabilistic index calculation yields an **Attained Index R** that must satisfy:
$$R \geq R_{\text{required}}$$

For bulk carriers (Reg. 27), the *worst deterministic scenario* (e.g., a single flooded cargo hold) is also required to meet:
- **Area A** under residual GZ from 0° to 30° ≥ **0.055 m·rad** (rolling range 0°–30°).
- **Range B** of positive residual stability ≥ **20°**.
- GZ at 30° ≥ **0.20 m** (note: original pre-2009 criterion; 2009 amendments adjust this).

---

## 2.3 The Weather Criterion — Detailed Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution A.749(18), Chapter 3** defines the criterion using *three curves* plotted against the heel angle θ:
> 1. **l_w1 (wind heeling arm curve)** — parabolic, $l_{w1} = l_{w0} \cos^2\theta$ where $l_{w0}$ is the wind heeling lever at 0° heel.
> 2. **l_w2 = l_w1 + l_p (wind + passenger crowding)** — constant offset.
> 3. **GZ (righting arm curve)** — computed from cross-curves of stability.

### 2.3.1 Wind Heeling Lever at Zero Heel

$$l_{w0} = \frac{M_w}{\Delta \cdot g}$$

where
$$M_w = p \cdot A \cdot z = 504 \cdot A \cdot z \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

- $p = 504 \text{ N/m}^2$ = wind pressure (constant for criterion).
- $A$ = lateral projected area of the ship above the waterline (m²).
- $z$ = vertical distance from the waterline to the centroid of $A$ (m).
- $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes or kg).
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s² (used only if $\Delta$ in kg).

### 2.3.2 The "b / a" Formulation

Plot $GZ$ and $l_{w1} = l_{w0} \cos^2\theta$ on the same axes:

$$\boxed{\; b \;=\; \int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_2} (GZ - l_{w1}) \, d\theta \;\geq\; \int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_f} l_{w1} \, d\theta + 0.006 \;\text{m·rad} \;}$$

- $\theta_0$ = steady heel angle under *l_w1 alone* (first intercept of GZ and l_w1 — sometimes called the *angle of equilibrium*).
- $\theta_1$ = second intercept of GZ and l_w1 (angle of vanishing stability under wind).
- $\theta_2$ = angle of flooding (downflooding) OR 60°, whichever is smaller.
- $a$ = area under l_w1 from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$ (or 60°).
- $b$ = area between GZ and l_w1 from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_1$, *minus* the area under l_w1 from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$.

In simpler form, the **pass condition** is:

$$\boxed{\; b - a \geq 0.006 \text{ m·rad} \;}$$

> **Note:** The 0.006 m·rad margin is small but critical — it accounts for the *uncertainty* in the wind pressure, ship's GM, and free surface.

### 2.3.3 When is the Criterion *NOT* Applicable?

> [!WARNING]
> The Weather Criterion does **not** apply to:
> - Ships less than 24 m in length.
> - Ships with non-conventional hull forms (e.g., SWATH, catamarans, hydrofoils).
> - Fishing vessels (separate IS Code).

### 2.3.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used during **damage-control assessments** of aged ships (e.g., post-fire, post-collision).
- Used in **route planning** — the master may be advised to heave-to or alter course if the criterion fails for a given loading condition.
- Used in **ship design** — the criterion often governs the *minimum freeboard* and *GM₀* of a passenger ship.
- Forms the basis of **operational stability limits** published in the Stability Booklet.

---

## 2.4 Stability Booklet — Detailed Contents (per IMO A.267/VIII & SOLAS Reg. 22–23)

| Section | Content | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| §1 | Ship's principal particulars | LBP, B, D, T, Δ, KG₀, LCG, VCG |
| §2 | Lines plan & hydrostatics | Form coefficients, KB, BM, MCT-1cm |
| §3 | Cross-curves of stability | GZ at various heel angles & displacements |
| §4 | Tank tables | Capacities, LCG, VCG, FSM (free surface moments) |
| §5 | Lightweight distribution | Empty ship's W & CG |
| §6 | Limiting KG / GM curves | Per-draft and per-displacement |
| §7 | Loading condition information | Homologated (approved) conditions |
| §8 | Loading manual | For the master to use during operations |
| §9 | Damage stability information | Flooding length, permeability, residual GZ, evacuation time |
| §10 | Specific stability notes & operational warnings | E.g., special cargoes, partially filled tanks |
| §11 | Intact stability information | Reference to criteria satisfied |
| §12 | Trim and stability booklet — summary of calculations | One-page summary per condition |
| §13 | Master's operating instructions | "How to use this booklet" |

### 2.4.1 Who Approves the Booklet?

- The **Flag Administration** (e.g., DG Shipping of India, MCA of UK, USCG of USA).
- Endorsed by the **Classification Society** (e.g., IRS — Indian Register of Shipping).
- Must be *ship-specific* — i.e., based on the actual lightweight survey, not a generic design.

### 2.4.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **On-board**: Master uses it daily to assess draft, trim, GM, and shear forces.
- **At terminal**: The terminal load-master uses it to ensure that the loading is within approved limits.
- **At audit**: Port State Control (PSC) inspectors may ask to see the booklet and a recent loading calculation.

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This section contains **exhaustive numerical and analytical derivations** that are *guaranteed* to appear in some form in the KTU 2024 University Exam.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Wind Heeling Arm Curve (l_w1)

The wind pressure on a vertical surface is given by:
$$p = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a \cdot V^2 \cdot C_D$$

where:
- $\rho_a$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** (at sea level, 15°C).
- $V$ = wind speed (m/s).
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient of the ship's superstructure (≈ 1.2 to 1.5 for a typical ship form).

> [!NOTE]
> The IMO assumes a **constant wind pressure** of **p = 504 N/m²** (≈ 10.0 m/s sustained for 5 seconds = Beaufort Force 8). This simplification removes the need for drag coefficient.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Wind force on the projected area:
$$F_w = p \cdot A$$

**Step 2:** Heeling moment about the centre of gravity (G):
$$M_w = F_w \cdot z = p \cdot A \cdot z$$

**Step 3:** Heeling arm at *zero heel*:
$$l_{w0} = \frac{M_w}{W} = \frac{p \cdot A \cdot z}{W}$$

where $W = \Delta \cdot g$ is the displacement in newtons.

**Step 4:** As the ship heels by angle $\theta$, the *moment arm* is reduced by $\cos^2\theta$ (the geometric projection of both the lever arm and the wind force normal to the inclined waterline):
$$l_{w1}(\theta) = l_{w0} \cdot \cos^2\theta$$

**Step 5:** When $\theta = 0°$: $l_{w1}(0) = l_{w0}$.
**Step 6:** When $\theta = 30°$: $l_{w1}(30) = l_{w0} \cdot 0.75$.
**Step 7:** When $\theta = 45°$: $l_{w1}(45) = l_{w0} \cdot 0.5$.
**Step 8:** When $\theta = 60°$: $l_{w1}(60) = l_{w0} \cdot 0.25$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory aid:** $\cos^2\theta$ at $0°, 30°, 45°, 60°$ gives **1.00, 0.75, 0.50, 0.25** — a clean 1:0.75:0.5:0.25 sequence. KTU problems often use this sequence.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Weather Criterion Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Problem (14 marks):**
> A passenger ship of displacement **Δ = 8000 t** has a lateral projected area above the waterline of **A = 2000 m²** with centroid at **z = 6.0 m** above WL. The cross-curve of stability data is:
>
> | θ (°) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | GZ (m) | 0.00 | 0.10 | 0.22 | 0.30 | 0.32 | 0.28 | 0.20 |
>
> Angle of flooding **θ_f = 50°**. Apply the Weather Criterion with **p = 504 N/m²**.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Compute l_w0 (Wind heeling arm at 0°)**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_w &= p \cdot A \cdot z \\
&= 504 \cdot 2000 \cdot 6.0 \\
&= 6.048 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \Delta \cdot g = 8000 \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.81 = 7.848 \times 10^7 \text{ N} \\
l_{w0} &= \frac{M_w}{W} = \frac{6.048 \times 10^6}{7.848 \times 10^7} = 0.0771 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute l_w1(θ) = l_w0 · cos²θ**

| θ (°) | cos θ | cos²θ | l_w1 (m) | GZ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0.0771 | 0.00 |
| 10 | 0.985 | 0.970 | 0.0748 | 0.10 |
| 20 | 0.940 | 0.883 | 0.0681 | 0.22 |
| 30 | 0.866 | 0.750 | 0.0578 | 0.30 |
| 40 | 0.766 | 0.587 | 0.0453 | 0.32 |
| 50 | 0.643 | 0.413 | 0.0319 | 0.28 |
| 60 | 0.500 | 0.250 | 0.0193 | 0.20 |

**Step 3: Find θ_0 (first intercept of GZ and l_w1)**

By linear interpolation between 0° and 10°:
$$\theta_0 \approx 7° \quad \text{(where GZ = l_w1)}$$

More precisely, at $\theta = 5°$:
$$GZ \approx 0.05, \quad l_{w1} = 0.0771 \cdot \cos^2 5° = 0.0766$$
$$GZ < l_{w1}$$

At $\theta = 10°$: $GZ = 0.10 > l_{w1} = 0.0748$. So $\theta_0 \approx 9°$ (interpolated).

**Step 4: Find θ_1 (second intercept of GZ and l_w1)**

By inspection of the table, the curves don't cross again before 60°. So $\theta_1 \approx 60°$ (treating it as the second intersection, with GZ > l_w1 throughout 10°–60°). The "vanishing stability" angle is far beyond 60°, so the relevant range is θ_f = 50°.

**Step 5: Compute 'a' (area under l_w1 from θ_0 to θ_f = 50°)**

Using the trapezoidal rule with Δθ = 10° and converting to radians (1° = π/180 rad):
$$\Delta\theta_{\text{rad}} = 10° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.1745 \text{ rad}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_f} l_{w1}(\theta) \, d\theta \\
&\approx \Delta\theta_{\text{rad}} \cdot \left[ \frac{l_{w1}(10) + l_{w1}(50)}{2} + l_{w1}(20) + l_{w1}(30) + l_{w1}(40) \right] \\
&= 0.1745 \cdot \left[ \frac{0.0748 + 0.0319}{2} + 0.0681 + 0.0578 + 0.0453 \right] \\
&= 0.1745 \cdot [0.0534 + 0.0681 + 0.0578 + 0.0453] \\
&= 0.1745 \cdot 0.2246 \\
&= 0.0392 \text{ m·rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Compute 'b' (area between GZ and l_w1 from θ_0 to θ_1)**

Using trapezoidal rule, with GZ values from 10° to 50°:
$$\begin{aligned}
b &\approx \Delta\theta_{\text{rad}} \cdot \left[ \sum_{i} (GZ_i - l_{w1,i}) \right] \\
&= 0.1745 \cdot [ (0.10 - 0.0748) + (0.22 - 0.0681) + (0.30 - 0.0578) + (0.40) - 0.0453) + (0.28 - 0.0319) ] \\
&= 0.1745 \cdot [0.0252 + 0.1519 + 0.2422 + 0.2747 + 0.2481] \\
&= 0.1745 \cdot 0.9421 \\
&= 0.1644 \text{ m·rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Check the criterion**

$$b - a = 0.1644 - 0.0392 = 0.1252 \text{ m·rad}$$

The criterion is:
$$b - a \geq 0.006 \text{ m·rad}$$

Since **0.1252 > 0.006**, the ship **passes** the Weather Criterion ✅

**Step 8: Verify the seven intact stability criteria (quick check)**

| Criterion | Computed | Required | Pass? |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(0–30°) | 0.0339 m·rad | ≥ 0.055 m·rad | ❌ FAIL |
| A(0–40°) | 0.0650 m·rad | ≥ 0.09 m·rad | ❌ FAIL |
| A(30–40°) | 0.0311 m·rad | ≥ 0.03 m·rad | ✅ PASS |
| GZ(30°) | 0.30 m | ≥ 0.20 m | ✅ PASS |
| GZ_max | 0.32 m at 40° | ≥ 0.20 m at θ ≤ 20° (pref) | ❌ FAIL (peak at 40°) |
| GM₀ | ≈ 0.6 m | ≥ 0.15 m | ✅ PASS |
| Weather | 0.1252 | ≥ 0.006 | ✅ PASS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** A ship can pass the Weather Criterion but FAIL the area/lever criteria — and vice versa. *All seven* must be satisfied. The above ship would NOT be considered intact-stable in the IMO sense even though it passed the Weather Criterion, because A(0–30°) is too small.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Damage Length as a Function of L (Passenger Ships, SOLAS 2009 Annex)

The SOLAS damage length is given by a *piecewise linear* relationship:

$$\ell_d = a \cdot L + b$$

For different ranges of L:
- $L = 60\text{–}100$ m: $\ell_d = 0.19L - 3.0$ m.
- $L = 100\text{–}200$ m: $\ell_d = 0.094L + 9.0$ m.
- $L = 200\text{–}300$ m: $\ell_d = 0.06L + 16.0$ m.

### Example Calculation

For a ship of **L = 150 m**:
$$\ell_d = 0.094 \cdot 150 + 9.0 = 14.1 + 9.0 = 23.1 \text{ m}$$

This means the ship must survive a *23.1 m length* of flooding centred amidships.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Free-Surface Effect Correction (FSM)

When a tank is partially filled, the centre of gravity of the liquid shifts as the ship rolls, creating a virtual rise in G:

$$\delta GG_v = \frac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot i}{\Delta}$$

where:
- $i$ = transverse moment of inertia of the free surface (m⁴).
- $\Delta$ = displacement (mass, tonnes or kg, consistent with i).

The IMO intact criterion uses:
$$GM_{\text{effective}} = GM_0 - \delta GG_v \geq 0.15 \text{ m}$$

### Example

A tank has $i = 5000$ m⁴, contains seawater ($\rho_{liquid} = 1.025$ t/m³), in a ship of $\Delta = 5000$ t.

$$\delta GG_v = \frac{1.025 \cdot 5000}{5000} = 1.025 \text{ m}$$

If $GM_0 = 1.20$ m, then $GM_{\text{eff}} = 0.175$ m, which **passes** the 0.15 m criterion. But if $\delta GG_v = 1.10$ m, then $GM_{\text{eff}} = 0.10$ m, which **fails** — and the master must either fill or empty the tank to eliminate the free surface.

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does Each Criterion Govern?

| Scenario | Governing Criterion | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Small cargo ship, light load | **GM₀** (Criterion 6) | Tendency to tender; minimum stiffness |
| Large passenger ship, full load | **Weather Criterion** | Tall superstructure → large wind heeling moment |
| Container ship, mid-deck | **GZ_max lever** | Wide beam → peak GZ at high angle; risk of parametric roll |
| Bulk carrier, partially filled holds | **Area A(0–30°)** | Free surface from grain / ore shifting |
| RO-RO ferry | **Range of positive stability** | Need to absorb passenger shifting + heavy vehicle loads |
| LNG carrier | **Damage Criterion** | Free surface from partial-fill cargo tanks |
