# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

The questions below are calibrated to the KTU 2024 B.Tech assessment pattern: **Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks**, with internal choice between two questions (A and B).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Weather Criterion" in ship stability. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Weather Criterion** is one of the seven mandatory intact-stability requirements codified in **IMO Resolution A.749(18), Chapter 3**. It tests the ability of a ship to withstand a steady beam wind of pressure **p = 504 N/m²** (representing a Beaufort Force 8 gale) together with passenger crowding and the dynamic effect of rolling. The criterion is satisfied if the difference between the righting-area (b) and the wind-heeling-area (a) exceeds a safety margin of **0.006 m·rad**. Mathematically:
> $$b - a \geq 0.006 \text{ m·rad}$$

---

### Question 2: List any six items that must be included in a Stability Booklet. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Principal particulars of the ship (LBP, B, D, T, Δ).
2. Hydrostatic data (KB, BM, form coefficients, MCT-1cm).
3. Cross-curves of stability (GZ vs. heel at various displacements).
4. Tank capacity and KG/LCG tables.
5. Lightweight distribution.
6. Limiting KG/GM curves.
7. Loading condition information (homologated conditions).
8. Damage stability information.
9. Master's operating instructions.

*(Any 6 = 3 marks, 4 = 2 marks, 3 or less = 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

---

### QUESTION A — Intact Stability + Weather Criterion [14 marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) List the seven IMO intact-stability criteria and explain the rationale of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The seven IMO intact-stability criteria (A.749(18)) are:
>
> **1. Area Criterion 1 (0°–30°):** The area under the GZ-curve from 0° to 30° heel must be **≥ 0.055 m·rad**. This ensures sufficient *low-angle* dynamical stability to resist everyday roll motions (e.g., turning, beam wind).
>
> **2. Area Criterion 2 (0°–40°):** The area from 0° to 40° must be **≥ 0.09 m·rad**. This ensures *total* dynamical stability over a wide heel range, including the risk of large-angle rolls in heavy seas.
>
> **3. Area Criterion 3 (30°–40°):** The area from 30° to 40° must be **≥ 0.03 m·rad**. This is the *high-angle* portion — it ensures the ship has sufficient stability *after* a large heel (e.g., after a broach or wave impact).
>
> **4. Lever Criterion (GZ at 30°):** GZ at 30° heel must be **≥ 0.20 m**. This is a *single-value* check ensuring the righting lever is large enough at the end of the first quadrant.
>
> **5. Lever Criterion (GZ_max):** The maximum GZ must be **≥ 0.20 m** occurring at an angle ≤ 25° (preferably ≤ 20°). This avoids the dangerous scenario of a *delayed* peak (where the ship is unstable near 0° and only stabilises at high angles — the "spilling" problem).
>
> **6. GM₀ Criterion:** Initial metacentric height must be **≥ 0.15 m** for passenger ships and **≥ 0.10 m** for cargo ships. This is the *first-order* check — the slope of the GZ curve at the origin.
>
> **7. Weather Criterion:** The b/a ratio check with the 0.006 m·rad safety margin. This is the *operational* test — can the ship survive a Force 8–10 storm?
>
> **Rationale Summary:** Criteria 1–3 check the *area* under the curve; 4–5 check the *shape*; 6 checks the *initial slope*; 7 checks the *dynamic* behaviour under realistic wind.

**(b) A ship of displacement 10,000 tonnes has projected lateral area A = 1500 m² above WL with centroid z = 8 m. Wind pressure p = 504 N/m². The cross-curve data are:**

| θ (°) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GZ (m) | 0.00 | 0.12 | 0.25 | 0.35 | 0.40 | 0.36 | 0.25 |

> **Compute the wind heeling arm l_w0, the wind heeling arm curve, and check the Weather Criterion. Take angle of flooding θ_f = 50°.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Step 1: Wind heeling moment:**
> $$M_w = p \cdot A \cdot z = 504 \cdot 1500 \cdot 8 = 6.048 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}$$
>
> **Step 2: Wind heeling arm at 0°:**
> $$l_{w0} = \frac{M_w}{W} = \frac{6.048 \times 10^6}{10000 \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.81} = 0.0617 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 3: Wind heeling arm curve l_w1(θ) = l_w0 · cos²θ:**

| θ (°) | cos²θ | l_w1 (m) | GZ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.000 | 0.0617 | 0.00 |
| 10 | 0.970 | 0.0598 | 0.12 |
| 20 | 0.883 | 0.0545 | 0.25 |
| 30 | 0.750 | 0.0463 | 0.35 |
| 40 | 0.587 | 0.0362 | 0.40 |
| 50 | 0.413 | 0.0255 | 0.36 |
| 60 | 0.250 | 0.0154 | 0.25 |
>
> **Step 4: Find θ_0 (first intercept):** Between 0° and 10°, GZ < l_w1 at small angles. At 10°, GZ = 0.12 > l_w1 = 0.0598. Linear interpolation gives θ_0 ≈ 5°.
>
> **Step 5: Compute 'a' from θ_0 ≈ 5° to θ_f = 50° (using trapezoidal rule, Δθ = 10° = 0.1745 rad):**
> $$a \approx 0.1745 \cdot \left[ \frac{l_{w1}(10) + l_{w1}(50)}{2} + l_{w1}(20) + l_{w1}(30) + l_{w1}(40) \right]$$
> $$a \approx 0.1745 \cdot [0.0426 + 0.0545 + 0.0463 + 0.0362] = 0.1745 \cdot 0.1796 = 0.0313 \text{ m·rad}$$
>
> **Step 6: Compute 'b' (area between GZ and l_w1 from θ_0 to θ_1 ≈ 60°):**
> $$b \approx 0.1745 \cdot [(0.12-0.0598) + (0.25-0.0545) + (0.35-0.0463) + (0.40-0.0362) + (0.36-0.0255) + (0.25-0.0154)]$$
> $$b \approx 0.1745 \cdot [0.0602 + 0.1955 + 0.3037 + 0.3638 + 0.3345 + 0.2346] = 0.1745 \cdot 1.4923 = 0.2604 \text{ m·rad}$$
>
> **Step 7: Check criterion:**
> $$b - a = 0.2604 - 0.0313 = 0.2291 \text{ m·rad}$$
>
> Since **0.2291 m·rad > 0.006 m·rad**, the ship **passes the Weather Criterion** ✅
>
> **Conclusion:** The ship has more than adequate weather stability margin.

---

### QUESTION B — Damage Stability + Stability Booklet [14 marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) Explain the SOLAS damage-stability requirements for a passenger ship under the 2009 amendments. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **SOLAS 2009 Damage Stability for Passenger Ships (Chapter II-1, Part B-1):**
>
> **1. Damage Length & Zone:** The damage length is a function of L (ship length) ranging from 8.4 m at L = 60 m to 30.0 m at L = 300 m, given by the SOLAS interpolation table. The damage is assumed to occur at the worst possible position amidships (or as defined by the probability analysis).
>
> **2. Damage Extent Transversely:** Damage extends from the shell to the centreline (0.46B or 0.45B depending on the rule version).
>
> **3. Damage Extent Vertically:** Damage extends from the keel (moulded) to the upper deck (bulkhead deck).
>
> **4. Permeability Assumed (μ):**
> - Machinery spaces: 0.85
> - Accommodation: 0.95
> - Dry cargo: 0.90
> - Tanks (void): 0 or 1
>
> **5. Final-Stage Survival Criteria (must all be met):**
> - **θ_max (angle of maximum residual GZ) ≥ 20°** (ideally; minimum 15°).
> - **Range of positive residual stability ≥ 20°** (for ships > 80 m).
> - **Residual GZ at 20° heel ≥ 0.1 m.**
> - **GZ between 20° and 30° ≥ 0.05 m.**
> - **Margin line** (3.75% of B or 75% of freeboard, whichever is smaller) **must not be immersed** at any stage.
> - **Heel at equilibrium ≤ 7°** in intermediate flooding stages.
>
> **6. Progressive Flooding:** The ship must satisfy the criteria in *every* stage of progressive flooding, not just the final equilibrium stage.
>
> **7. Special Requirements for RO-RO Passenger Ships:** Faster evacuation time (60 min for ships built after 2010), steeper cross-flooding time limits.
>
> **Rationale:** The 2009 amendments were prompted by the *Costa Concordia* (2012, retroactively) and *Herald of Free Enterprise* (1987) accidents — emphasising survivability during the *evacuation phase*, not just the post-flooding equilibrium.

**(b) Describe the contents of a Stability Booklet as required by IMO Resolution A.267 and SOLAS Regulations 22–23. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **A Stability Booklet (Trim and Stability Information)** must contain the following:
>
> **1. Principal Particulars:** LBP, B, D, T, Δ, KG₀, LCG, VCG, GMT, type of ship.
>
> **2. Hydrostatic Data:** Form coefficients (CB, CM, CP), KB, BM, MCT-1cm, TPC, and other hydrostatic particulars at each draft.
>
> **3. Cross-Curves of Stability:** GZ values at various heel angles and displacements (typically every 10° from 0° to 90°).
>
> **4. Tank Tables:** Capacity, LCG, VCG, and free-surface moment (FSM) for each tank, for filling levels from 0% to 100%.
>
> **5. Lightweight Distribution:** Surveyed weight distribution of the empty ship, with a small diagram.
>
> **6. Limiting KG / GM Curves:** For each draft, the maximum permissible KG and minimum GM.
>
> **7. Loading Condition Information:** All homologated (approved) loading conditions, including departure, arrival, ballast, and special conditions.
>
> **8. Loading Manual:** Step-by-step procedure for the master to use when planning loading or trim.
>
> **9. Damage Stability Information:** Damage length assumed, permeability used, residual GZ-curves, downflooding angles, evacuation time, margin line.
>
> **10. Operational Notes & Master's Instructions:** Special cargoes, partial filling rules, free surface precautions.
>
> **11. Intact Stability Information:** Reference to all seven criteria satisfied.
>
> **12. Summary Calculations:** One-page summary per condition, showing draft, trim, GM₀, freeboard, intact and damage stability compliance.
>
> **Approval:** The booklet is *ship-specific* and is approved by the **Flag Administration** and the **Classification Society**. It is part of the statutory documents and is checked during Port State Control inspections.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in area calculations: 1° = π/180 rad = 0.01745 rad. Using degrees directly inflates the area by ~57×.
> 2. **Confusing a and b**: 'a' is the area *under* l_w1 (wind heeling area), 'b' is the area *between* GZ and l_w1 (the residual righting area).
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.006 m·rad safety margin** — the criterion is b - a ≥ 0.006, NOT b - a ≥ 0.
> 4. **Mixing up GM₀ and GM_eff** — Criterion 6 uses *effective* GM (after free-surface correction).
> 5. **Listing only six criteria** when asked for seven — the Weather Criterion is the *seventh* and is often forgotten.
> 6. **Wrong permeability values** in damage stability — μ = 0.85 (machinery) is the most-commonly-mistaken value.
> 7. **Stability Booklet sections mis-named** — the *Trim and Stability Information* is a specific document, not a generic "ship manual".
> 8. **Project topics that are too broad** — e.g., "study of ship stability" (too vague). A good topic has: *a specific ship*, *a specific problem*, *a specific method*.
